name: Home
title: Home
description: Entry page that loads an overview of coaching services and content, provides global navigation, and offers a site-wide search across coaches, packages, challenges, worksheets, and blog articles.

core_features: Load homepage overview data using getHomePageOverview and expose summary sections for featured coaches, popular packages, current challenges, and recent blog articles.

Display a functional overview panel using the heroMessage from getHomePageOverview, with navigation links to key sections such as Coaches, Services, Blog, Challenges, Tools & Assessments, Resources, Build Your Plan, Reading Lists, and Dashboard.
Provide a global search form that uses searchAllContent to search across coaches, coaching packages, challenges, worksheets, and blog articles, driven by URL parameters.
Show grouped global search results (by content type) when a search query is present in the URL, and allow navigation to the appropriate detail pages via URL parameters.
Offer direct navigation from homepage sections (featured cards and overview links) to other pages using URL parameters where needed (e.g., detail pages with id parameters).
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Discover a coach via homepage overview: (1) User lands on Home without URL parameters. (2) home-overview-loader calls getHomePageOverview. (3) featured-coach-list displays featuredCoaches. (4) User clicks a coach card in featured-coach-list. (5) Browser navigates to coach_detail.html?id={Coach.id}. (6) If the user prefers to see all coaches, they click the navigation link for Coaches, navigating to coaches.html.

Workflow 2 – Explore coaching services and packages: (1) User lands on Home and getHomePageOverview returns popularPackages. (2) popular-packages-list displays popularPackages. (3) User clicks a package card, triggering navigation to package_detail.html?id={CoachingPackage.id}. (4) Alternatively, user clicks a Services navigation link or a “view all services” action inside popular-packages-list, navigating to services.html.

Workflow 3 – Join or review a challenge: (1) User lands on Home and currentChallenges is loaded via getHomePageOverview. (2) current-challenges-list displays currentChallenges. (3) User clicks a challenge card. (4) Browser navigates to challenge_detail.html?id={Challenge.id}. (5) If the user wants to browse all challenges, they click the Challenges navigation link, navigating to challenges.html.

Workflow 4 – Read recent blog content: (1) User lands on Home and recentArticles is loaded via getHomePageOverview. (2) recent-articles-list displays recentArticles with titles and metadata. (3) User clicks an article title. (4) Browser navigates to article_detail.html?id={BlogArticle.id}. (5) If user wants broader browsing, they click the Blog navigation link, navigating to blog.html.

Workflow 5 – Use global search to find content: (1) User focuses the global-search-form input in the header or main area. (2) User types a search term and submits the form. (3) onSubmit in global-search-form updates the URL to include ?q={encodedQuery}&limit={optionalLimit}, causing a navigation or history update. (4) global-search-results observes the URL, detects q, and triggers searchAllContent with parameters { query: q, limitPerType: limit or a default value determined by the implementation }. (5) searchAllContent returns grouped arrays for coaches, packages, challenges, worksheets, and articles. (6) global-search-results displays results grouped by type. (7) User clicks a result; the component navigates to the appropriate detail page: coach_detail.html?id={Coach.id}, package_detail.html?id={CoachingPackage.id}, challenge_detail.html?id={Challenge.id}, worksheet_detail.html?id={Worksheet.id}, or article_detail.html?id={BlogArticle.id}.

Workflow 6 – Navigate to tools, resources, custom plans, reading lists, and dashboard from Home: (1) User lands on Home. (2) overview-summary-panel exposes navigation links or buttons for Tools & Assessments, Resources, Build Your Plan, Reading Lists, and Dashboard. (3) User clicks one of these links. (4) Browser navigates to the corresponding page: assessments.html, resources.html, custom_plan.html, reading_lists.html, or dashboard.html respectively.
interactions: Click main navigation links in main-navigation-bar to navigate to coaches.html, services.html, blog.html, challenges.html, assessments.html, resources.html, custom_plan.html, reading_lists.html, dashboard.html, or index.html.
Click footer links in footer-navigation to navigate back to Home (index.html) or to about_contact.html.
Submit a search query via global-search-form, which updates URL parameters (q and optional limit) and triggers a searchAllContent call in global-search-results.
Click on a coach result (either from featured-coach-list or from global-search-results) to navigate to coach_detail.html?id={Coach.id}.
Click on a package result (from popular-packages-list or from global-search-results) to navigate to package_detail.html?id={CoachingPackage.id}.
Click on a challenge result (from current-challenges-list or from global-search-results) to navigate to challenge_detail.html?id={Challenge.id}.
Click on a worksheet result (from global-search-results) to navigate to worksheet_detail.html?id={Worksheet.id}.
Click on a blog article (from recent-articles-list or from global-search-results) to navigate to article_detail.html?id={BlogArticle.id}.

Click overview-summary-panel quick links for actions like finding a coach, viewing services, starting a self-assessment, viewing resources, building a plan, opening reading lists, or opening the dashboard, to navigate to the corresponding pages.

Use optional section-specific links or controls in overview-summary-panel or within list components (e.g., “view all coaches”, “view all services”, “view all blog articles”) to navigate to the corresponding top-level pages.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page state is driven by URL parameters and API responses rather than local storage. On initial load, regardless of parameters, home-overview-loader calls getHomePageOverview to populate heroMessage, featuredCoaches, popularPackages, currentChallenges, and recentArticles. If a search query parameter q is present in the URL (e.g., index.html?q={encodedQuery}&limit={optionalNumber}&section={optionalSectionId}), the global-search-form initializes its input with the decoded value of q, and global-search-results calls searchAllContent with { query: q, limitPerType: limit or an implementation-defined default when limit is absent }. When q is present and non-empty, search results are displayed; when q is absent or empty, no search results are shown and only the overview content is visible. The optional limit parameter controls how many items per type are requested from searchAllContent. An optional section parameter may be used by overview-summary-panel or by individual list components to auto-focus or scroll to a specific section (e.g., coaches, services, challenges, blog); this does not affect API calls but can change which section is emphasized or brought into view after load. Navigation from Home to detail pages always includes the corresponding id as a URL parameter (e.g., coach_detail.html?id={Coach.id}), ensuring that detail pages can load the appropriate entity. Navigation from search or featured sections to high-level listing pages (e.g., coaches.html, services.html, blog.html, challenges.html, assessments.html, resources.html, custom_plan.html, reading_lists.html, dashboard.html) does not require additional URL parameters unless those pages define their own filters. All transient UI state for loading, errors, and expanded or collapsed result groups is maintained in in-memory page state only and is not persisted between navigation events.

components.functionality: Provides primary site navigation links from Home to all main sections, including Home, Coaches, Services, Blog, Challenges, Tools & Assessments, Resources, Build Your Plan, Reading Lists, and Dashboard. Indicates the active page when the current URL corresponds to Home.
event_handlers: onNavClick(linkId) -> navigates to the appropriate URL (e.g., 'coaches' -> coaches.html, 'services' -> services.html, 'blog' -> blog.html, 'challenges' -> challenges.html, 'assessments' -> assessments.html, 'resources' -> resources.html, 'custom_plan' -> custom_plan.html, 'reading_lists' -> reading_lists.html, 'dashboard' -> dashboard.html, 'home' -> index.html).

components.functionality: Provides secondary navigation links from the Home page footer, including a link back to Home and a link to About & Contact.

On page initialization, calls getHomePageOverview to retrieve heroMessage, featuredCoaches, popularPackages, currentChallenges, and recentArticles for display in overview and list components. Manages loading and error states for these data sets.
event_handlers: onPageLoad() -> invokes getHomePageOverview(), stores the returned object in shared page state, and updates loading/error flags.
onRetryLoad() -> re-invokes getHomePageOverview() if the initial call failed.

components.functionality: Displays a concise introductory text using heroMessage and offers functional quick links to key flows such as finding a coach, viewing services, visiting the blog, exploring challenges, starting tools and assessments, accessing resources, building a custom plan, viewing reading lists, and opening the dashboard.
Displays a list of featured coaches on the Home page using data from getHomePageOverview. Each item shows key coach information and supports navigation to the coach detail page.

Displays a list of popular coaching packages on the Home page using data from getHomePageOverview. Each package item allows navigation to its detail page and optionally provides a link to the full services listing.

Displays a list of current or active challenges on the Home page using data from getHomePageOverview. Each challenge item links to its dedicated detail page, and the component can provide a link to view all challenges.

Displays a list of recent blog articles on the Home page using data from getHomePageOverview. Each article title links to the corresponding article detail page, and the component can provide a link to the main blog listing.

Provides a single input for global search across all site content. Reads the current query from the URL (if present), lets the user modify it, and on submit updates the URL parameters so that global-search-results can perform searchAllContent.
data_binding: URL parameter q (used to initialize the search input value).
URL parameter limit (optional, used to initialize an optional per-type result limit control).
event_handlers: onInputChange(newValue) -> updates internal form state for the search query but does not immediately call searchAllContent.
onLimitChange(newLimit) -> updates internal form state for the per-type result limit and, if desired, can update the URL immediately or on form submit.

onSubmit(currentQuery, currentLimit) -> updates the browser URL to index.html?q={encodedCurrentQuery}&limit={currentLimit if provided}, without reloading where possible (e.g., via history API), which triggers global-search-results to invoke searchAllContent.
onClearSearch() -> removes q and optional limit from the URL (e.g., navigates to index.html without those parameters), causing global-search-results to hide and resetting the search field to empty.

components.functionality: Displays grouped search results for coaches, packages, challenges, worksheets, and articles whenever a query parameter q is present in the URL. Calls searchAllContent with the query and an optional limit per type, then renders each group and provides navigation to the relevant detail pages.
data_binding: URL parameter limit (optional, used as searchAllContent.limitPerType).
event_handlers: onUrlChange(newQ, newLimit) -> when q changes and is non-empty, invokes searchAllContent({ query: newQ, limitPerType: newLimit or an implementation-defined default }) and updates local state with the returned result groups.
onToggleGroupVisibility(groupType) -> toggles the visibility (expanded/collapsed) of a specific result group (coaches, packages, challenges, worksheets, or articles) using in-memory state only.
onRetrySearch() -> re-invokes searchAllContent with the current q and limitPerType if a previous call failed.

components.functionality: Reads an optional section parameter from the URL and coordinates which content section should be brought into view or emphasized on load (e.g., coaches section, services section, challenges section, blog section). Acts as a glue component that does not render content itself but orchestrates scrolling or focusing behavior for other components.
event_handlers: onPageLoadWithSection(sectionId) -> after home-overview-loader completes, scrolls to or focuses the corresponding component: 'coaches' -> featured-coach-list, 'services' -> popular-packages-list, 'challenges' -> current-challenges-list, 'blog' -> recent-articles-list, etc.
onSectionLinkClick(sectionId) -> updates the URL to include section={sectionId} (optionally using history API to avoid reload) and immediately scrolls to or focuses the corresponding section.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom page flow anchored by a fixed full-width header at the top. Directly beneath, a full-width hero section uses a two-column split: left column for the home overview summary and global search (replacing the blog title area from the mockup), right column reserved for a large visual block (imagery or illustrative content owned by the hero section container, not a separate component). Below the hero, content is stacked in clearly separated sections: global search results (when active), then featured coaches, popular packages, current challenges, and recent articles, all within a centered max-width container. The footer navigation spans full width at the bottom. Non-visual controllers (loader, section focus controller) are layered over or behind these main structures without disrupting the linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom page flow anchored by a fixed full-width header at the top. Directly beneath, a full-width hero section uses a two-column split: left column for the home overview summary and global search (replacing the blog title area from the mockup), right column reserved for a large visual block (imagery or illustrative content owned by the hero section container, not a separate component). Below the hero, content is stacked in clearly separated sections: global search results (when active), then featured coaches, popular packages, current challenges, and recent articles, all within a centered max-width container. The footer navigation spans full width at the bottom. Non-visual controllers (loader, section focus controller) are layered over or behind these main structures without disrupting the linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a modest vertical header band (~10–15% of viewport height) and let the hero occupy most of the above-the-fold area (~55–70% of viewport height on desktop). Within the hero, allocate roughly 55–60% of the content width to the left text/search column (overview-summary-panel + global-search-form) and 40–45% to the right visual column. All subsequent sections share the same max-width centered container (around 1140–1280px) with generous vertical spacing: ~64px between major sections and 32–48px internal padding. Cards and lists sit on lighter surfaces within the dark teal background, using the 8px spacing scale for padding and gutters.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep content density low and editorial. Use large, bold typography for key headings and plenty of whitespace between elements. Each section (overview, lists, search results) has a clear heading, a small amount of supporting text (if any), and a few high-impact items visible by default rather than long dense lists. Interactive elements such as search, filters, and 'view all' links are present but visually quiet, respecting the spacious, premium feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Maintain an F/Z-pattern: eye starts at the logo on the top-left of the navigation bar, travels across the main navigation links toward the right-side actions, then drops diagonally down to the small category pill and large overview heading on the left of the hero. From there, attention moves slightly downward to the prominent global search form, then across to the hero's right visual column. As the user scrolls, each content section is introduced by a left-aligned heading, leading the eye horizontally across each row of cards (coaches, packages, challenges, articles). Global search results, when present, appear immediately below the hero and become the primary focal region, temporarily intercepting the natural scroll flow before the user moves further down into the evergreen sections.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a deep, dark teal canvas with a fixed, full-width header at the top and a centered content column below. Above the fold, the hero dominates: on the left, a small pill label and a large, multi-line headline introduce the coaching platform and invite exploration. Directly beneath that headline, a wide, prominent search bar invites users to search across coaches, packages, challenges, worksheets, and articles. On the right, a large visual area (image or illustration) balances the hero, aligned vertically with the headline and search, occupying slightly less width to keep the focus on the left text block. As the user scrolls down, spacious sections slide into view one at a time: first, a search-results panel (only when actively searching) that mirrors the centered layout and shows cards in a clean grid; then horizontally aligned cards for featured coaches, followed by similarly structured sections for popular packages, current challenges, and recent articles. Each section is clearly separated with generous vertical spacing and left-aligned section headings that guide the eye. A dark, structured footer anchors the page with global navigation links, while non-visual components like the loader and section focus controller subtly manage state without cluttering the visual experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main navigation bar is fixed to the very top of the viewport, spanning the full browser width from left to right. It occupies a relatively shallow horizontal band, approximately 72–80px tall (about 10–15% of the viewport height), with a dark teal background that matches the page’s brand color. Inside, content is constrained to the max-width centered container (approximately 1140–1280px) aligned in the center of the page. Within this container, the logo sits at the far left, the primary navigation links are arranged horizontally starting just to the right of the logo and extending toward the center-left, and any key actions (e.g., sign in, primary CTA) are aligned along the right edge of the container. Vertical spacing inside the bar follows the 8px scale, with roughly 16px top and bottom padding around the nav items. Visually, the bar is clean and minimal, with no bulky shadows; its fixed position ensures that as the user scrolls past the hero and subsequent sections, the navigation remains visible at the top while content moves underneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed at the top of the viewport, full-width; content inside is centered within a max-width container and remains visible while underlying page content scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-overview-loader occupies the main content region beneath the fixed navigation bar whenever initial data is loading. It sits directly on top of the hero area and the first set of sections, spanning the same max-width content container and extending vertically to cover at least the full height of the hero plus some of the content below. Visually, it appears as a semi-transparent overlay over the dark teal background, with skeleton placeholders aligned to the underlying layout: a pill-shaped skeleton in the top-left of the hero area, a large rectangular skeleton representing the hero heading, a wide bar for the search form below it, and a tall rectangle on the right side echoing the hero image column. Additional horizontal skeleton bars or card-shaped skeletons align with where the featured coaches and other list sections will appear further down. The overlay is centered and respects the same left and right margins as the main content, creating the impression that the actual page structure is loading in place rather than shifting.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed as an absolute or fixed overlay anchored to the main content container just below the navigation bar, covering interactions and visually dimming the underlying layout until data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The overview-summary-panel forms the left column of the hero section directly beneath the main navigation bar. It begins approximately 64px below the bottom edge of the fixed header to create a generous breathing space. The hero section as a whole spans the full viewport width with a dark teal background, but the overview-summary-panel content is constrained to the max-width centered container and occupies roughly 55–60% of that width on desktop (about 6–7 columns of a 12-column grid). It is left-aligned within this area. At the top of the panel, a small pill label (e.g., 'Overview' or similar category) sits flush with the left margin, with about 8–16px of internal padding, creating a subtle but clear category marker. Directly beneath the pill, separated by about 16–24px, a large, multi-line headline spans much of the panel’s width, set in bold type and taking up a substantial vertical area, roughly one third of the hero’s height. If supporting copy is used, it appears as a short, 1–2 sentence paragraph below the headline, with comfortable line spacing and around 16–24px of vertical separation from the headline. The panel’s right edge aligns near the midline of the page, leaving the remaining 40–45% of horizontal space to the hero’s right-side visual column. Overall, the panel anchors the hero, occupying about half to two-thirds of the above-the-fold area on the left side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section, left-aligned in the max-width container; it scales responsively, collapsing toward full width above the hero visual on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-search-form is integrated into the lower part of the hero’s left column, directly beneath the overview-summary-panel’s headline and any supporting text. It aligns with the same left margin as the category pill and headline, visually belonging to that same text block. Horizontally, it spans roughly 70–80% of the width of the left hero column (about 4–5 grid columns), leaving some breathing space on the right within that column. Vertically, it is separated from the text above by 24–32px, following the spacious editorial feel. The search input itself is a large, single-line field with rounded corners, internal horizontal padding of roughly 16–24px, and vertical padding of around 8–12px, making it visually substantial but not heavy. A search icon and/or submit button is aligned to the right of the field, either integrated inside the field or as a compact button that aligns flush with the right edge of the input. The entire form sits roughly in the lower third of the hero’s left column, such that its vertical center aligns approximately with the midpoint of the hero visual on the right. On larger screens, there is generous empty space beneath the search form before the hero ends, preserving the low-density, premium aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s left column; on smaller viewports, it stacks directly below the headine and eventually spans close to 100% of the available width when the layout collapses to a single column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-search-results section sits directly beneath the entire hero section (overview-summary-panel + global-search-form + hero visual), replacing the normal scroll sequence of content when active. It spans the full width of the content container with the same centered max-width constraint and left alignment. There is a generous top margin of approximately 64px from the bottom of the hero to the first row of search results content, echoing the major section spacing. At the top-left of this section, a compact heading or label like 'Search Results' appears, followed by a brief line of meta-information (e.g., number of results) to its right or directly underneath, with 8–16px of vertical spacing. Below this, results are laid out in a clean grid that uses 2–3 columns across on desktop, depending on card width: each result card occupies approximately 3–4 grid columns, with consistent 24–32px horizontal gutters and about 24px vertical spacing between rows. The section extends vertically, potentially filling more than a full viewport height if many results exist, but still leaves ample whitespace between clusters of content so that it feels airy rather than crowded. When results are present, this section becomes the first scroll target after the hero; the standard sections (featured coaches, packages, etc.) begin below it, maintaining their own 64px+ separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline section that appears directly after the hero and pushes subsequent sections further down; when inactive, it collapses entirely, freeing the space for the evergreen sections to move up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-coach-list occupies the first evergreen content section after the hero and (if present) the global search results. It begins with a generous top margin of around 64px from the preceding section and is contained within the same centered max-width area. At the top-left of the section, a bold section heading such as 'Featured Coaches' is left-aligned. Opposite this, aligned to the far right within the same horizontal row, a subtle 'View all' link or secondary action sits, creating a clear left-right tension line along the top of the section. This header row occupies about 1/5 of the section’s height. Beneath the header row, separated by about 24px, a row of coach cards is laid out horizontally. On desktop, 3 coach cards are shown side-by-side in a single row, each card occupying roughly 3–4 columns of the 12-column grid with equal horizontal spacing between them. The cards have generous internal padding (16–24px) and sit on lighter surfaces (white or light teal) that contrast with the dark teal page background, giving them the appearance of floating tiles. If more than one row is needed, subsequent rows are aligned directly below with about 24–32px of vertical spacing. The section ends with a comfortable bottom margin of around 64px before the next section begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in the main content flow; cards reflow responsively from a 3-column grid to 2 columns and then 1 column as the viewport narrows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The popular-packages-list appears as the next major section below the featured-coach-list, separated by roughly 64px of vertical space. It adheres to the same centered max-width container and left-aligned heading pattern, with a bold 'Popular Packages' heading at the top-left and an optional tertiary action link at the top-right. To subtly differentiate it from the section above, the immediate background behind the package cards may shift slightly in tone (e.g., a very dark teal band or a slightly lighter panel), while still sitting on the overall dark teal page canvas. Below the section header row, spaced by about 24px, package cards are laid out in a row of 3 across on desktop, similar in width to the featured coach cards but slightly taller to accommodate pricing and feature lists. Internal card padding remains in the 16–24px range, with clear vertical separation between the package name, price, and short description. If additional packages exist, they flow into a second row with about 24–32px between rows. The overall section height is similar to or slightly taller than the featured-coach-list due to richer card content, but large amounts of whitespace around and between cards keep the section feeling light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width section (within the max-width container), with the card grid reflowing to fewer columns on smaller devices while retaining top-left alignment for the section heading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The current-challenges-list section follows beneath the popular-packages-list, again separated by a vertical gap of about 64px. It uses the same max-width centered container and a left-aligned heading, such as 'Current Challenges,' placed at the top-left. Any supporting text (e.g., a one-line explanation) can sit directly beneath the heading with 8–16px of spacing. The challenges themselves are presented in a visually dynamic but still spacious layout: on desktop, a 2-column grid of larger, banner-like cards that span about 5–6 grid columns each, with 24–32px of horizontal spacing between the two columns and 24px vertical spacing between rows. Each challenge card emphasizes a strong title and a short description, possibly featuring a small badge for status or difficulty aligned near the top-left inside the card. The overall height of this section may be slightly taller if multiple rows are present, but generous padding at the top and bottom ensures it feels consistent with the editorial style of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section; cards rearrange to a single column on narrower viewports, maintaining consistent left alignment and vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recent-articles-list is the final major content section before the footer, reinforcing the blog-like aspect of the platform. It begins about 64px below the current-challenges-list and shares the same centered max-width alignment. At the top-left, a 'Recent Articles' or similar heading is left-aligned, possibly accompanied by a brief subheading beneath it indicating that these are the latest from the blog, with 8–16px of spacing. Below the heading, separated by 24px, article cards are arranged in an editorial grid: typically 2 columns across on desktop, each card occupying about 5–6 grid columns with a comfortable gutter of 24–32px. Cards are taller, with room for a category pill at the top, a title, a brief excerpt, and meta information like date or reading time. The category pill at the card level echoes the small pill used in the hero, reinforcing the visual language. The section stretches vertically to accommodate at least 4–6 article cards, but maintains generous vertical spacing between rows (~24px) and a bottom margin of 64–80px before the footer begins, allowing the page to end on a calm, uncluttered note.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section; the article grid collapses from 2 columns to a single column on smaller screens, with cards stacking vertically while preserving generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer-navigation anchors the very bottom of the page, spanning the full browser width. It has a background that is either the same dark teal as the header or a slightly darker shade to visually ground the layout. Inside, the same max-width centered container is used, aligning the footer content with the rest of the page. There is generous vertical padding, approximately 48–64px at the top and bottom, giving the footer a substantial but not heavy presence. Navigation links, logo, and any secondary information are arranged in 2–3 vertical columns on desktop: for example, logo and a short description on the left, and 1–2 columns of grouped links to the right. All content is left-aligned within its columns, with 16–24px spacing between link rows. The footer begins after a clear gap from the last content section, roughly 64px, ensuring it feels like a separate, final band rather than a continuation of the content stream.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the document flow, occupying full width; on smaller viewports, the columns stack into a single column with left-aligned content and preserved vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-focus-controller is a non-visual, state-management layer that does not occupy explicit visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits behind the main content sections, monitoring scroll position and interaction states such as active search or section selection. When a certain section (e.g., search results or a particular list) is in focus, it may update subtle UI treatments like highlighting a corresponding navigation item in the header or adjusting which section is scrolled into view. It aligns logically with the top-left of the main content area, but has no rendered box, background, or typographic footprint. Its influence is perceived through the behavior of other components rather than through its own visual presence.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered controller that does not take up layout space; it affects scroll behavior, section highlighting, or visibility toggles (such as showing or hiding global-search-results) in response to user actions.

name: Coaches
title: Coaches
description: Browse, filter, and sort available coaches for one-on-one sessions, and open individual coach profiles.

core_features: Fetch dynamic filter and sort options via getCoachFilterOptions and initialize the filter UI based on available specialties, price ranges, session lengths, ratings, review counts, offers, and sort choices.
Parse incoming URL parameters (specialty, maxPrice, sessionLength, offer, sortBy, and optional pagination parameters) to pre-populate filters and sort state before the initial search.

Search and list active coaches using searchCoaches with the current filters, sort order, and pagination state, displaying key information such as name, specialties, price per session, session length options, rating, review count, and free consultation availability.

Allow users to adjust filters (specialty/topic, price range, session length, minimum rating, minimum review count, offers) and immediately update both the URL parameters and searchCoaches query, resetting pagination as needed.
Allow users to change sort order (e.g., by rating, price, review count) using sort options provided by getCoachFilterOptions, updating URL parameters and reloading results via searchCoaches.
Support paginated browsing of coaches via searchCoaches page and pageSize parameters, while preserving all active filters and sort state in the URL.
Provide a clickable coach card for each search result that navigates to coach_detail.html with the corresponding coach ID in the URL parameters.
Handle loading, empty, and error states during calls to getCoachFilterOptions and searchCoaches without using any static or hardcoded data for coaches or options.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Arrive from another page with preselected filters
1. User clicks a link to coaches.html from index.html or blog.html with one or more query parameters (e.g., specialty, maxPrice, sessionLength, offer, sortBy).
2. Coaches page loads and the page controller parses the URL parameters.
3. The page calls getCoachFilterOptions to retrieve all available filter and sort options.
4. Once getCoachFilterOptions resolves, the filter components are initialized: the specialty, price, session length, offer, rating, and review filters are set based on the URL parameters where applicable.
5. The page controller constructs a filters object from the current filter state and calls searchCoaches with filters, sortBy (from URL or default), page (from URL or default), and pageSize.
6. The results list component displays the returned list of coaches and a summary showing the number of results, while pagination controls are configured based on totalCount.
7. The user can further refine or clear filters; each change updates the URL and triggers a new searchCoaches call.

Workflow: Manually filter and sort coaches
1. User navigates directly to coaches.html without specifying filters, or after the initial load described above.
2. The page has already called getCoachFilterOptions and searchCoaches for the initial state.
3. User selects or changes a specialty filter, adjusts the price range, chooses a session length, selects a minimum rating, selects a minimum review count, or toggles an offer (such as free consultation) using the filter UI.
4. On any filter change, the filter component notifies the page controller; the controller updates the URL query string to reflect the new filters and resets the page parameter to the first page.
5. The controller builds an updated filters object and calls searchCoaches with the new filters, the current sortBy value, page=1, and the current pageSize.
6. The list of coaches, summary, and pagination update to reflect the filtered results.
7. The user may then change the sort order using the sort control; the controller updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets page to 1, and calls searchCoaches again.
8. The results list re-renders in the new order, and the URL now fully encodes the active filters and sort state for sharing or bookmarking.

Workflow: Paginate through coaches
1. User is viewing a list of coaches returned by searchCoaches with some combination of filters and sort order.
2. User interacts with pagination controls (e.g., clicks a next/previous page button or selects a specific page number).
3. The pagination component notifies the page controller of the desired page number.
4. The controller updates the page parameter in the URL query string, preserving all existing filter and sort parameters.
5. The controller calls searchCoaches with the same filters and sortBy, but with the new page parameter (and current pageSize).
6. The results list updates to show the new page of coaches; the pagination component reflects the current page.
7. If the user navigates back/forward using the browser controls, the page controller listens for URL changes, re-parses parameters, and re-runs searchCoaches so that the displayed page is always in sync with the URL.

Workflow: Open an individual coach profile
1. User scans the list of coach cards in the results list.
2. User clicks on a specific coach card (any clickable area defined for navigation, such as the coach name or the entire card surface).
3. The coach card component reads the coach's id from the bound Coach data.
4. The page navigates to coach_detail.html with a query parameter for the coach ID (e.g., coach_detail.html?id=<coachId>), without altering any other state on the Coaches page.
5. When the user later returns to coaches.html using browser back, the page controller re-parses the URL (including filters, sort, and page) and calls searchCoaches to restore the same state of the listing.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data loading
- Trigger: Page load event.
- Behavior: Page controller parses URL parameters, calls getCoachFilterOptions once, then initializes filter components. After filters are ready and initial state is known, it calls searchCoaches with the derived filters, sortBy, page, and pageSize. Loading indicators are displayed while these requests are in progress.

Interaction: Filter change
- Trigger: User changes any filter control (specialty selection, price range adjustment, session length selection, minimum rating selection, minimum review count selection, offer toggle).
- Behavior: Filter component emits an onFiltersChange event with the new filter state. Page controller updates the URL query string for all relevant filter parameters and resets page to the first page. Then it calls searchCoaches with the new filters and current sortBy and pageSize. Loading indicators appear for the results area until the call completes.

Interaction: Sort order change
- Trigger: User selects a different sort option from the sort control.
- Behavior: Sort control emits an onSortChange event with the selected sort value. Page controller updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and resets page to the first page, then calls searchCoaches with unchanged filters, the new sortBy, and current pageSize.

Interaction: Pagination
- Trigger: User changes the page via pagination controls.
- Behavior: Pagination component emits an onPageChange event with the target page number. Page controller updates the page parameter in the URL (preserving all other parameters) and calls searchCoaches with the existing filters and sortBy and the new page value.

Interaction: URL change via browser navigation
- Trigger: User clicks the browser back or forward button, causing the query string to change.
- Behavior: Page controller listens for URL change events, re-parses all recognized parameters (filters, sortBy, page, pageSize, and the original incoming parameters), updates the filter and sort components to reflect the new state, and calls searchCoaches accordingly.

Interaction: Coach card click
- Trigger: User clicks on a coach card in the list.
- Behavior: Coach card component reads the bound Coach.id, and the page navigates to coach_detail.html with id set to that value as a URL parameter.

Interaction: Handling no results
- Trigger: searchCoaches returns an empty results array with a totalCount of 0 for the current filters.
- Behavior: Results list displays a functional empty state message and may suggest adjusting filters. Pagination controls are hidden or disabled. No static or example coach data is shown.

Interaction: Error handling
- Trigger: getCoachFilterOptions or searchCoaches rejects or fails.
- Behavior: Page controller displays an error notification component and allows the user to retry loading filters or running the search. No static fallback data is used; the UI only reflects successful responses or error states.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters are the single source of truth for the page's filter, sort, and pagination state.

Recognized inbound parameters:
- specialty (string): Used to set the initial specialty filter. If present, it is mapped to the filter component's selected specialty value and included in the filters object passed to searchCoaches.
- maxPrice (number): Used to set the upper bound of the price range filter. If present, it is applied to the price filter UI and included in the filters object (e.g., as a maximum price_per_session constraint) for searchCoaches.
- sessionLength (number): Used to set an initial session length filter (in minutes). If present, it is mapped to the corresponding session length option and included in filters for searchCoaches.
- offer (string): Used to set an initial offer filter (for example, an offer corresponding to free consultation). If present, it is matched to one of the offerOptions returned by getCoachFilterOptions and added to filters for searchCoaches.
- sortBy (string): Used to set the initial sort order. If present, it is matched against the sortOptions from getCoachFilterOptions and passed as the sortBy argument to searchCoaches.

Additional internal parameters for state (also kept in the URL query string):
- ratingMin (number): Represents the current minimum rating filter selected from ratingOptions. When present, it is included in the filters object passed to searchCoaches as a minimum average_rating constraint.
- reviewCountMin (number): Represents the current minimum review count filter selected from reviewCountOptions. When present, it is included in the filters object passed to searchCoaches as a minimum review_count constraint.
- priceMin (number): Represents the current lower bound of the price range filter (if the UI allows adjusting this). When present, it is included in the filters object as a minimum price_per_session constraint.
- sessionLength (number): Beyond the initial use, the same parameter continues representing the currently selected session length; it is updated whenever the user changes the session length filter.
- page (number): Represents the current page of results. When not provided, the implementation may default to the first page. It is updated whenever the user changes page via pagination controls and is included in the searchCoaches call.
- pageSize (number): Represents the number of results per page. If the UI allows changing it, any change updates this parameter and triggers a searchCoaches call with the new size; otherwise, it may be set once by the implementation.

State update rules:
- On initial load, the page controller reads all recognized URL parameters and constructs a filter state object and other state (sortBy, page, pageSize). It then calls getCoachFilterOptions. When options are loaded, the controller verifies that any parameter values correspond to available options (e.g., matching specialties, offers, sort options, rating options, review count options). Only valid values are applied; invalid values may be ignored in filter state while still remaining in the URL or being sanitized according to implementation.
- After filter options are validated, the controller calls searchCoaches with an object constructed from the active filters (including any of specialty, priceMin, maxPrice, sessionLength, offer, ratingMin, reviewCountMin) plus the sortBy value and current page and pageSize. The data returned populates the results list and pagination.
- Whenever the user changes any filter (including clearing filters), the controller updates or removes the corresponding URL parameters to reflect the new state, resets the page parameter to the first page, and calls searchCoaches with the updated filters and existing sortBy and pageSize.
- Whenever the user changes the sort order, the controller updates the sortBy parameter in the URL, resets the page parameter to the first page, and calls searchCoaches again with unchanged filters and pageSize.
- Whenever the user changes the page via pagination, the controller updates the page parameter (leaving filters and sortBy unchanged) and calls searchCoaches with the same filters and sortBy but new page value.
- If the user navigates using browser history causing the query string to change, the controller re-runs the same initialization logic for state (parse parameters, sync filter/sort components, call searchCoaches) so that the view is always consistent with the URL.
- At no point does the page read from or write to localStorage or other persistent client-side storage; the URL query string and in-memory state during the session are the only state mechanisms used.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, URL parameter parsing, API calls via getCoachFilterOptions and searchCoaches, and synchronization between filters, sort, pagination, and the results list.

Displays and manages all filter controls for specialties, price range, session lengths, minimum rating, minimum review count, and offers using options from getCoachFilterOptions, and emits filter state changes to the page controller.

Shows available sort options from getCoachFilterOptions.sortOptions, reflects the currently active sortBy value from URL state, and notifies the page controller when the user selects a different sort option.
Displays a summary of the search results, such as totalCount returned by searchCoaches, and the range of items currently visible based on page and pageSize.

Renders the list of coach cards using the results array from searchCoaches, passing each Coach record to a coach card component and handling loading, empty, and error display states as directed by the page controller.

Represents a single coach in the results list, showing key fields from the Coach entity (such as name, specialties, price_per_session, session_lengths_minutes, offers_free_consultation, average_rating, review_count, and location if available) and enabling navigation to the coach detail page when clicked.

Provides controls for navigating between result pages based on totalCount from searchCoaches and current page and pageSize, emitting page change events to the page controller and staying in sync with the page URL.
Indicates loading states during calls to getCoachFilterOptions and searchCoaches, particularly for the results area and filters, without providing any static placeholder data.
Displays error messages when getCoachFilterOptions or searchCoaches fail, and offers a way to retry the failed operation via the page controller.
Shows a functional message when searchCoaches returns no results (totalCount is zero) for the current filters, and can provide controls to clear or adjust filters via the filter panel.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global header at the very top (out of scope), then a full-width, dark-teal hero-like page controller band for the Coaches page, and below that a two-column main content area. In the hero band, content is left-aligned within a centered max-width container, with a small category pill ('Coaches'), a large page title, and a short supporting line. Below the hero, the main content splits into a left filter sidebar and a right results column. At the top of the right column, a horizontal control bar holds the results summary on the left and sort control on the right, and below this, the coaches results list fills the column. Pagination sits at the bottom of the results column. Cross-cutting states (loading, error, empty) appear within or over the results column, never breaking the core structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global header at the very top (out of scope), then a full-width, dark-teal hero-like page controller band for the Coaches page, and below that a two-column main content area. In the hero band, content is left-aligned within a centered max-width container, with a small category pill ('Coaches'), a large page title, and a short supporting line. Below the hero, the main content splits into a left filter sidebar and a right results column. At the top of the right column, a horizontal control bar holds the results summary on the left and sort control on the right, and below this, the coaches results list fills the column. Pagination sits at the bottom of the results column. Cross-cutting states (loading, error, empty) appear within or over the results column, never breaking the core structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content sits within a centered container roughly 1140–1280px wide. The hero/page-controller band takes the full width of the container, with about 60% of that width used by the left-aligned text block and the remaining 40% left mostly as calm negative space or light decorative content, echoing the mockup’s hero asymmetry. Vertically, the hero occupies a generous band of about 30–40% of the initial viewport height to create a confident, editorial feel. Below it, the main content area is split into a left column for filters (~30–35% of the container width, about 3–4 grid columns) and a right column for results (~65–70%, about 8–9 grid columns). Major vertical separations between hero and main content, and between the results list and pagination, use approximately 64px spacing, while internal gaps between controls and cards use 16–32px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a low, calm content density consistent with a premium SaaS feel: large headings, comfortable line spacing, and generous padding in all interactive elements. The hero band shows only key information (category, page title, optional short description) without clutter. The filter sidebar groups controls with clear vertical spacing and section headings, avoiding dense, multi-column filter grids. The results column shows a moderate number of coach cards in a clean grid or stacked layout, each card with ample padding and whitespace so no area feels crowded. Controls like the sort dropdown, pagination, and notifications are visually compact but surrounded by breathing room to keep the page feeling light and deliberate.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a horizontal F/Z-pattern similar to the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left of the hero on the small 'Coaches' pill and large page title, moves across the width of the page to the calmer, more open right side, then drops diagonally down into the main content: first to the filter sidebar on the left and the results summary at the top of the results column, then across to the sort control on the top-right of the results area. From there, the eye naturally travels down the vertical stack of coach cards, ending at the pagination controls at the bottom. Error and empty states appear at the top of the results column, intercepting the flow early, while the loading indicator is centered over the results grid, briefly dominating attention without affecting the overall layout structure.

layout.overall_description: Below the site’s fixed global header, the Coaches page opens with a full-width hero-like band on a dark teal background. Within a centered content container, the left two-thirds of this band present a small, pill-shaped label reading 'Coaches' above a large, bold page title such as 'Find your perfect coach', set in white or near-white for strong contrast. A single, short sentence beneath it calmly explains the page’s purpose. The right third of this band is visually lighter: mostly negative space with perhaps a subtle illustrative accent, echoing the mockup’s image area without adding functional clutter. This entire hero band feels spacious, with generous top and bottom padding so the text can breathe. Below, after about 64px of vertical space, the main content area begins. It splits into two columns: a filter sidebar on the left occupying about one-third of the width, and a broader results column on the right taking the other two-thirds. The filter sidebar is a softly elevated panel that runs down the page, with clearly separated filter groups stacked vertically. In the right column, a slim horizontal bar at the top shows the results summary aligned left and the sort control aligned right, both on a subtly lighter surface that stands out from the teal background. Under this bar, the coach cards appear in a clean grid or single-column stack, each white card floating against the dark teal, giving a polished SaaS-listing feel. Pagination sits below the grid, aligned to the right and surrounded by ample whitespace. When loading, a centered spinner overlays just the results area; when errors occur, a prominent notification band appears above the results controls; and when no coaches match the criteria, a clear, centered empty state replaces the cards within the results column. Overall, the page feels modern, calm, and professional, with the hero setting the tone and the filters and results arranged in a clear, confident layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller forms the hero-like band immediately below the global header, stretching full-width across the viewport but constrained to the 1140–1280px centered content container. It sits on the dark teal brand background and uses generous vertical padding (approximately 48–64px top and bottom). Within this band, content is left-aligned and occupies roughly 60% of the container width (about 7 out of 12 grid columns), starting from the left edge. At the top of this text block, a small pill-shaped label (e.g., 'Coaches' or 'Our Coaches') is positioned flush left, with around 8–16px of space above and 16px below it. Directly beneath the pill, a large, multi-line heading spans most of this text column, with ample line height and 8–16px spacing between lines, creating a strong editorial anchor. Optionally, a brief one- or two-line descriptive sentence sits below the heading, separated by about 16–24px. The remaining 40% of the container width on the right side of the hero is mostly open space, potentially used for a subtle illustration or accent but without any of the functional components from this list. The bottom edge of the page-controller band aligns with the top of the main content area, with about 64px of vertical space before the filter sidebar and results controls begin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width hero band anchored below the global header; it does not scroll with the user (no sticky behavior) and remains at the top of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter panel begins just below the hero band, after a vertical gap of roughly 64px, and is positioned on the left side of the main content area. It sits within the same centered content container and occupies approximately 30–35% of the width (about 3–4 grid columns). The panel itself appears as a tall, card-like surface on top of the dark teal background—a slightly lighter teal or off-white surface with subtle shadow and rounded corners—starting near the top of the viewport and extending downward with the content. Inside the panel, filter groups (e.g., specialization, availability, price) are stacked vertically, each group separated by about 24–32px of vertical spacing. Each group has its heading aligned left, with its controls (checkboxes, sliders, tags, etc.) arranged in a single column to maintain low visual density. The panel has comfortable internal padding of about 24–32px on all sides. On wide screens, it runs nearly from just below the hero down to the bottom of the results list; on scroll, it appears pinned relative to the viewport’s top edge so users can adjust filters while browsing results. On narrower screens, this panel can collapse into a top-drawer or overlay, but in the primary desktop layout it remains a left sidebar aligned with the top of the results summary.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, behaves as a sticky left sidebar within the main content area: it remains fixed relative to the viewport top once scrolled into place, while the results list scrolls independently to its right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is positioned at the top of the right-hand results column, horizontally aligned with the results summary. It sits within a slim horizontal control bar that spans the full width of the results column (about 65–70% of the page’s content width, or 8–9 grid columns), starting approximately 64px below the bottom of the hero band. Within this bar, the sort control is right-aligned, hugging the right edge with a margin of about 16–24px from the container edge. Visually, it might appear as a compact labeled dropdown or segmented control, with internal padding around 8–12px vertically and 16–20px horizontally, echoing the spacing of buttons in the design DNA. There is around 16–24px of vertical padding above and below the control bar so the sort control does not feel cramped against the results summary or the first row of cards. This places the sort control in the upper-right quadrant of the main content, making it an easily discoverable secondary adjustment tool that users encounter just before engaging with the list of coach cards.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element anchored to the top-right of the results column; it scrolls away with the content rather than remaining fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results summary shares the same horizontal strip as the sort control at the top of the right-hand results column. It is left-aligned within this bar, starting flush with the left edge of the results column, and typically occupies about 50–60% of the width of that column (roughly 4–5 grid columns). The summary might read 'Showing 12 of 58 coaches' in a medium-weight, smaller type than the hero title, maintaining generous line height. It sits on the same subtle lighter background or divider line as the sort bar to visually define this area as a control/header zone above the results grid. There is about 8–16px of space above the summary text and about 16–24px below it before the first row of coach cards, ensuring that this text does not collide visually with the card content. The alignment and spacing make the summary the first thing the eye encounters when scanning down from the hero into the results area, giving quick feedback on the current filter/sort state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static text-like element within the results column; it scrolls normally with the results list, with no sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results list occupies the entirety of the right-hand column beneath the control bar that contains the results summary and sort control. It starts about 16–24px below this control bar, spanning the full width of the results column (8–9 grid columns, approximately 65–70% of the central container). On desktop, coach cards are arranged in a low-density grid, typically two cards per row, each card taking about 48–50% of the results column width with a horizontal gap of around 24px between them and vertical gaps of 24–32px between rows. The list extends downward as needed, providing a clear, scrollable area of white cards floating on the dark teal page background. The left edge of the list aligns vertically with the left edge of the results summary; the right edge aligns with the sort control above, keeping a clean visual column. There is ample bottom margin (about 32–48px) between the last row of cards and the pagination controls, preserving the airy feel. On narrow screens, the layout gracefully stacks into a single column of full-width cards, maintaining the same vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrollable content region within the right-hand column; it expands vertically with results and scrolls behind any sticky filter panel on the left.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each coach card appears as a distinct, elevated tile within the results list, set against the dark teal background. On desktop, each card typically occupies roughly half of the results column width minus the inter-card gutter, so two cards sit side by side in each row. The cards have a white or very light background, subtle shadow, and rounded corners to reinforce the premium SaaS aesthetic. Internal padding is generous—about 24px on all sides—to keep the card’s contents (coach photo, name, specialization, rating, key tags, and primary call-to-action) from feeling cramped. The coach’s photo or avatar is usually positioned towards the top-left of the card, with text content aligned to its right or directly beneath, and the primary CTA (e.g., 'View profile' or 'Book session') aligned towards the bottom or bottom-right, following a simple top-to-bottom reading order. Vertical spacing between the card’s internal elements (name, role, attributes) is typically 8–16px, mirroring the base spacing scale. Around each card, there is a consistent gap of about 24px to adjacent cards horizontally and vertically, so no card edges touch. The cards form a clean, rhythmical grid that supports rapid scanning without visual overload.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Cards are static items within the grid; they align themselves responsive to the results column (two per row on wide screens, stacking to one per row as the viewport narrows).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls sit directly below the results list in the right-hand column, separated from the final row of coach cards by a generous vertical gap of about 32–48px to maintain visual clarity. They are horizontally aligned toward the right edge of the results column, echoing the alignment of the sort control above, while still leaving some right padding (around 16–24px) from the container edge. The controls themselves—page numbers, previous/next buttons, or 'Load more'—are arranged in a single horizontal row with consistent spacing of about 8–16px between elements. Each pagination button or pill uses the same component padding as other interactive elements (roughly 8–12px vertically and 16–20px horizontally) and might include a subtle border or filled style for the active page. Below the pagination, there is additional bottom margin (about 48–64px) before the end of the main content area, preserving the spacious, editorial finish to the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control fixed to the bottom of the current result set; it scrolls with the results and remains within the right-hand column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears in the context of the results column rather than taking over the entire viewport. When active, it is centered both horizontally and vertically within the visible area of the results list. On initial load or during heavy filter/sort changes, it may be presented as a spinner or subtle animated icon sitting on a semi-transparent overlay that covers only the results list region (the grid area where cards normally appear), leaving the hero, filter sidebar, and control bar visible but visually subdued behind the overlay. The overlay slightly darkens or blurs the card area beneath, with padding of at least 32–48px around the indicator itself, ensuring it is clearly in focus without feeling aggressive. In cases where the list is empty during loading (e.g., first load), the indicator sits roughly at the vertical center of the results column, maintaining consistent margins to the top controls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays the results list area as a centered floating layer; it is positioned relative to the results column and disappears once data is loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notification appears as a full-width alert bar within the central content container, specifically anchored at the top of the right-hand results column but below the hero band. It spans the same width as the results column (aligned with its left and right edges) and pushes the results summary and sort control downwards while it is visible. The bar has a high-contrast background (for example, a desaturated red or warm tone over the dark teal environment) and rounded corners consistent with other surface elements, with internal padding around 16–24px. The error message text is left-aligned within the bar, and any dismiss or retry action is aligned to the right within the same bar, keeping inter-element spacing of about 8–16px. Vertically, there is around 24px of space between the bottom of the error notification and the control bar (summary and sort), maintaining clarity between the error state and the standard controls. The filter panel remains visible and unchanged to the left, so users understand they can adjust filters even while an error is present.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline banner inserted above the results summary within the right-hand column; it scrolls away with the content and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state occupies the same spatial area as the results list within the right-hand column whenever no coaches match the current filters. The control bar at the top (results summary and sort control) remains visible above, and the filter sidebar on the left stays unchanged, emphasizing that the page structure is intact. Below the control bar, instead of the grid of coach cards, a single, centered empty-state panel appears. This panel is positioned in the middle of the visible portion of the results column, with ample top and bottom padding (about 64–96px) to feel calm and deliberate. It typically consists of a simple illustration or icon at the top, a concise headline such as 'No coaches match your filters', and a short descriptive line suggesting next steps (e.g., 'Try adjusting your specialization or availability filters'), all center-aligned. Any action buttons (like 'Clear filters') are placed directly beneath the message with a comfortable 16–24px spacing. Horizontally, the content is centered and constrained to about 50–60% of the results column width so it doesn’t stretch too wide. The empty-state background remains the same dark teal as the page, with the panel content sitting on a lighter card or simply on the background, depending on the illustration style, always maintaining generous whitespace around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline replacement for the results list within the right-hand column; it scrolls normally and disappears as soon as results become available.

name: Coach Profile
title: Coach Profile
description: Displays details for a selected coach, with options to book a session, send a contact message, or request a free consultation, based on dynamic data loaded via interfaces.

core_features: Load and display full coach details (bio, specialties, session options, pricing, ratings, review count) using getCoachProfile based on the coach ID URL parameter.
Display a booking widget that lets users select session length, date range, and an available time slot using getCoachAvailability.
Provide a booking form that captures client details (name, email, goals/notes, optional topic) and books a session via bookCoachSession for the selected availability slot.
Provide a contact form and free consultation request option (when available) that capture name, email, preferred time of day, and message, and send them via sendCoachContactMessage.
Display success and error feedback after booking or sending contact/consultation messages based on responses from bookCoachSession and sendCoachContactMessage.
List and summarize coach reviews, using review information returned within getCoachProfile.
user_workflows:
View coach details workflow:
1. User navigates to coach_detail.html with a URL parameter specifying the coach ID (e.g., coach_detail.html?id={coachId}).
2. On page initialization, the page reads the id parameter from the URL.
3. The page calls getCoachProfile with coachId=id.
4. When the response returns, the page binds coach, reviews, averageRating, reviewCount, and availableSessionLengthsMinutes to the relevant components.
5. The user can scroll to view basic info, specialties, pricing, ratings, and reviews.

Book a coaching session workflow:
1. After the coach profile loads, the booking widget pre-selects a default session length (if any) from availableSessionLengthsMinutes and may read a sessionLengthMinutes value from the URL parameter if present.
2. The booking widget reads an optional date parameter from the URL; if present, it uses this as the initial date or date range, otherwise it uses an interface-driven default (such as the earliest date with availability derived from getCoachAvailability).
3. The widget calls getCoachAvailability with coachId, startDate, endDate, and the selected sessionLengthMinutes to load available slots.
4. The user selects a session length option, updating the selected sessionLengthMinutes state and triggering a new getCoachAvailability call for the chosen length and date range. The selected session length is written to the URL as a query parameter.
5. The user navigates between dates using the date selector or calendar, updating the startDate and endDate in the component state and optionally encoding them into URL parameters, and each change triggers getCoachAvailability.
6. The user clicks on a specific available time slot, which sets a selected availabilitySlotId and start_datetime in the booking component state.
7. Once a slot is selected, the booking form is enabled, pre-filled with the chosen session length and computed price from the coach data (price_per_session and/or other pricing logic managed outside this page).
8. The user enters clientName, clientEmail, optional topic, and goalsNotes.
9. The user submits the booking form, which triggers bookCoachSession with coachId, availabilitySlotId, sessionLengthMinutes, topic, clientName, clientEmail, and goalsNotes.
10. If bookCoachSession returns success=true, the page displays a booking confirmation message (and may show basic booking details from booking) and clears or locks the form. If success=false, an error message is displayed and the slot selection and form remain available for correction and resubmission.

Send a general contact message workflow:
1. On the coach profile page, the user navigates to the contact section (for example via a tab or internal link; the selected section can be reflected in a URL parameter such as view=contact).
2. The contact form is pre-bound with coachId from the page state derived from the URL.
3. The user fills in name, email, selects preferred_time_of_day from the defined enum values, and types a message.
4. The user submits the contact form, which triggers sendCoachContactMessage with coachId, name, email, preferredTimeOfDay, message, and isFreeConsultationRequest=false.
5. If the response indicates success=true, a confirmation messageText is displayed and the form may reset. If success=false, an error message is shown and the user can correct the inputs and resubmit.

Request a free consultation workflow (for coaches that offer it):
1. After getCoachProfile loads, if coach.offers_free_consultation is true, the contact section enables a dedicated free consultation mode (for example, a toggle or separate button) and may reflect this selection in the URL with a parameter such as consultation=free.
2. When free consultation mode is active, the contact form may pre-fill or adjust the message field (via non-hardcoded, implementation-specific messaging) and sets isFreeConsultationRequest=true in the internal state.
3. The user fills in name, email, selects preferred_time_of_day, and optionally edits the message.
4. The user submits the form, which triggers sendCoachContactMessage with isFreeConsultationRequest=true and the other fields as entered.
5. On success, the page displays confirmation using messageText from the response; on failure, it displays an error and allows resubmission.

Review exploration workflow:
1. When getCoachProfile returns, the page binds reviews, averageRating, and reviewCount to the review listing component.
2. The user can expand or collapse review details, navigate through pages of reviews if pagination is implemented, or sort/filter locally (any such sorting/filtering is done purely on the reviews collection returned by getCoachProfile and does not call additional interfaces).
3. The page may use URL parameters (e.g., view=reviews) to deep-link directly into the reviews section; when such a parameter is present, the review section is scrolled into view or set as the active tab on load.
interactions: Click 'Back to all coaches': Navigates to coaches.html without modifying data. Uses an anchor or navigation handler that disregards the current page's dynamic state.

Change session length in booking widget: When the user changes the selected session length option, the booking component updates its internal selectedSessionLengthMinutes state, writes this value to a sessionLengthMinutes URL parameter, clears the previously selected availability slot (if any), and triggers getCoachAvailability with the updated sessionLengthMinutes and the current date range.

Change date or date range for availability: When the user changes the selected date or date range in the booking widget, the component updates its startDate and endDate state (and optional matching URL parameters), resets the currently selected availability slot if it no longer falls in the visible range, and calls getCoachAvailability with coachId, startDate, endDate, and the selected sessionLengthMinutes.

Select an availability slot: When the user clicks on an available time slot in the availability list or calendar view, the component updates selectedAvailabilitySlotId and the associated start_datetime in its state. It enables the booking form submit button and optionally encodes the slot selection in a URL parameter such as slotId for deep-linking into a preselected time (implementations must ensure that rehydration from URL parameters is validated against fresh getCoachAvailability data before submission).

Fill booking form fields: As the user types into the clientName, clientEmail, topic, and goalsNotes fields, the booking component maintains these values in state. Client-side validation may mark required fields as invalid before submission; no static default values are hardcoded for these fields.

Submit booking form: When the user submits the form, if required fields are present and a selectedAvailabilitySlotId and sessionLengthMinutes are set, the component calls bookCoachSession. While the call is in progress, the submit button can be disabled and a loading state displayed. On success, it shows a confirmation message; on error, it shows an error state based on the returned message and allows retry.

Switch between 'Booking', 'Contact', and 'Reviews' sections: If the page uses a tabbed or segmented interface, clicking a tab updates the activeSection state and writes a view URL parameter (e.g., view=booking, view=contact, or view=reviews). On loading the page, the component reads this value and sets the initial section accordingly.

Change contact form preferred time of day: The user selects a preferred_time_of_day from the defined enum values (such as morning, afternoon, evening, or anytime). The selection is maintained in the contact form state and passed directly as preferredTimeOfDay to sendCoachContactMessage upon submission.

Toggle or choose 'Free consultation' mode: When the coach supports free consultations, the user can activate a free consultation mode (for example via a dedicated button). This sets an internal isFreeConsultationRequest flag to true, may adjust form labels/message placeholders dynamically (without hardcoded static text in the specification), and can write consultation=free to the URL. Deactivating this mode sets isFreeConsultationRequest to false and may remove or update the consultation-related parameter.

Submit contact or free consultation form: On submit, the component validates required fields, then calls sendCoachContactMessage with the current form state. It displays loading, success, or error messages based on the response. On success, it may clear the form and update the URL to remove transient states such as a slotId or other booking-specific parameters while retaining the coachId.

Scroll or navigate within reviews: Users can click controls to show more reviews, navigate pages, or expand full review text. These interactions update only client-side state within the review list component and do not invoke additional interfaces, as all review data is provided via getCoachProfile.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary page state is derived from and synchronized with URL parameters, supplemented by in-memory component state.

URL parameters:
- id (required): Represents the coachId whose profile should be displayed. On page load, if id is missing or invalid, the page displays an error and does not call any interfaces. When present, the page calls getCoachProfile with coachId=id. All subsequent calls to getCoachAvailability, bookCoachSession, and sendCoachContactMessage use this same coachId.
