- view (optional): Controls which main section is active (e.g., booking, contact, reviews). On load, if view is set to a known value, the corresponding section is activated. When the user switches sections via the UI, the page updates the view parameter accordingly using the current routing mechanism.
- sessionLengthMinutes (optional): Represents the currently selected session length in minutes. On load, if present and if it exists in availableSessionLengthsMinutes returned by getCoachProfile, this value is used as the initial session length in the booking widget. If it is absent or invalid, the booking widget selects an initial session length using an interface-driven or application-level default and may then update the URL with that value. When the user changes the selected session length, this parameter is updated and a new availability query is made.
- date or date-related parameters (optional): The page may use one or more parameters (such as date, startDate, and endDate) to reflect the current date filter in the booking widget. On load, if these parameters are present, they are parsed and used as the initial date range. If they are missing or invalid, the booking widget initializes its date range using an application-level default (for example, a period around the current date) and may write the resolved dates back into the URL. Any change of dates by the user results in refreshed getCoachAvailability calls and updated date parameters.
- slotId (optional): May represent a preselected availabilitySlotId. On load, if slotId is present, the booking widget loads availability via getCoachAvailability and then checks whether an availability slot with that ID exists, is not booked, and falls within the date range and session length specified or implied by other parameters. If valid, it sets selectedAvailabilitySlotId to that slot and highlights it. If invalid, it clears the selection and may remove slotId from the URL without persisting outdated state.
- consultation (optional): May reflect whether the contact form is currently in free consultation mode (e.g., consultation=free) or standard mode (parameter absent or using another predefined value). On load, this parameter initializes the isFreeConsultationRequest flag in the contact component. When the user toggles consultation mode, the parameter is updated to keep the URL in sync.

State transitions:
- Initial state: On first load (or when id changes), the page clears all coach-related state, shows a loading indicator, and calls getCoachProfile. Only after this call resolves successfully are the booking widget, contact form, and reviews enabled.
- After getCoachProfile success: The coach data and related aggregates are stored in the page-level state. The page then reads sessionLengthMinutes and date-related URL parameters to seed the booking widget state. If initial parameters are incomplete, it uses defaults derived from the interface outputs or application-level rules, and may update the URL accordingly.
- Booking widget state: Consists of selectedSessionLengthMinutes, a date range, the list of availability slots returned by getCoachAvailability, and selectedAvailabilitySlotId. Any change to the date range or session length triggers a new getCoachAvailability call and resets selectedAvailabilitySlotId if it no longer matches the new filter. The URL is updated to reflect the current session length and date range.
- Booking form submission state: Tracks whether a submission is in progress, the last response from bookCoachSession, and any validation errors. On success, it stores booking in state for display in the confirmation component and may clear selectedAvailabilitySlotId to prevent accidental duplicate submissions. The URL retains id but generally does not encode transient submission state.
- Contact form state: Includes name, email, preferredTimeOfDay, message, and isFreeConsultationRequest. It is initialized from URL parameters (consultation and any others the implementation supports) and updates in response to user input. On successful sendCoachContactMessage, the component may reset its internal state or retain some values (such as name and email) for convenience while clearing the message. The URL may clear consultation when appropriate.
- Error and loading states: If getCoachProfile or getCoachAvailability fails, the corresponding components show error messages and retry options without altering the id parameter. If bookCoachSession or sendCoachContactMessage fails, the error component receives the message from the interface response and displays it; no external state is persisted other than showing the error. No state is stored in localStorage; all cross-navigation state is encoded in the URL parameters as described.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, reads URL parameters, triggers data loading via getCoachProfile, manages top-level state for coach data, and passes props/state down to child components.

Displays coach name, photo, location (if available), specialties, average rating, review count, and indicators such as offers_free_consultation and is_active, based entirely on data from getCoachProfile.
Provides a clickable link that navigates the user back to the coaches listing page without altering any stored data.
Allows the user to switch between main sections such as booking, contact, and reviews, keeping the active section synchronized with the view URL parameter.
Handles selection of session length and date range, loads availability via getCoachAvailability, and tracks the currently selected availability slot to be used by the booking form.
Displays the list or calendar of available CoachAvailabilitySlot entries returned by getCoachAvailability, allows the user to select a single slot, and visually indicates which slot is selected.

Collects clientName, clientEmail, topic, and goalsNotes for the currently selected coach and availability slot, then calls bookCoachSession to create a SessionBooking and displays success or error feedback.

Displays a confirmation message and key booking details when bookCoachSession returns success=true, or an error message when success=false, based on the message field from the interface response and the SessionBooking object.

Provides a form for sending a general contact message to the coach, capturing name, email, preferred_time_of_day, and message, then calling sendCoachContactMessage with isFreeConsultationRequest=false and handling the response.

If coach.offers_free_consultation is true, allows the user to toggle into a free consultation request mode that sets isFreeConsultationRequest=true for subsequent sendCoachContactMessage calls and adjusts the contact form behavior accordingly.
Shows success or error messages after sendCoachContactMessage calls, using the messageText in the interface response to inform the user about contact or consultation submission status.

Displays the list of CoachReview entries, along with aggregate information such as averageRating and reviewCount from getCoachProfile, and supports local pagination, sorting, or expansion of review details.
Shows extended coach information such as bio, specialties, and session_lengths_minutes based on the Coach entity from getCoachProfile, to help users decide whether to book or contact the coach.
Displays loading indicators while getCoachProfile or getCoachAvailability requests are in progress, and hides itself when the corresponding data is loaded or when an error occurs.
Displays error messages for failures of getCoachProfile, getCoachAvailability, bookCoachSession, or sendCoachContactMessage, and may offer retry actions where applicable.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure inside a centered max-width container (≈1140–1280px). Immediately below the global site header, a slim back-navigation row sits at the top-left, followed by a hero-like coach summary section that mirrors the mockup’s two-column blog hero: coach information on the left, coach photo on the right. Beneath this hero, a full-width tab strip allows switching between logical content groups (e.g., Overview, Booking, Contact, Reviews). The content under the tabs is arranged in vertically stacked sections, each left-aligned within the same central content column: bio/details, reviews, booking block, and contact block. All elements respect the same underlying 12-column grid and left-aligned editorial flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom structure inside a centered max-width container (≈1140–1280px). Immediately below the global site header, a slim back-navigation row sits at the top-left, followed by a hero-like coach summary section that mirrors the mockup’s two-column blog hero: coach information on the left, coach photo on the right. Beneath this hero, a full-width tab strip allows switching between logical content groups (e.g., Overview, Booking, Contact, Reviews). The content under the tabs is arranged in vertically stacked sections, each left-aligned within the same central content column: bio/details, reviews, booking block, and contact block. All elements respect the same underlying 12-column grid and left-aligned editorial flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height. The coach header hero takes the majority of the initial above-the-fold area (≈45–60% of viewport height), with around 55–60% of the width dedicated to the text/controls and 40–45% to the photo, echoing the mockup. Below the hero, the tab bar is compact but full-width. Each main content section under the tabs uses the same centered column, with generous vertical spacing (≈64px) between sections and 24–32px padding within. The booking and contact areas occupy the full content width in their tab context, but inner elements are spaced to keep a light, uncluttered feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page maintains a low content density: large headings, ample line height, and substantial white (or dark teal) space around all blocks. Only essential information is surfaced at each level: the hero focuses on identity and primary CTAs; the tab content shows one main task-flow at a time (overview, booking, or contact) instead of crowding everything simultaneously. Controls and form fields are grouped into clearly separated clusters with 24–32px gaps, while micro-elements (links, toggles, pills) use 8–16px spacing in line with the 8px base scale.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F/Z pattern inspired by the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left with the back link, sweeps right across the global header/nav (outside this spec), then drops into the hero: first the coach category/name/role and primary CTAs on the left, then moves horizontally to the right-aligned coach image. Scrolling down, the eye encounters the tab bar spanning left to right, then descends through each content section, which is left-weighted: section headings and key messages anchor on the left, while secondary information and controls are arranged beneath or to the right within each section. Sticky behavior of the tab bar (if applied) reinforces this horizontal top edge of the F-pattern during scroll.

layout.overall_description: The Coach Profile page feels like a polished SaaS detail view translated into a life-coaching context. Inside a dark teal, full-width background, a centered content column creates a calm, premium frame. At the top, a subtle back link anchors the page in the coaches listing. Just below, a hero-style coach summary spans most of the first screen: on the left, the coach’s name, specialty, and a small set of primary actions are set in large, confident type, with a pill-like label echoing the original ‘Blog’ category chip. On the right, a tall, rectangular coach portrait aligns visually with the text’s mid-to-upper area, occupying about 40% of the width and creating a strong two-column tension. Generous negative space around both columns keeps the layout airy. Beneath this hero, a clean, horizontal tab strip allows users to switch between overview, booking, and contact/reviews modes. As users scroll or switch tabs, they move through one well-defined block at a time: an overview area for bio and details, a booking flow with availability and forms, and a contact section with a free-consultation toggle—all separated by wide vertical breathing room. Confirmation banners slide into view as slim, full-width strips above their corresponding sections, while loading and error states gracefully overlay or replace the main content when needed. The overall effect is uncluttered, intentional, and professional, with a clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading path that mirrors the original editorial hero design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller is an invisible structural layer that defines the central content frame. It spans the full viewport width but constrains all child components to a centered content container, approximately 1140–1280px wide, aligned horizontally in the middle of the screen. It sets a consistent top margin below the global header (≈64px) before the first in-page element (the back link), and establishes the vertical rhythm—using roughly 64px spacing between major sections (hero, tab bar, main content blocks) and 24–32px between related sub-elements. All visible components described below flow within this controller’s stacking context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines the main layout grid and vertical rhythm; participates in standard document flow without being visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coach header section functions as the hero and occupies the upper portion of the page content, immediately below the back link and header spacing. It stretches the full width of the centered content container and rises to about 45–60% of the viewport height, depending on content. Inside, it uses a two-column layout aligned to a 12-column grid: the left column spans roughly 7 of 12 columns (≈55–60% width) and the right column spans 5 of 12 columns (≈40–45% width). The background continues the dark teal brand color, while the content sits on this field with generous internal padding (≈32–48px on all sides). In the left column, a small category pill (e.g., “Coach Profile” or specialization) appears at the top-left, followed by a large, multi-line heading with the coach’s name and primary descriptor beneath it. Supporting meta-details (location, modalities, session price range) stack with 16–24px vertical spacing below the heading. Primary actions like “Book a Session” and “Contact Coach” are placed in a horizontal row or tight vertical stack beneath the meta-details, aligned to the left. In the right column, a tall, rectangular coach portrait or card vertically aligns so that its top edge is roughly level with the middle or upper third of the heading block, echoing the original hero image alignment. There is visible breathing room between the right edge of the image and the container, preserving the spacious, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, non-sticky hero section anchored near the top of the page, expanding in height based on content but maintaining a strong two-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-coaches link is a small, understated navigation element positioned at the very top-left of the page content area, directly under the global header and inside the central content container. It sits on its own row above the coach header hero, aligned with the left margin of the hero’s text column. Vertically, it has comfortable top and bottom padding (≈16–24px) to avoid crowding the hero, but remains visually compact. The link may be accompanied by a left-pointing chevron icon, reinforcing its role as a breadcrumb-like control. It does not span the full width; its visual footprint is limited to the space needed for the link label, leaving generous empty space to its right across the row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the normal document flow; serves as a starting point in the visual hierarchy for returning navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coach detail tabs form a horizontal section-switcher placed directly below the coach header hero, separated from it by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px. The tab bar spans the full width of the central content container but is visually anchored to the left, with tabs arranged left-to-right in a single row. Each tab label is sized with medium-large text for legibility, and the active tab is emphasized via a high-contrast underline or pill-style background. There is ample horizontal spacing (≈24–32px) between tab labels to maintain the clean, low-density feel. A subtle bottom border or divider line can stretch across the entire width of the tab bar to visually separate it from the content below. As the user scrolls, the tab bar may become sticky at the top of the viewport (just under the global header), maintaining a fixed horizontal band and ensuring quick access to section switching while the underlying content scrolls beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Spans full content width; optionally becomes sticky at the top of the viewport after scrolling past the hero, while the main content scrolls underneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking widget appears within the content region controlled by the coach-detail-tabs, typically under a ‘Booking’ tab. When active, it is the first major block below the tab bar for that tab. It is aligned to the left edge of the content container and spans roughly 70–80% of the width, leaving breathing space on the right to maintain the editorial aesthetic. The widget itself is a card-like block with soft corners and internal padding of about 24–32px. At the top of the card, a clear section title (e.g., “Book a Session”) sits in larger type, followed by a short explanatory line. Below this, selection controls (service type, duration, session format) are arranged either in a single row wrapping on smaller screens or in two compact rows with 16–24px vertical spacing, ensuring the widget does not feel crowded. The right and bottom edges of the card maintain at least 32px of margin from neighboring elements, giving this booking block clear prominence within its tab section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard card within the Booking tab; stays within normal scroll flow, always anchored below the tab bar when the Booking tab is selected.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability list or calendar sits directly below the booking-widget within the same Booking tab content, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 24–32px. It is horizontally aligned with the booking-widget, matching its left edge and width (≈70–80% of the content container) to visually read as part of the same flow. If a calendar view is used, it appears as a clean, lightly bordered grid with generous cell padding; if a list view is used, it appears as vertically stacked time slots with 8–16px spacing between entries. A subheading (e.g., “Available Times”) may sit above the component, left-aligned and slightly smaller than the main section heading to keep the hierarchy clear. The spacing around the calendar/list ensures that no other elements crowd it from the right or bottom, preserving easy legibility and a calm layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, scrollable content within the Booking tab, expanding vertically as needed based on the amount of availability displayed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The session booking form is positioned below the availability component within the Booking tab, separated by a clear vertical gap of about 32–48px to signal a new step. It aligns to the same left edge and width as the booking-widget (≈70–80% of content width), forming a cohesive column of booking-related controls. The form is arranged in one main column of fields with a consistent vertical rhythm (≈16–24px between fields). Key fields such as name, email, and notes appear in a logical order, with any optional fields visually de-emphasized. At the bottom of this column, the primary ‘Confirm Booking’ button is left-aligned, with ample padding (≈12px vertical, 24px horizontal) and clear separation (≈24–32px) from the last input field. The entire form block has enough padding and spacing that it feels like a distinct, approachable step, not a dense form wall.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level form within the Booking tab; expands in height as fields are added and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking confirmation banner appears only after a successful booking and is visually tied to the Booking section. When shown, it is inserted as a slim but full-width strip at the top of the Booking tab’s content area, directly beneath the coach-detail-tabs and above the booking-widget. It spans the entire width of the central content container with a contrasting but calm background color (e.g., a soft green overlay on top of the dark teal theme) and includes a success icon and concise text aligned to the left, with generous horizontal padding (≈24–32px). Vertical height is modest (≈56–72px) to convey feedback without overwhelming the layout. Its presence pushes the booking-widget and subsequent elements downward but maintains the same central alignment and spacing logic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears conditionally; occupies a fixed position at the top of the Booking tab’s scrollable area, moving with the content as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form section is the primary block within the Contact-related content area (often under a dedicated ‘Contact’ tab). When active, it sits directly beneath the coach-detail-tabs (or beneath any contact-specific confirmation banner if present), aligned to the left edge of the central content container. It spans about 70–80% of the width for the form body, with the remaining space on the right left intentionally empty to preserve the light, editorial feeling. At the top of the section, a prominent heading like “Send a Message” appears, followed by a single-sentence description. The form fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px spacing, similar to the booking form, and any grouped fields (e.g., name + email) may be laid out side by side within the same width if space allows. The submit button is placed at the bottom-left of the form with generous margin (≈24px) above and below. The entire section is separated from adjacent sections (e.g., reviews or overview content, if shown in the same tab) by about 64px vertical spacing, clearly framing the contact action as its own task.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard section within its tab, expanding vertically with content and scrolling with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The free-consultation toggle is a compact control embedded near the top of the contact-form section, positioned on the same horizontal line as or just below the section heading. It is right-aligned within the 70–80% width used by the contact form: the left side carries the heading and description, while the toggle floats to the upper-right corner of the form block, with about 16–24px of separation from the form’s outer edges. The control itself appears as a pill-style switch with a concise label (e.g., “Request free consultation”), sized comfortably with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. When activated, it may subtly adjust the form fields below (for example, revealing different input fields or notes), but the spacing between elements remains generous to avoid crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the contact-form section; maintains a fixed position at the top-right of the form block and moves with the section as the user scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact confirmation banner appears on successful submission of the contact form. It is positioned directly above the contact-form section within its tab, spanning the full width of the central content container in a slim strip similar to the booking confirmation banner. The banner is left-aligned with its content: a small success icon and a short confirmation message. It uses a calm, positive background color distinct from, but harmonious with, the primary dark teal, and includes ample internal padding (≈16–24px). When present, it pushes the contact-form section downward, preserving the overall vertical rhythm and not overlapping other content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally rendered; sits at the top of the Contact tab content and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coach reviews section is a substantial block that can appear under an ‘Overview’ or ‘Reviews’ tab, or as a dedicated tab’s primary content. It is placed below the coach-bio-and-details when on the same tab, separated by a generous 64px vertical gap. The section heading (e.g., “Client Reviews”) is left-aligned at the top, matching the typographic scale of other section titles. Below it, individual reviews are arranged in a clear, vertical list, each occupying the full 70–80% content width used elsewhere, with 24–32px spacing between review cards. Each review card has modest padding and may include star ratings, names, dates, and the review text. The empty space on the right side of the section is preserved to maintain a low-content-density feel and visual cohesion with other sections. A ‘Load more’ link or pagination control, if present, appears at the bottom-left of the section, with adequate separation from the last review.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable section within its tab; grows vertically with the number of reviews and keeps to the central column alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coach bio and details section is the core ‘Overview’ content block. It appears as the first major section below the coach-detail-tabs when the Overview tab is active. The section spans the primary content width (≈70–80% of the central container) and is left-aligned, with a section heading like “About [Coach Name]” at the top. Below the heading, the coach’s bio text flows in multi-line paragraphs with generous line height and 24–32px spacing between paragraphs. Additional structured details (credentials, specialties, languages, approaches) may be placed in a lightly separated sub-block at the bottom or side of the text, potentially arranged as two columns of bullets if space allows but still within the same overall width. There is a clear vertical gap (≈64px) below this section before the next major block (e.g., reviews), reinforcing the editorial, long-form reading experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard content section within its tab, occupying a prominent position in the scroll flow and expanding vertically based on text length.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page loading indicator appears over the main content area when the coach profile data is being fetched. It overlays the central content container with a semi-transparent dark teal or neutral scrim that subtly dims the underlying layout while keeping the global header visible. At the center of the viewport, a minimalist spinner or progress indicator is accompanied by a short loading label in medium-sized text. The overlay covers from just below the global nav down to the bottom of the viewport, aligning with the width of the main content but extending edge-to-edge horizontally on smaller screens. It intentionally keeps the interface clean, avoiding any clutter or additional text beyond what is necessary to communicate that loading is in progress.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily fixed overlay above all other page content (except maybe the global header), blocking interaction with underlying elements until loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page error state replaces the main content area when a critical error occurs (for example, failing to load the coach data). It occupies the same central content container beneath the global header, with ample vertical padding so that the error message is visually centered or slightly above center in the viewport. The error block spans approximately 60–70% of the container width, centered horizontally to emphasize the message. A simple icon, a concise headline (e.g., “We couldn’t load this coach right now”), and a short explanatory paragraph are stacked with comfortable 16–24px spacing. A primary or secondary button (e.g., “Try again” or “Back to all coaches”) is placed below the text, centered or left-aligned, with generous padding to remain easy to interact with. No other page sections (hero, tabs, content blocks) are visible behind this state, reducing cognitive load and clearly signaling that resolution is required.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces the standard page content within the central container; remains static in the scroll flow (often filling the first viewport) until dismissed or resolved.

name: Services & Packages
title: Services & Packages
description: Browse, filter, and sort coaching packages, and navigate to detailed package information using dynamic data from backend interfaces.

core_features: Display a paginated list of active coaching packages retrieved via searchCoachingPackages, showing key fields such as name, topic, sessions_count, and price.
Allow users to filter packages by topic, number of sessions, and price range using options provided dynamically by getPackageFilterOptions.
Allow users to sort packages by available sort options (e.g., price or popularity) provided by getPackageFilterOptions and applied via searchCoachingPackages.
Support deep-linking and pre-filtered views using URL query parameters (topic, sessionsCount, maxPrice, sortBy, page, pageSize) and keep UI controls in sync with those parameters.
Enable navigation to a specific package detail page (package_detail.html) by clicking a package card in the list, passing the selected package ID as a URL parameter.
Provide navigation to the cart page (cart.html) via a cart icon in the header, without performing any cart mutations from this page.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Land on Services & Packages with or without pre-filters
Step 1: User opens services.html directly or via navigation from another page (e.g., index.html or blog.html), optionally with URL parameters such as topic, sessionsCount, maxPrice, and sortBy.
Step 2: The page reads current URL parameters and constructs an initial filters object and sortBy value.
Step 3: The page calls getPackageFilterOptions to retrieve dynamic topic options, sessionCountOptions, priceRange, and sortOptions.
Step 4: Filter and sort UI controls are initialized so their selected values match the current URL parameters where applicable.
Step 5: The page calls searchCoachingPackages with the derived filters, sortBy, and pagination values (page and pageSize), then renders the returned list of CoachingPackage results and totalCount.
Step 6: The user sees a package listing that already reflects any pre-filters encoded in the URL.

Workflow: Adjust filters (topic, sessions, price) and refresh results
Step 1: User changes a filter in the filter component (e.g., selects a different topic, session count, or adjusts the price range within the allowed bounds from getPackageFilterOptions.priceRange).
Step 2: The filter component emits a filter-change event with the updated filter values.
Step 3: A state management handler converts the filter values into URL query parameters (topic, sessionsCount, maxPrice, and any other filter-relevant params) and updates the browser URL (e.g., via history.replaceState or navigation) so that it reflects the new filters, while resetting the page parameter to the first page.
Step 4: After the URL changes, the page constructs a new filters object from the URL and calls searchCoachingPackages again with the updated filters and current sortBy.
Step 5: The package list component receives the new results and re-renders, and the results summary and pagination components update accordingly.

Workflow: Change sort order
Step 1: User selects a different sort option from the sort control, using options returned by getPackageFilterOptions.sortOptions.
Step 2: The sort control emits a sort-change event with the selected sortBy value.
Step 3: The URL state handler updates or adds the sortBy query parameter to the URL and typically resets the page parameter to the first page.
Step 4: The page reads the updated URL, rebuilds the filters and sortBy, and calls searchCoachingPackages with the same filters but new sortBy.
Step 5: The package list re-renders in the new sort order, and the sort control remains in sync with the sortBy parameter in the URL.

Workflow: Paginate through results
Step 1: User clicks a pagination control (e.g., next page or a specific page number) in the pagination component.
Step 2: The pagination component emits a page-change event containing the new page index.
Step 3: The URL state handler updates or adds the page query parameter in the URL, leaving other parameters (topic, sessionsCount, maxPrice, sortBy, pageSize if present) unchanged.
Step 4: The page reads the updated URL, builds filters and sortBy, and calls searchCoachingPackages with the new page value (and the current pageSize).
Step 5: The package list re-renders the results for that page; pagination controls highlight the new current page.

Workflow: Open a package detail view
Step 1: User clicks on a package card displayed in the package list component.
Step 2: The card click handler retrieves the CoachingPackage.id for the clicked item.
Step 3: The application navigates to package_detail.html, adding a query parameter id equal to the clicked package's id (e.g., package_detail.html?id={packageId}).
Step 4: The package detail page is responsible for loading and displaying full package details using its own interfaces.

Workflow: Navigate to the cart from Services & Packages
Step 1: User clicks the cart icon in the header component while on services.html.
Step 2: The header navigation handler navigates directly to cart.html, without issuing any API calls or modifying cart data from this page.
Step 3: The cart page is responsible for retrieving and rendering cart contents using its own interfaces.
interactions:
Interaction: Topic filter selection
- Trigger: User selects a topic option from the topic filter control, whose options are populated from getPackageFilterOptions.topics.
- Behavior: The filter component updates its internal filter state, emits an onFilterChange event with the selected topic value, and the URL state component updates the topic query parameter. The page then re-invokes searchCoachingPackages with the updated filters.

Interaction: Sessions count filter selection
- Trigger: User selects a sessions count option from the sessions filter control, whose options are populated from getPackageFilterOptions.sessionCountOptions.
- Behavior: The filter component updates its sessions filter state, emits onFilterChange with the selected sessions_count value, and the URL state component writes sessionsCount to the URL. The page re-calls searchCoachingPackages using the updated filters.

Interaction: Price range adjustment
- Trigger: User adjusts a price range control (e.g., slider or numeric inputs) bounded by getPackageFilterOptions.priceRange.minPrice and maxPrice.
- Behavior: The filter component validates that the selected range is within the allowed bounds, then emits onFilterChange with the selected maximum price (and optionally minimum price if supported). The URL state handler updates the maxPrice query parameter (and any other price-related params if implemented), and the page re-runs searchCoachingPackages with updated filters.

Interaction: Sort option change
- Trigger: User selects a sort option from a dropdown populated with getPackageFilterOptions.sortOptions.
- Behavior: The sort component emits onSortChange with the selected sortBy value; the URL state component updates the sortBy parameter and resets page to the first page. The page then re-calls searchCoachingPackages using the current filters and new sortBy.

Interaction: Pagination control use
- Trigger: User clicks next/previous page or a specific page number in the pagination component.
- Behavior: The pagination component emits onPageChange with the newly selected page index. The URL state handler updates the page parameter in the URL, and the page calls searchCoachingPackages with the same filters and sortBy but updated page value.

Interaction: Package card click
- Trigger: User clicks anywhere on an interactive region of a package card in the package list.
- Behavior: The card triggers onPackageClick with the associated CoachingPackage.id. The page navigates to package_detail.html?id={id} without calling any additional interfaces from this page.

Interaction: Clear filters
- Trigger: User clicks a "clear filters" action in the filter component.
- Behavior: The filter component emits onClearFilters, indicating that filters should be reset to their unfiltered state (as defined by the application). The URL state handler removes filter-related query parameters (topic, sessionsCount, maxPrice, and page) from the URL or resets them to defaults, then the page calls searchCoachingPackages with an empty or default filters object and current sortBy.

Interaction: Header cart icon click
- Trigger: User clicks the cart icon in the page header.
- Behavior: The header component triggers onCartClick, and the router navigates directly to cart.html without performing any API calls from this page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Services & Packages page uses URL query parameters as the single source of truth for its filter, sort, and pagination state. On every page load or URL change, the page reads the following parameters: topic, sessionsCount, maxPrice, sortBy, page, and pageSize (if present). These values are transformed into a filters object and sortBy value that are passed to searchCoachingPackages, along with page and pageSize. The UI components (filters, sort dropdown, and pagination) are initialized and updated based on these parameters, ensuring that the visible state always matches the URL. When the user interacts with filters, sorting, or pagination controls, the components do not directly call the interfaces; instead, they emit events that cause the URL parameters to be updated. Once the URL is updated, the page recalculates the filters and sortBy from the URL and calls searchCoachingPackages again. The getPackageFilterOptions interface is called on initial load (and optionally on refresh) to populate available topics, session counts, price range bounds, and sort options, which define what filter values are valid. No persistent client-side storage (such as localStorage) is used to store state on this page; all sharable state is encoded in the URL so that the page can be bookmarked or shared in a pre-filtered configuration.

components.functionality: Provides page-level navigation including a link to the Services & Packages page, links to other site sections, and a cart icon that navigates to cart.html.

Reads URL query parameters on load and on browser navigation events, converts them into a filters object, sortBy value, page index, and pageSize, and exposes this state to other components. When filters, sort, or pagination change via UI events, this component updates the URL parameters accordingly and triggers new data fetches using searchCoachingPackages.

Displays filter controls for topic, sessions count, and price range, plus a clear-filters action. It receives dynamic options and numeric bounds from getPackageFilterOptions, initializes its selected values based on current URL-derived state, and emits events when the user changes any filter.

Provides a sort dropdown populated with sortOptions from getPackageFilterOptions. It initializes its selected option based on the sortBy URL parameter and emits a sort-change event when the user selects a different sort option.

Displays a summary of the current search results, such as the total number of packages matching the filters and which page of results is currently visible, using totalCount and the current page and pageSize derived from searchCoachingPackages and the URL.

Renders a list or grid of coaching packages returned by searchCoachingPackages, displaying key fields from each CoachingPackage. Each package entry is clickable and triggers navigation to the package detail page using the package ID.

Displays pagination controls based on totalCount from searchCoachingPackages and the current page and pageSize from URL state. When the user changes the page, it emits a page-change event that updates the page query parameter and triggers a refreshed call to searchCoachingPackages.

Calls getPackageFilterOptions on initial page load (and optionally on demand) to retrieve topics, sessionCountOptions, priceRange, and sortOptions, then passes this configuration data to the filter and sort components.

Uses the current filters, sortBy, page, and pageSize from the URL-state manager to call searchCoachingPackages. It exposes the returned results array and totalCount to the package-list, package-results-summary, and pagination-controls components.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow. A full-width, fixed header sits at the very top. Below it, a hero-like 'Services & Packages' band spans the full width within a centered content container, using a two-column layout: on the left, the package-results-summary functions as the hero text block (category pill, H1-style title, and short description/summary); on the right, a large supporting hero image (not a separate component) visually balances the layout. Beneath this hero band, the package-filters-bar and sort-control share a single horizontal toolbar row, with filters left and sort right. Below that, the package-list fills the main content area, followed by pagination-controls centered at the bottom. Invisible infrastructure components (url-state-manager, filter-options-loader, package-search-loader) either occupy no space or temporarily overlay the visible components they support.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear, top-to-bottom flow. A full-width, fixed header sits at the very top. Below it, a hero-like 'Services & Packages' band spans the full width within a centered content container, using a two-column layout: on the left, the package-results-summary functions as the hero text block (category pill, H1-style title, and short description/summary); on the right, a large supporting hero image (not a separate component) visually balances the layout. Beneath this hero band, the package-filters-bar and sort-control share a single horizontal toolbar row, with filters left and sort right. Below that, the package-list fills the main content area, followed by pagination-controls centered at the bottom. Invisible infrastructure components (url-state-manager, filter-options-loader, package-search-loader) either occupy no space or temporarily overlay the visible components they support.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header-navigation occupies a slim horizontal band at the top, roughly 10–15% of the initial viewport height. The hero-like area (dominated by the package-results-summary text on the left and a hero image on the right) takes up most of the above-the-fold area, about 40–50% of the viewport height, with wide left/right margins inside a max-width (~1140–1280px) container. Within this container, the left hero text column spans roughly 55–60% of the width, and the right image column spans 40–45%. The filters/sort toolbar forms a low-height band directly below the hero (about 64–80px tall). The package-list occupies the bulk of the remaining vertical space, with pagination-controls taking a narrow band at the bottom. Vertical spacing between these major bands follows a generous rhythm (32–64px), consistent with the base 8px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: All visible components are presented with low content density: large, bold typography for the page title and key labels, ample line-height, and generous padding around interactive elements. The hero area has substantial breathing room, with noticeable dark teal background visible above, below, and around the text block and image. The filters and sort controls are visually compact but not crowded, arranged in a single clear row. The package-list uses a grid with generous gutter spacing and clear card padding, ensuring each package feels distinct and scannable. Loaders appear as minimal, unobtrusive overlays or skeletons, preserving the clean, premium aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows a Z-pattern similar to the mockup. The eye starts at the logo in the top-left of the header, moves across the horizontal navigation to any primary action on the top-right, then drops diagonally down to the left side of the hero where the small category pill and large 'Services & Packages' heading sit. From there, the eye moves horizontally to the right-side hero image. After resolving the hero, attention flows downward to the filters and sort row, then into the first row of package cards. Within the toolbar, the eye starts with the filters on the left and then moves to the sort-control on the right, mirroring the primary-left, secondary-right pattern. Pagination at the bottom is centered to provide a clear, final stop to the scroll.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The page presents as a modern, SaaS-inspired services catalog set on a rich dark teal background. At the very top, a fixed, full-width header anchors the experience, with the logo on the left, concise navigation near the center-left, and primary actions on the right. Below, a spacious hero band fills most of the above-the-fold area: on the left, a small pill (e.g., 'Services') sits above a large, confident 'Services & Packages' heading and a short line summarizing how many packages are available—this is the package-results-summary acting as the hero text block. On the right, a large, high-quality lifestyle or coaching-related image occupies slightly less width than the text but aligns vertically with it, creating a balanced two-column composition. The generous margins around both columns let the dark teal background show through, reinforcing a calm, premium mood. Beneath this hero band, the content transitions into a functional toolbar: filters stretch across the left side while the sort-control aligns with the right edge of the content container, all on the same horizontal line. Below that, a grid of package cards flows down the page with clear gutters and consistent card sizes, maintaining the airy feel. At the very bottom of the list, centered pagination controls give a clear sense of progression. Non-visual components such as state management and loaders either sit behind the scenes or briefly overlay their respective regions as subtle skeletons or spinners, never disturbing the overall clarity and visual hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation forms a fixed, full-width bar pinned to the very top of the viewport, spanning 100% width with a height of roughly 64–80px (about 10–15% of the initial viewport). It uses the same dark teal as the page background but may be slightly darker or semi-elevated to distinguish it. Inside, content is constrained to the central max-width container (approximately 1140–1280px) and horizontally centered. The logo is anchored to the far left of this container, aligned vertically in the middle of the header. To the right of the logo, with 24–32px of horizontal separation, the main navigation links sit in a horizontal row, left-aligned or center-left overall. On the far right side of the container, balanced by generous empty space between nav and actions, any primary header actions (e.g., 'Book a Call') sit aligned to the right edge. Space above and below the nav items is generous (16–24px padding), ensuring the header feels slim yet airy. As the user scrolls, this header remains in place (fixed), while the rest of the content scrolls beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the top of the viewport; full-width, with a centered max-width content container. Other components scroll beneath it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is a non-visual infrastructure component and does not occupy any visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits behind the main content area, synchronizing the filter, sort, pagination, and search states with the URL. It aligns its behavior with the components below the hero—primarily the package-filters-bar, sort-control, package-list, and pagination-controls—without altering their layout. There is no dedicated visual region, padding, or margin for this component.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered, purely functional; no box in the layout, no impact on spacing. It only affects state and interactions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-filters-bar sits directly below the hero band that contains the package-results-summary and hero image. It is the first clearly functional strip after the more editorial hero. The bar is anchored to the left side of the central content container and runs nearly the full width of that container, leaving 24–32px of padding to the container edges. Vertically, it occupies a modest height of about 64–80px including internal padding. Within this band, filter controls (such as dropdowns, pill-style toggles, and checkboxes) are arranged from left to right, starting at the very left edge of the bar. Filters are grouped logically with 16–24px gaps between groups, ensuring each control has at least 8–16px of internal padding and does not touch adjacent elements. The bar visually occupies approximately the left 70% of the content container’s width, leaving visual space on the right where the sort-control aligns to the container’s right edge on the same horizontal line. There is about 32–48px of vertical space (margin) separating this bar from the hero above and 24–32px separating it from the package-list below, maintaining the spacious editorial feel. On scroll, the bar may optionally become sticky, hugging the top of the viewport just below the fixed header to keep filters easily accessible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Normally flows in the document below the hero; can be configured as sticky so it pins to the top of the viewport (just under the fixed header-navigation) once scrolled into place.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control shares a horizontal row with the package-filters-bar but anchors to the far right side of the same central content container. Within that row, it occupies roughly the right 20–30% of the width, visually balancing the denser filter controls on the left. The control itself (e.g., a label like 'Sort by' plus a dropdown) is right-aligned within this region, with 16–24px of padding between the dropdown and the container’s right edge. Vertically, it is centered within the 64–80px height of the toolbar band, matching the vertical alignment of the filters. There is clear whitespace between the last filter control on the left and the sort-control on the right, preventing crowding and reinforcing the premium minimalism. When the filters bar becomes sticky on scroll, the sort-control moves with it, maintaining its right-aligned position in the same fixed toolbar row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the filters toolbar row; participates in any sticky behavior of the filter bar, remaining right-aligned as the toolbar sticks to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-results-summary is positioned as the hero text block of the page, immediately below the fixed header and forming the left column of a two-column hero layout. It sits within the centralized content container, starting approximately 64px below the bottom edge of the header-navigation to create a generous visual buffer. Horizontally, it occupies about 55–60% of the container width, anchored to the left. At the top of this block, a small pill element (e.g., 'Services') appears, left-aligned with the main heading, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside the pill. Below this pill, separated by about 16px of vertical space, a large, multi-line, H1-level heading (e.g., 'Services & Packages') spans the width of this left column with generous line-height and a bold style that stands out against the dark teal background. Beneath the heading, after another 16–24px gap, a brief line or two of summary text communicates how many packages are available and/or a short descriptive phrase. This text remains left-aligned and uses a more subdued, slightly smaller type size for hierarchy. The total height of this summary block roughly aligns vertically with the midpoint or upper half of the hero image on the right, giving a balanced, midline alignment across the hero band. Below this hero text block, there is 48–64px of vertical spacing before the filters/sort toolbar begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky content at the top of the main area; it defines the left column of the hero band, with its height informally aligning to the hero image on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-list sits directly below the filters/sort toolbar, separated by about 24–32px of vertical margin to keep the layout airy. It occupies the full width of the central content container (minus standard side padding of 24–32px), aligning flush under the left edge of the package-results-summary above. The list is presented as a grid of cards: on larger screens, it uses a 12-column grid with each card spanning 4 columns (yielding a 3-column layout) with 24–32px gutters between cards horizontally and 32–48px vertical spacing between rows. On medium screens, cards may span 6 columns (2-column layout), and on small screens they stack to a single column. Each card has generous internal padding (24–32px), a subtle elevation or border, and ample white or light surface area against the dark teal surroundings, making individual packages easy to scan. The top row of cards begins approximately 24–32px below the filters toolbar. The list extends downward as far as needed, with the pagination-controls centered underneath after a 32–48px vertical gap. When the package-search-loader is active, this area is overlaid with skeleton cards or a soft dimming effect that follows the same card grid pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows naturally as the main scrollable content region; its height grows with the number of cards. It may receive an overlay from the package-search-loader during data fetching, but otherwise remains static.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are positioned directly beneath the package-list, separated from the last row of cards by about 32–48px of vertical space. They are horizontally centered within the main content container, creating a clear visual anchor at the end of the list. The controls themselves (e.g., previous/next buttons and page numbers) are laid out in a single horizontal row with 8–16px spacing between elements, and may use pill-like styling that echoes the category pill in the hero, albeit in a smaller, more understated form. There is ample whitespace above and below the controls (16–24px of internal padding, plus the aforementioned margins), so they feel light and not cramped against the edges of the viewport. Below the pagination, an additional 48–64px of bottom padding ensures the page ends softly rather than abruptly at the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the content flow; scrolls with the content and remains centered. It does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-options-loader is visually tied to the package-filters-bar and does not create an independent region. When active, it manifests either as a thin progress indicator along the top edge of the filters toolbar or as subtle skeleton placeholders in place of filter controls. In both modes, it is constrained to the height and width of the filters bar: spanning its full width or only the sub-areas where filters will appear, without overflowing into the sort-control area. It preserves the overall toolbar dimensions so that the layout does not shift during loading. Once the filter options have loaded, this loader disappears entirely, leaving the filters toolbar at the same position, size, and alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or temporarily replaces parts of the package-filters-bar without altering its outer box; appears and disappears in place, not affecting layout flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-search-loader is associated with the package-list area and appears whenever search or filter changes trigger a data refresh. Visually, it overlays the entire package-list region, matching its width (full width of the content container) and approximate height (at least enough to cover one to two rows of cards, or the full content height if known). The overlay may take the form of skeleton card shapes aligned to the same grid as the actual package cards, or a centered loading indicator within a softly dimmed version of the list area. Horizontal and vertical spacing of skeletons matches the card gutters (24–32px horizontally, 32–48px vertically), keeping the rhythm consistent. The loader does not push other components up or down; pagination-controls remain in their position beneath the list, and the filters/sort toolbar remains above. Once loading completes, the overlay fades out and reveals the updated package-list in the same location.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays the package-list region without changing its dimensions or position; appears and disappears without causing reflow, maintaining the existing scroll position and layout.

name: Package Detail
title: Package Detail
description: Displays full information for a selected coaching package, allows the user to add it to their cart, and shows related packages.

core_features: Read the package ID from the URL parameter `id` and load full package details using `getCoachingPackageDetail`.
Display the selected coaching package’s key information (name, topic, description, number of sessions, price, activity status).
Display a list of related coaching packages returned by `getCoachingPackageDetail` and allow navigation to their detail pages via URL parameter changes.
Provide an add-to-cart form that uses `addPackageToCart` with the current package ID and a user-selected quantity.
Show feedback after adding a package to the cart, including the success flag, message, and cart summary as returned by `addPackageToCart`.
Provide navigation links to go back to the list of all packages (services.html) and to navigate to the cart page (cart.html) after adding items.
user_workflows:
View a package and its details:
1. User navigates to `package_detail.html?id=<packageId>` from `services.html` or `index.html`.
2. On page load, the page reads the `id` URL parameter.
3. If `id` is present, the page triggers `getCoachingPackageDetail` with `packageId` = `id`.
4. While loading, a loading indicator is shown.
5. On success, the page displays the package’s details and a list of related packages.
6. If the package is not active, the page shows a non-purchasable state and a message that it is unavailable.

Add a package to the cart:
1. While viewing a package detail, the user adjusts the quantity in the add-to-cart form.
2. The user clicks the add-to-cart button.
3. The page triggers `addPackageToCart` with `packageId` = current `id` and the selected `quantity`.
4. While the request is in progress, the submit button is disabled or shows a loading state.
5. On success (`success = true`), the page updates in-memory cart state with the returned `cart` and `items`, displays the success message from the response (or a default when absent), and updates the URL query to include `status=added` without reloading the page.
6. A “View Cart” action is shown, which navigates to `cart.html` when clicked.
7. On failure (`success = false`), the page shows the error message from the response and does not navigate away.

Navigate to a related package:
1. The user scrolls to the related packages section.
2. The user clicks a related package.
3. The browser navigates to `package_detail.html?id=<relatedPackageId>` using a standard link.
4. The page reloads (or reruns the data loader), triggering `getCoachingPackageDetail` with the new `id`.
5. The new package’s details and its set of related packages are displayed.

Return to all packages:
1. From the package detail page, the user clicks the “Back to all packages” navigation element.
2. The browser navigates to `services.html` without additional parameters.

Go to cart after adding a package:
1. After a successful add-to-cart operation, the feedback area displays a “View Cart” action.
2. The user clicks “View Cart”.
3. The browser navigates to `cart.html` (no additional parameters).
interactions: On page load, read the `id` URL parameter. If missing, skip the API call and show an error state with a link back to `services.html`.
On page load or whenever the `id` URL parameter changes, invoke `getCoachingPackageDetail(packageId=id)`.
On successful `getCoachingPackageDetail` response, bind `package` to the main package detail components and `relatedPackages` to the related list component.
On `getCoachingPackageDetail` error (network or invalid ID), display an error panel and a navigation option to return to `services.html`.
When the user changes the quantity input, update local component state for quantity (without persisting it outside the page).
When the user submits the add-to-cart form, call `addPackageToCart(packageId=currentPackageId, quantity=currentQuantity)`.
While `addPackageToCart` is in progress, disable the add-to-cart button and prevent duplicate submissions.

On `addPackageToCart` success, update local cart-related state with the returned `cart` and `items`, show the success message, and update the URL query (e.g., set `status=added`) using the History API without reloading.
On `addPackageToCart` failure, display the returned error message (if provided) or a generic error, and keep the user on the same page with the form still available.
When the user clicks a related package in the related packages list, navigate via a standard link to `package_detail.html?id=<relatedPackageId>`.
When the user clicks “Back to all packages”, navigate via a standard link to `services.html`.
When the user clicks “View Cart” from the feedback area or navigation component, navigate via a standard link to `cart.html`.
When the user closes the feedback banner, hide it in the current in-memory session without altering URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the URL parameter `id`. On every page load, the page reads `id` from the query string (e.g., `package_detail.html?id=<packageId>`). If `id` is present, the page calls `getCoachingPackageDetail` with `packageId = id`; if not, the page shows a missing-parameter error state and does not call the interface. The page optionally reacts to an additional query parameter `status`. When `status=added`, the page displays an 'item added to cart' state; this parameter is set or updated by the page after a successful `addPackageToCart` call using History API methods so that no full reload is required. All other detailed cart information (subtotal, items) is maintained only in in-memory state based on the most recent `addPackageToCart` response; it is not persisted via URL or localStorage. When a user clicks a related package, the browser navigates to `package_detail.html?id=<relatedPackageId>`; this ID change causes the data loader component to rerun and fetch the new package details. Navigation to `services.html` and `cart.html` does not pass additional parameters from this page. No page state is stored in localStorage or other persistent client-side storage; the URL query parameters are the only persistent state mechanism.

components.functionality: Reads the `id` parameter from the URL, invokes `getCoachingPackageDetail` with that ID, manages loading and error states, and provides the resulting `package` and `relatedPackages` data to child components.

Displays the primary information for the selected coaching package, including its name, topic, session count, price, and activity status. If the package is inactive (`is_active` is false), shows a non-purchasable state and hides or disables the add-to-cart form.

Shows the full description text of the coaching package when available. If the description is absent, the component either stays hidden or shows a minimal placeholder that indicates no description is provided.

Displays auxiliary metadata for the package such as topic, number of sessions (`sessions_count`), popularity score, and created date when available. Used to give the user more context about what the package covers and how it compares in popularity.

Allows the user to select a quantity and add the current package to the cart. On submission, calls `addPackageToCart` with the current package ID and selected quantity, then passes the response to the feedback banner and cart status components.

Displays feedback after an add-to-cart attempt. For successful operations, it uses `success`, `message`, and the returned `cart` data (e.g., subtotal or item count) from `addPackageToCart` to show confirmation and a 'View Cart' option. For failures, it displays the error message. Visibility can also be influenced by the `status` URL parameter (e.g., `status=added`).

Shows a lightweight summary of the cart based only on the most recent `addPackageToCart` response (e.g., subtotal or total item count). It does not fetch data itself; it only reflects state that `add-to-cart-form` passes in after a successful call.

Displays the list of related packages returned by `getCoachingPackageDetail`. Each related package item is rendered as a clickable link to `package_detail.html?id=<relatedPackageId>` so that selecting one triggers navigation to its detail page.

Represents a single related coaching package within the related list, showing brief data such as name, topic, and price. Clicking the item navigates to the full detail page for that package using a URL with the appropriate `id` parameter.

Provides simple navigation controls: a link back to the list of packages (`services.html`) and a link to view the cart (`cart.html`). The cart link is especially emphasized after a successful add-to-cart operation.
Shows a loading state while `getCoachingPackageDetail` or `addPackageToCart` are in progress. It can be shared across the page or scoped to specific components (e.g., within the add-to-cart form).

Displays errors related to loading the package detail, including missing or invalid `id` parameters or API errors from `getCoachingPackageDetail`. Provides a clear message and a link back to `services.html`. It can also be reused for generic add-to-cart errors if a consolidated error area is desired.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: Below the global fixed header (logo left, nav center-left, primary CTA right), the page uses a hero-style package header area that mirrors the mockup’s two-column split: the left column contains the package-main-info (category pill, title, short summary) with the package-navigation-links sitting subtly above or near it; the right column contains a compact purchasing stack with cart-status-summary on top and the add-to-cart-form directly beneath it. Immediately below this hero band, a secondary content section presents the package-description-section on the left and the package-meta-info on the right as a 2/3–1/3 split. Further down, a full-width but centered related-packages-list section shows related-package-item cards in a grid. The add-to-cart-feedback-banner sits just beneath the global header, spanning the width as a thin, prominent status band. Non-visual data-loader logic governs whether the loading-indicator or error-state-panel temporarily replace the main content region when necessary.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Below the global fixed header (logo left, nav center-left, primary CTA right), the page uses a hero-style package header area that mirrors the mockup’s two-column split: the left column contains the package-main-info (category pill, title, short summary) with the package-navigation-links sitting subtly above or near it; the right column contains a compact purchasing stack with cart-status-summary on top and the add-to-cart-form directly beneath it. Immediately below this hero band, a secondary content section presents the package-description-section on the left and the package-meta-info on the right as a 2/3–1/3 split. Further down, a full-width but centered related-packages-list section shows related-package-item cards in a grid. The add-to-cart-feedback-banner sits just beneath the global header, spanning the width as a thin, prominent status band. Non-visual data-loader logic governs whether the loading-indicator or error-state-panel temporarily replace the main content region when necessary.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero band occupies most of the above-the-fold area, using a centered max-width container (~1140–1280px). Within this, roughly 55–60% of the width (about 7 of 12 grid columns) is allocated to the text-heavy package-main-info on the left, and 40–45% (about 5 of 12 columns) to the cart-status-summary and add-to-cart-form column on the right. The header occupies 10–15% of the viewport height; the hero fills the rest of the initial view. Vertical spacing between the hero, the description/meta section, and the related-packages-list follows a generous rhythm (typically 64px or more). Each section’s content is inset from the viewport edges with wide lateral margins, creating visible dark teal background bands around the primary content blocks.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is intentionally low throughout. The package title is large and multi-line, with ample line-height and space above and below. Category pills, navigation links, and status banners are compact but surrounded by generous padding so they don’t feel crowded. The add-to-cart-form and cart-status-summary use card-like blocks with 24–32px inner padding. The description text column uses comfortable line length and line spacing, with clearly separated paragraphs and subheadings. The related-package-item cards are laid out in a sparse grid with noticeable gaps between cards and roomy padding inside each card, echoing a premium SaaS editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows the mockup’s F/Z pattern. The user’s eye starts at the global header’s logo on the top-left, moves across the top navigation and action CTA, then drops diagonally down to the left side of the hero where the package-navigation-links and category pill sit above the large package title. From this strong text anchor, the eye moves horizontally to the right-side purchasing column (cart-status-summary and add-to-cart-form). When the user scrolls, the flow continues down the left through the package-description-section, then across to the package-meta-info on the right. Finally, attention moves down to the related-packages-list grid. The add-to-cart-feedback-banner, when visible, temporarily intercepts the flow by appearing as a slim, high-contrast strip just under the header, then the eye returns to the main F-pattern in the hero.

layout.overall_description: The page feels like a premium SaaS product detail view dressed in a life-coaching brand: a dark teal backdrop fills the viewport behind a centered content column. At the top, the fixed header sets the frame. Just beneath it, a slim add-to-cart-feedback-banner occasionally appears, spanning the width with a soft highlight, then the main hero band begins. Within this hero, the left side dominates with a small pill-like label indicating the package type, followed by a bold, multi-line package name and a concise summary, all left-aligned and breathing within generous margins. To the right, a shorter but substantial card stack holds the cart-status-summary and add-to-cart-form, visually behaving like the hero image block in the original mockup. Below, the background continues into a quieter content area where a wide description column on the left and a narrower meta-information column on the right mirror the grid used above. Farther down, the hero transitions into a slightly lighter or subtly differentiated background band for related packages: a centered heading and a row of airy cards that showcase other coaching packages. When data is loading, the main hero and subsequent sections are temporarily replaced by a centered loading-indicator; on error, an error-state-panel takes their place. The entire composition is spacious, left-aligned within a stable center column, with clear horizontal bands and a consistent two-column rhythm that carries the user from headline, to purchase, to details, to exploration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data-loader is a non-visual wrapper that conceptually surrounds all main content sections (hero, description/meta, related packages) within the centered content area below the global header. It does not render any visual chrome itself but defines the primary content region starting approximately 64px below the header and extending down to the bottom of the page. Within this region, it conditionally shows either the loading-indicator, the error-state-panel, or the normal content stack (hero + subsequent sections). In practice, the data-loader occupies the full width of the centered content container (about 80–90% of the viewport width up to the 1140–1280px max) and the full available height beneath the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual structural wrapper that follows normal document flow; controls whether child components or loading/error overlays are visible but adds no padding or fixed positioning itself.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-main-info sits in the hero section as the dominant left column, aligned with the start of the centered grid. It begins roughly 64px below the bottom edge of the global header (or below the feedback banner if present), with an additional 32–48px of internal top padding before its content. It spans approximately 55–60% of the hero’s width, corresponding to about 7 of the 12 grid columns, and extends vertically enough that its midsection aligns roughly with the vertical middle of the right-hand purchasing column. At the very top-left inside this block, the package-navigation-links appear (breadcrumbs or section links), followed by a small vertical gap, then a pill-shaped label indicating the category (e.g., “Package” or “Coaching”). Directly beneath, a large, multi-line heading for the package stretches 2–3 lines, in white or near-white against the dark teal, with generous line-height and around 16–24px of space before a brief one or two-line subheading or summary. All text is left-aligned, with substantial horizontal padding (24–32px) between the text and the left/right edges of this column, leaving visible teal margins around the text block and a clear vertical gutter between this column and the right-hand purchasing column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Follows normal document flow within the hero section, anchoring to the left side of the centered grid; on narrower screens it converts to full-width and stacks above the right column contents.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-description-section is placed directly below the hero band, separated by a generous vertical gap of around 64px from the bottom of the package-main-info / purchasing stack. Within the centered content container, it occupies the left 2/3 of the width (approximately 8 of 12 columns), starting at the same left alignment as the hero’s left column, creating a consistent vertical alignment for headings and body text. The description content is set as a long-form text block with a comfortable line length, leaving ample right margin before the meta info column. Internal spacing includes 24–32px of padding or margin above its main heading and between major text sections, maintaining a calm editorial feel. On mobile, this section becomes full-width and appears above the package-meta-info, preserving the spacious spacing between paragraphs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Follows normal document flow, forming the primary content column in the mid-page section; transitions to full-width stacked behavior on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-meta-info appears in the same horizontal band as the package-description-section but anchored to the right side of the centered container. It occupies roughly the rightmost 1/3 of the width (about 4 of 12 grid columns), aligning its top edge with the top of the description’s heading so both columns start at the same horizontal baseline. It behaves visually as a secondary column: a vertical stack of short labeled fields (e.g., duration, session count, format) presented in a compact, card-like block with 24px padding on all sides, and a subtle separation (e.g., a soft border or lighter teal background) from the main page background. There is a generous gutter of 32–40px between this column and the description column to maintain the low-density feel. On narrower screens, this panel drops beneath the description and becomes full-width with increased top margin (32–48px) to clearly separate it as a distinct but related block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Follows normal document flow, occupying the right column in a two-column layout; stacks below the description as a full-width block on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-form sits in the hero’s right column, directly beneath the cart-status-summary, forming a cohesive purchasing stack. Together with the summary, this column spans about 40–45% of the hero width (approximately 5 of 12 columns) and is right-aligned within the centered container, leaving a wide gutter between it and the package-main-info. The form itself is contained within a card-like panel that shares the same left and right alignment as the cart-status-summary above, with 24–32px internal padding. Form controls (e.g., quantity selector, session options, start date) are laid out in a vertical stack with clear 16–24px spacing between them. The primary “Add to cart” button sits at the bottom of this panel, aligned left within the form or stretched full width, with 24px of space above it to separate it from the last input.
