The total height of the purchasing column is designed so that its midpoint roughly aligns with the middle of the package-main-info text block, echoing the alignment of the hero image in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Follows normal document flow within the right column of the hero; on wide screens it may participate in a sticky purchasing stack via the cart-status-summary, while on smaller screens it stacks beneath the main info and expands to full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-feedback-banner appears as a slim, full-width band immediately below the global header and above the hero section. It spans 100% of the viewport width but keeps its text content aligned to the same centered content container as the rest of the page (max 1140–1280px) so the message aligns vertically with the package title below. The banner has a height of roughly 48–64px, with 12–16px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding within the text area, creating a calm but noticeable strip of color distinct from the dark teal background (e.g., a softer teal or success tone). It pushes the hero content downward while visible, maintaining the overall spaciousness. The message text is left-aligned, with an optional close icon aligned to the right within the same centered container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as a temporary bar anchored below the fixed header; behaves like a standard block element that pushes content down, but visually reads as a transient overlay. On scroll it moves with the page rather than remaining fixed once it has scrolled out of view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-status-summary forms the top of the hero’s right column and acts as the focal point for purchase context, analogous to the hero image block in the original design. It is a compact card placed at the same vertical level as the category pill and large heading in the package-main-info, so that when you look across the hero, the left text and right purchase area visually align in height. The card spans the full width of the right column (about 40–45% of the hero width, 5 of 12 columns) with 24–32px of internal padding. Inside, it displays key info like price, billing details, or current cart status in a vertical layout with 12–16px spacing between lines. The bottom edge of this card sits 16–24px above the add-to-cart-form card, creating a small but clear separation while keeping them visually grouped. The right edge of the card is aligned with the right edge of the centered page content, leaving a comfortable margin to the viewport edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop and larger tablets, the summary (and its paired add-to-cart-form) can be configured as a sticky column that remains in view as the user scrolls through the description, constrained within the vertical bounds of the main detail section. On smaller viewports, it behaves as a normal block stacked above the add-to-cart-form and below the main info.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-packages-list occupies its own section near the bottom of the page, following a sizeable vertical gap (64–80px) after the description/meta section. It spans the full width of the page background but keeps its content within the standard centered container. At the top of this section, a left-aligned heading (e.g., “You might also like”) sits with 16–24px of space below it. Beneath, related-package-item cards are arranged in a horizontal grid: on large screens, 3 items per row, each taking roughly 30% of the container width with 4–5% horizontal gaps; on medium screens, 2 items per row; on small screens, a single column. Vertical spacing between rows is 32–48px. The section maintains generous top and bottom padding (48–64px) so the grid feels airy and distinct from the content above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Follows normal document flow as a distinct bottom section; the internal grid is responsive, reflowing the related-package-item cards based on viewport width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each related-package-item is presented as an individual card within the related-packages-list grid. The card occupies approximately one-third of the content width on large screens (with margins between cards), and expands to half-width or full-width as the grid responds to smaller breakpoints. It has a subtle elevation or border, with 24px padding on all sides and a minimum height sufficient to include a small category label, a 1–2 line title, and a brief description or price. The text is left-aligned, with 12–16px spacing between lines, and there is at least 24px of space between the card’s content and its outer edges, maintaining the low-density aesthetic. The card’s width and spacing ensure visible dark teal or section background between cards, preventing any sense of crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a responsive grid item within the related-packages-list; no special positioning beyond standard flex or grid layout behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The package-navigation-links appear at the top of the hero’s left column, just above the category pill in the package-main-info block. They are left-aligned, inline text links (such as a breadcrumb trail or section tabs) that span only as wide as their content requires, typically occupying less than one-third of the left column’s width. A small vertical gap (8–12px) separates these links from the category pill below, preserving a clear hierarchy. The links are rendered in a lighter, subtler style compared to the main title, so they guide orientation without competing visually. Their baseline aligns horizontally with the top portion of the right column’s cart-status-summary, so the user’s eye can move across the hero from navigation to purchase context in a clean line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Follows normal document flow as the very first element inside the package-main-info block; on small screens it remains at the top of the stacked content, maintaining its role as a contextual orienting element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator temporarily replaces the main content within the data-loader’s region whenever package data is being fetched. Visually, it is centered both horizontally and vertically within the viewport area beneath the fixed header, often appearing as a spinner or progress glyph with a short status label beneath it. The indicator’s container spans the full width of the centered content area, with enough height (at least 50–60% of the viewport) to give the impression of a focused, uncluttered loading state. The background remains the same dark teal or a slightly muted variant, and the rest of the page content (hero, description, related items) is hidden or visually suppressed, ensuring that attention is solely on the loading state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears as an overlay or state replacement within the main content region controlled by the data-loader; centered in the available space below the fixed header and scrolls with the page if the viewport is small.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-state-panel replaces the normal content stack when there is a data-loading or retrieval error. It is displayed within the same centered content container used by the hero, starting roughly 64px below the header. The panel itself is a prominent card, no more than 600–720px wide, horizontally centered in the page, with generous padding (32–40px) and clear typography. A concise error headline appears at the top, left-aligned, followed by a short explanatory message and an action (e.g., retry) spaced 16–24px apart. The panel is surrounded by ample empty dark teal background on all sides, emphasizing the error state while maintaining the clean, calm aesthetic. No other page components (hero, forms, related items) are visible behind it, ensuring a focused experience.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered in place of the main content within the data-loader’s region; remains centered in the content area and scrolls normally if its height exceeds the viewport.

name: Cart
title: Cart
description: Review and manage selected coaching packages before purchase, including viewing items, adjusting quantities, and reviewing the subtotal.

core_features: Load and display the current cart using getCart, including all CartItem records and their associated CoachingPackage details, along with the computed subtotal.
Allow users to adjust the quantity of each cart item and immediately reflect updated pricing by calling updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove items from the cart via removeCartItem and update the cart view and subtotal based on the API response.
Handle an empty-cart state with guidance to continue browsing packages (link to services.html).
Provide a summary section showing the current subtotal and a primary next-step action (e.g., proceed to checkout) without introducing additional APIs.
user_workflows: View cart contents: User opens cart.html (e.g., via header cart icon) -> page-init component calls getCart -> cart items and subtotal are displayed in a list and summary panel -> user can review package names, unit prices, quantities, and line totals derived from the response.

Update item quantity: User locates a cart item -> edits the quantity field or uses increment/decrement controls -> quantity form validates that the new quantity is a positive integer -> on confirmation (blur/enter/click), component calls updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId and the new quantity -> page updates items and subtotal from the API response -> URL parameter msg is optionally set to indicate success (e.g., msg=quantityUpdated) and read by the notification bar.

Remove an item: User clicks the remove control for a cart item -> confirmation (if implemented) occurs at UI level -> component calls removeCartItem with the item’s cartItemId -> page replaces the cart state with the returned cart/items/subtotal -> if the cart becomes empty, empty-state message is shown and URL parameter msg may be updated (e.g., msg=itemRemoved or msg=cartEmpty).

Handle empty cart: User opens cart.html and there are no items in the cart -> getCart returns a cart with no associated items -> item list hides and empty-state message appears -> summary section is hidden or disabled -> user clicks the 'Continue browsing packages' action -> browser navigates to services.html.

Continue shopping from a non-empty cart: User reviews cart and decides to add more packages -> user clicks 'Continue browsing packages' button or link -> navigation to services.html occurs, optionally including URL parameters such as returnTo=cart to allow that page to provide a link back to the cart.

Proceed to checkout: User reviews items and subtotal -> user clicks the 'Proceed to checkout' button in the summary panel -> component triggers a navigation event (e.g., to a checkout page determined by the application routing) without invoking additional APIs, using only the current cart state as context.
interactions: On page load, a page-init component triggers getCart and populates the cart item list and summary panel; while awaiting the response, loading states are shown and interaction with quantity/removal controls is disabled.

Editing a quantity input or using quantity stepper controls updates local UI state; when the user confirms the change (such as blurring the field or pressing an 'Update' control), updateCartItemQuantity is called, and the returned cart/items/subtotal replace the current state.
Clicking a 'Remove' button for a cart row calls removeCartItem; after a successful response, that item’s row is removed from the list, and the subtotal is updated using the returned data.
If the URL includes a highlightItem parameter, the corresponding cart item row is scrolled into view and visually marked as focused (pure UI behavior based on matching CartItem.id from getCart).

If the URL includes a msg parameter, the notification bar component reads it and displays a corresponding informational or success message; dismissing the notification updates the URL to remove the msg parameter without reloading the page.
Clicking 'Continue browsing packages' triggers navigation to services.html, optionally preserving or adding query parameters (e.g., returnTo=cart) to allow users to easily navigate back.

Clicking 'Proceed to checkout' triggers navigation using the current URL plus or minus parameters determined by the application (e.g., adding step=checkout); this interaction does not call any new API and assumes the cart state is already up to date from previous getCart/updateCartItemQuantity/removeCartItem calls.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The Cart page does not rely on localStorage; it uses URL parameters and API responses to manage state. On initial load of cart.html, the page-init component always calls getCart to retrieve the current Cart, items, and subtotal. The cart is treated as a single-user, session-managed resource, so no cartId parameter is required in the URL.

URL parameters influence only UI and navigation behavior, not the underlying cart data:
- msg: A string flag for user-facing messages (e.g., msg=quantityUpdated, msg=itemRemoved, msg=cartEmpty). The notification bar reads this parameter and renders a message accordingly. When the user dismisses the notification, the component updates the browser URL via the History API to remove the msg parameter without reloading or calling any API.
- highlightItem: If present, this parameter is expected to be a CartItem.id returned by getCart. After getCart resolves, the cart-item-list component checks for a matching CartItem and, if found, scrolls that row into view and applies a focused visual state (e.g., expanded details). This is purely front-end state derived from URL + API data.
- returnTo: Optional parameter used when navigating from services.html or other pages (e.g., returnTo=services). The cart-navigation-actions component can use this to determine secondary navigation options (for example, showing a specific 'Back' link in addition to 'Continue browsing packages'). The Cart page itself always defines a direct link to services.html for 'Continue browsing packages'; any additional handling of returnTo is implemented at the routing/navigation layer.
- view: Optional parameter to control display mode (e.g., view=compact or view=detailed). The cart-item-list and cart-summary-panel components read this flag to adjust their layout or level of detail without changing the data they display.

After any mutation via updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem, the page does not manually compute totals; it trusts the returned cart, items, and subtotal from the API and replaces its current state with that response. If desired, the components may update the URL parameters (e.g., set msg=quantityUpdated after a successful quantity update, or msg=itemRemoved after removal) using the History API to keep navigation and state in sync without additional network requests.

components.functionality: On page load, initialize the cart page by calling getCart, managing loading and error states, and distributing the returned Cart, items, and subtotal to other components through shared state or props.

Displays contextual messages at the top of the cart page based on the msg URL parameter (e.g., after quantity updates or item removal) and allows users to dismiss these messages, updating the URL to remove msg without reloading.

Renders the list of cart items returned by getCart, or shows an empty-cart state if there are no items. For each item, it passes data to cart-item-row components, supports optional highlighting via the highlightItem URL parameter, and disables controls while API operations are in flight.

Represents a single line in the cart for a specific CartItem and its associated CoachingPackage, displaying package name, descriptive details (from the CoachingPackage data), unit price, quantity, and computed line total based on unit_price and quantity from the CartItem. It embeds quantity and removal controls and reacts to highlight state.

Allows users to modify the quantity of a CartItem via an input field and/or increment/decrement buttons. It validates user input (e.g., ensuring a positive integer), then calls updateCartItemQuantity with the cartItemId and new quantity. On success, it updates the cart state using the returned cart/items/subtotal and may update the URL msg parameter to reflect success.

Provides a control for removing an item from the cart. When clicked, it optionally confirms the action at the UI level and then calls removeCartItem with the appropriate cartItemId. After a successful call, it updates the local cart state with the returned cart/items/subtotal, and may set a msg URL parameter to inform the user that an item was removed or that the cart is now empty.

Displays the current subtotal of the cart and a computed total based purely on the subtotal value returned by the API. It also hosts the primary next-step action (e.g., a 'Proceed to checkout' button). The panel listens for cart updates and re-renders whenever getCart, updateCartItemQuantity, or removeCartItem returns new subtotal data. When the cart is empty, the summary can be hidden or disabled.

Handles the case where the cart has no items. When the cart-item-list or page-init components detect that the items array from getCart is empty, this component renders a simple message and a primary action to continue browsing packages (navigating to services.html).

Provides navigation controls at the bottom or top of the cart page, including 'Continue browsing packages' (link to services.html) and the primary 'Proceed to checkout' action. It may also read the returnTo URL parameter to support conditional navigation labeling or additional back links, while ensuring the main outgoing navigation to services.html is always available.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The cart page follows the same top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global header at the very top (outside this spec), then a full-width notification bar, followed by a hero-like main cart section that behaves as a two-column layout. The left column holds the cart title and cart-item-list (the primary content), while the right column holds the cart-summary-panel (taking the role of the hero image as the compact, high-importance block). The cart-navigation-actions sit below the main two-column area as a closing band. The cart-empty-state replaces the two-column layout when there are no items, becoming the central hero content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The cart page follows the same top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: global header at the very top (outside this spec), then a full-width notification bar, followed by a hero-like main cart section that behaves as a two-column layout. The left column holds the cart title and cart-item-list (the primary content), while the right column holds the cart-summary-panel (taking the role of the hero image as the compact, high-importance block). The cart-navigation-actions sit below the main two-column area as a closing band. The cart-empty-state replaces the two-column layout when there are no items, becoming the central hero content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A centered content container (approximately 1140–1280px max-width) sits on a dark teal background band, with generous horizontal padding (24–32px) and large vertical spacing (48–64px) above and below. On desktop, the main cart content uses a 12-column-like grid with roughly 7 columns (≈60–65%) for the cart-item-list on the left and 5 columns (≈35–40%) for the cart-summary-panel on the right. The notification bar stretches full-width but its content is constrained to the same central container. Major sections (notification vs main cart vs bottom actions) are separated by at least 48–64px of vertical whitespace.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and calm. Large headings, clear type hierarchy, and 16–24px vertical spacing between rows produce an editorial, premium feel. Interactive elements (quantity form, buttons) follow the 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding guideline, and each cart-item-row sits on its own card-like block with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px spacing between items. No side clutter: only essential elements—items, quantities, subtotal, CTAs—are visible in the main band.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow preserves the F/Z pattern from the mockup. The eye starts at the logo in the global header, moves through navigation to any primary action there, then drops to the notification bar, then down-left to the cart title and first item in the cart-item-list. From there it sweeps right toward the cart-summary-panel, which serves as the key decision block (subtotal and checkout CTA). Scrolling then continues vertically down through the list of items, ending at the cart-navigation-actions at the bottom. On empty state, the eye lands directly on a centered illustration/title, with secondary text and navigation actions stacked below.

layout.overall_description: The cart page sits on a dark teal canvas that echoes the hero from the original mockup. A fixed-height global header runs across the top, with the logo on the left, navigation in the center-left, and account/primary actions on the right. Directly beneath it, a slim notification bar spans the full width, containing a concise message centered within the same max-width content container. Below this, the main cart section reads like a hero: within a generously padded central container, the left side begins with a confident, large “Your Cart” heading and immediately below it the cart-item-list stacks each item as a clean, white card. To the right, aligned with the top of the first item, a compact cart-summary-panel floats on the teal background as a white card, visually analogous to the hero image in the mockup—tight, visually prominent, and slightly narrower than the text area. The left column is slightly wider than the right, emphasizing content over chrome. Each cart-item-row arranges its details horizontally: title and details on the left, quantity and remove controls in the mid/right, and per-item price aligned to the far right, all with generous whitespace. At the bottom of the content, after the last row and below the summary’s visual field, the cart-navigation-actions form a subtle horizontal band, with secondary actions on the left and a supporting primary or secondary CTA on the right. If the cart is empty, the two-column layout collapses to a single centered empty state panel within the same container, with a large, calm headline and a clear route back to browsing. The overall effect is spacious, modern, and confident, with an editorial SaaS-like hero applied to a transactional cart experience.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component defines the overall cart page canvas. It applies the dark teal brand background across the full viewport width and height, and centers all main content within a max-width container of approximately 1140–1280px. This container is horizontally centered with left and right padding of about 24–32px to maintain breathing room on large screens. Vertically, it creates a clear separation from the global header above, introducing about 64px of top margin before the main cart section begins, and at least 64px of bottom padding before the page end. The main cart section inside this container is structured as a 12-column responsive grid: on desktop, it splits into a wider left content area (≈7/12 width) and a narrower right sidebar area (≈5/12 width), with a 24–32px gutter between them. On tablet and mobile, this grid collapses to a single column with the cart-summary-panel stacking below the cart-item-list, while maintaining generous vertical spacing between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-page wrapper that controls max-width, background, and vertical rhythm; it responds responsively but does not scroll independently or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification bar appears directly beneath the global header, spanning the full width of the viewport as a horizontal band. Within this band, its content is centered inside the same max-width container as the rest of the page, aligning visually with the cart content below. The bar’s height is relatively compact—around 48–56px—with 12–16px of vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding. The message text is left-aligned within the container, starting near the left edge, with sufficient whitespace on the right so it feels airy rather than cramped. There is at least 24–32px of vertical spacing between the bottom of this bar and the start of the main cart content, preserving the layered, editorial feel from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width band anchored below the header; scrolls away with the page (not sticky).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list occupies the left column of the main cart section. It spans roughly 60–65% of the content container width on desktop, starting flush with the left grid edge and extending to the central gutter between columns. At the very top of this column, a large page heading (e.g., “Your Cart”) is placed, using bold, multi-line typography similar in scale to the hero title in the mockup; it sits about 16–24px above the first cart-item-row. Below the heading, each cart-item-row is stacked vertically, separated by 16–24px of vertical space to maintain a low-density, card-like rhythm. The list itself can extend below the fold, with the right column’s summary panel aligning to the top of this list. On smaller screens, the list expands to nearly 100% width of the container, with cart-item-rows remaining full-width blocks and the heading centered or left-aligned at the top with consistent spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the left grid column; its height grows with the number of items and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-row is presented as a horizontal, card-like block within the cart-item-list, sitting on a white or very light surface against the dark teal background. The row spans the full width of the left column, with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides and slightly rounded corners to echo the polished SaaS aesthetic. Inside the row, content is divided into two main horizontal zones: a left details section and a right controls/pricing section. The left section occupies roughly 60–70% of the row width and is aligned to the left; it contains the package name in a medium-large font at the top, followed by one or two lines of secondary details in smaller, softer text with 4–8px of spacing between lines. The right section takes the remaining 30–40% of the width and aligns content to the right edge. At the top-right, the per-item price is right-aligned, bold, and spaced about 4–8px above the cart-quantity-form. Directly beneath or just to the left of the price, the cart-quantity-form is positioned, aligned to the right or slightly inset to create a tight cluster. The cart-remove-item-button is placed either immediately below the quantity form or as a small link/button aligned to the top-right under the price, with 4–8px of vertical separation. The vertical center of the row is kept relatively spacious, with 12–16px between the main text block and the control block, so the card doesn’t feel dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width row within the list; maintains its two-zone internal layout on desktop and collapses to a vertical stack (details above controls/price) on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-quantity-form sits within the right control area of each cart-item-row. On desktop, it is horizontally compact and aligned to the right edge of the row, typically just below the item price or right next to it, forming a tight grouping. It may consist of a minus button, a numeric input, and a plus button aligned on a single line. The form’s total width is modest—around 25–35% of the row width at most—so it doesn’t overshadow the title, and its height follows the component padding guideline: about 8–10px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding inside each control. There is 4–8px of vertical spacing between the price label above and the quantity form below, ensuring visual separation while still making them feel connected. On mobile, the quantity form shifts to a full-width or nearly full-width row beneath the item title, centered or left-aligned, with 8–12px of vertical margin above and below so it doesn’t feel cramped against other elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline/inline-block element within the row’s right section; reflows under the text block on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-remove-item-button is a subtle, tertiary action placed within each cart-item-row’s right side, visually tied to the quantity and price controls. On desktop, it appears as a small text-style button or ghost button, right-aligned and positioned either directly below the cart-quantity-form or in a small gap beneath the price, with 4–8px of vertical separation so it doesn’t compete with the main controls. Its size is compact, with 6–8px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding, giving it enough click area without drawing excessive attention. The label is set in a small, calm type size in a muted color, differentiating it from primary actions. On mobile, this button can sit on a separate line below the quantity form, left- or center-aligned with 8–12px of top margin, maintaining clarity and touch-friendly spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control within the cart-item-row, always visually adjacent to the quantity/price cluster; reflows beneath them on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-panel occupies the right column of the main cart section, visually analogous to the hero image in the original mockup. It sits on a white or light card that contrasts against the dark teal background, aligned to the right edge of the central content container. The panel’s width is about 35–40% of the main container on desktop, with 24–32px internal padding and slightly rounded corners. Its top edge aligns roughly with the top of the cart-item-list heading, so when the page loads, the user sees the heading and the summary side by side, forming a strong left-right pairing. Inside the panel, summary elements (subtotal, any promo info, and the main checkout button) are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing between rows. The primary checkout button spans most of the panel’s width, with generous padding and stands out as the main CTA in this area. Below it, smaller informational rows (like tax notes) use lighter typography and smaller sizes. There is at least 24px of space between the panel’s bottom edge and the next major section below, so it feels like a distinct block rather than crowded into the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, behaves as a sidebar that can be made sticky relative to the viewport top (e.g., sticking after the header) so that as the user scrolls through a long list of items, the summary remains visible. On tablet/mobile, it loses stickiness and stacks below the cart-item-list as a regular block.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is empty, the cart-empty-state replaces the two-column layout within the main content container. It is centered both horizontally and, as much as feasible, vertically within the available viewport below the header, creating a hero-like centerpiece. The empty-state content is contained within a narrower column—around 50–60% of the main container width—so it feels focused and intentional. At the top of this block, a simple icon or illustration can sit with 16–24px of space below it. Below that, a large, confident heading (e.g., “Your cart is empty”) is center-aligned with generous line spacing, followed by one or two lines of lighter, explanatory copy with 8–12px spacing. At the bottom of this stack, the primary navigation action (e.g., “Browse coaching packages”) is centered and uses a full or medium-width button with ample padding. The surrounding dark teal background remains visible on both sides, maintaining the expansive, calm feel of the original hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shown only when there are no cart items; it is a static, centered block within the main container that scrolls normally if content height exceeds the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-navigation-actions component appears below the main cart section, after the last cart-item-row and visually just beneath the vertical extent of the cart-summary-panel. It spans the full width of the content container but remains within the same horizontal bounds as the two-column layout. Vertically, it is separated from the above content by about 32–48px of whitespace, creating a distinct closing band. Inside this band, actions are laid out in a horizontal, two-sided arrangement on desktop: a secondary action like “Continue browsing” or “Back to packages” is left-aligned, appearing as a low-emphasis text link or ghost button, while any additional supporting action (e.g., a secondary checkout or “Update cart”) is right-aligned, potentially rendered as a secondary button. The band’s height is relatively modest—around 64–80px including padding—with 16–24px of vertical padding to feel comfortable. On mobile, these actions stack vertically centered, each with 8–12px of vertical separation and full or near-full width, maintaining touch-friendly spacing and preserving the calm, spacious aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored beneath the main cart content; scrolls with the page and, on long carts, serves as the natural endpoint of the visual flow.

name: Blog
title: Blog
description: Browse and search blog articles with keyword search, filters, sorting, and pagination, and open individual article pages.

core_features: Keyword search across blog articles using the search interface.
Filter articles by topic, audience, date range, and minimum read time using options from the filter options interface.
Sort articles using sort options provided by the filter options interface.
Display a paginated list of blog articles showing key metadata such as title, excerpt, publish date, and estimated read time.
Navigate to an article detail page when an article title is clicked.
Synchronize search, filters, sorting, and pagination with URL parameters so the current state can be shared and restored.
user_workflows:
Landing with pre-applied filters from another page:
1. User arrives on blog.html with optional URL parameters (query, topic, audience, dateRange, minReadTime, sortBy, and optionally page).
2. Page controller parses URL parameters and initializes internal state.
3. Page calls getBlogFilterOptions with no parameters.
4. When getBlogFilterOptions resolves, the filter UI is populated and the currently selected values are set based on the URL parameters (if any).
5. Page calls searchBlogArticles with the parsed query, filters, sortBy, and page-related values.
6. The article list, results summary, applied filters bar, and pagination controls render based on the searchBlogArticles response.
7. User sees results already tailored to the incoming parameters and can refine them further.

Refining search via keyword input:
1. User types a keyword into the search input within the search form component.
2. On submit (or on an explicit search action), the search-form component updates the URL parameter 'query' and resets the page parameter to the first page.
3. Page controller detects the URL change and calls searchBlogArticles with the new query while preserving currently selected filters and sortBy.
4. The article list is refreshed with results matching the updated keyword search.

Applying or changing filters (topic, audience, date range, minimum read time):
1. User selects or changes one or more filter controls in the filter panel component (e.g., chooses a topic or adjusts minimum read time).
2. Filter panel updates the relevant URL parameters (topic, audience, dateRange, minReadTime) and resets page to the first page.
3. Page controller observes the updated URL and constructs a filters object compatible with searchBlogArticles.
4. Page calls searchBlogArticles with the current query, updated filters, existing sortBy, and page = first page.
5. Article list, results summary, applied filters bar, and pagination reflect the new filter combination.

Changing sort order:
1. User selects a sort option in the sort control component (e.g., one of the sortOptions returned by getBlogFilterOptions).
2. Sort control updates the 'sortBy' URL parameter and resets page to the first page.
3. Page controller calls searchBlogArticles with the current query, existing filters, and new sortBy.
4. Article list is re-rendered according to the new sort order.

Clearing individual filters or all filters:
1. User clicks on a control in the applied-filters bar to remove a specific filter or to clear all filters.
2. Applied-filters bar removes the corresponding URL parameter(s) and resets the page parameter.
3. Page controller re-runs searchBlogArticles with updated parameters (possibly leaving only query or none).
4. Filter panel and search form reflect the cleared state (e.g., inputs reset to neutral values) based on the updated URL and getBlogFilterOptions data.

Paginating through article results:
1. User clicks a pagination control (next, previous, or specific page number) in the pagination component.
2. Pagination component updates the 'page' parameter in the URL, leaving query, filters, and sortBy unchanged.
3. Page controller calls searchBlogArticles with the new page value and the current query, filters, and sortBy.
4. Article list updates to show the results for the requested page; pagination control highlights the active page.

Opening an article detail page:
1. User clicks on an article title or designated clickable area in the article list component.
2. Article list component reads the corresponding BlogArticle.id value and initiates navigation to article_detail.html with URL parameter id set to that articleId.
3. Browser navigates to article_detail.html?id={articleId}, where the detail page is responsible for loading and showing the full article content.
interactions:
Search input interaction:
- Component: blog-search-form
- Interaction: User types into the text input and submits the form.
- Behavior: onSubmit, the component reads the entered keyword and updates the URL parameter 'query'. It also clears or resets the 'page' parameter to start at the first page, then triggers a new search via the page controller.

Filter selection interaction:
- Component: blog-filter-panel
- Interaction: User selects or deselects options for topic, audience, date range, and minimum read time.
- Behavior: onChange, the filter panel updates the corresponding URL parameters (topic, audience, dateRange, minReadTime). It resets the 'page' URL parameter, then requests the page controller to re-run searchBlogArticles with the updated filter state.

Sort option interaction:
- Component: blog-sort-control
- Interaction: User chooses a sort option from a control based on sortOptions from getBlogFilterOptions.
- Behavior: onChange, the component sets the 'sortBy' URL parameter and resets 'page'. The page controller then invokes searchBlogArticles with the new sortBy value.

Applied filters bar interaction:
- Component: blog-applied-filters-bar
- Interaction: User clicks to remove a specific active filter or to clear all filters.
- Behavior: onClick for a specific filter, the component removes that filter's URL parameter (e.g., topic, audience, dateRange, or minReadTime). On a clear-all action, it removes all filter-related parameters and optionally the query parameter unless specified otherwise. After updating the URL, it resets 'page' and triggers searchBlogArticles.

Pagination interaction:
- Component: blog-pagination
- Interaction: User clicks to go to the next/previous page or a specific page index.
- Behavior: onClick, the component updates the 'page' URL parameter to the selected page index. It leaves query, filters, and sortBy unchanged. Page controller then calls searchBlogArticles with the updated page value.

Article selection interaction:
- Component: blog-article-list
- Interaction: User clicks on an article title (or whole article item if defined).
- Behavior: onClick, the article list component reads the id of the clicked BlogArticle and navigates to article_detail.html?id={id}.

URL-driven state sync interaction:
- Component: blog-state-controller (page initializer/controller)
- Interaction: Browser loads blog.html or the URL changes (e.g., via history API).
- Behavior: onLoad and onURLChange, controller parses current URL parameters (query, topic, audience, dateRange, minReadTime, sortBy, page). It initializes internal state accordingly, calls getBlogFilterOptions, and once filter options are available, calls searchBlogArticles with a filters object assembled from these parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully derived from and synchronized with URL parameters, without relying on localStorage.

1. Initial state derivation:
- On page load, the controller reads URL parameters: query, topic, audience, dateRange, minReadTime, sortBy, and optionally page. If some parameters are absent, the controller treats them as unset (no filter applied), rather than assigning fixed hardcoded values.
- The controller calls getBlogFilterOptions first. Once filter options are loaded, the controller checks whether the current URL parameter values correspond to valid options (for parameters that map to options, such as topic, audience, dateRange, and sortBy). If a parameter's value does not match any available option, the controller may ignore that parameter when building the filters object but does not modify the URL directly; any URL modification should be driven by explicit user actions or clearly defined rules.

2. Filters object construction for searchBlogArticles:
- The controller constructs the 'filters' argument for searchBlogArticles using the available URL parameters. For example, it includes topic, audience, dateRange, and minReadTime values if those parameters are present.
- If 'minReadTime' exists in the URL, it is interpreted as a numeric value and passed as part of filters. No fixed thresholds are assumed; the value is passed through as provided.
- The 'query' parameter is passed directly to the 'query' argument of searchBlogArticles if present. If not present, an empty or null-equivalent value is used to indicate no keyword filtering.
- The 'sortBy' parameter is passed directly as the sortBy argument if present and valid according to sortOptions. If absent, the controller may pass a null-equivalent value to indicate default backend sorting behavior.
- The 'page' parameter, if present, is parsed and passed to searchBlogArticles. If 'page' is absent or not usable, the controller uses a first-page value that is consistent with the pagination strategy and does not rely on hardcoded data-driven assumptions.
- The 'pageSize' argument for searchBlogArticles is determined by application-wide configuration or runtime configuration outside the scope of this page definition. This page only passes through whatever pageSize value is determined by that configuration or retrieved from the environment and does not embed a fixed numeric constant.

3. URL updates from user actions:
- Whenever the user changes query, filters, sortBy, or page through the UI, the respective components update the URL parameters using the browser's navigation or history API.
- After a URL update, the controller re-parses the URL and computes the new state, then re-runs searchBlogArticles. This ensures that the rendered state always matches the URL.
- Pagination actions update only the 'page' parameter; query, filters, and sortBy remain unchanged, preserving the current search context.

4. Restoring state from URL (deep linking and back/forward navigation):
- If the user saves or shares a blog.html URL containing parameters, visiting that URL reconstructs the same search state by repeating the initial logic.
- When users use browser back/forward navigation, the controller listens for URL changes, re-parses parameters, and re-runs searchBlogArticles to restore the previous search, filter, sort, and page state.

5. Handling inconsistent or missing parameters:
- If any URL parameter is missing, the corresponding filter or setting simply remains unset and is not applied in filters.
- If any parameter is malformed or not accepted by current filter options (for example, a topic not present in topicTags), the controller omits that value from the filters object. UI components reflect the stored state based on valid parameters only.

Overall, the page logic ensures that all visible search/filter/sort/pagination state is always a function of the current URL parameters and the data returned from getBlogFilterOptions and searchBlogArticles, without using localStorage or hardcoded data values.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, URL parameter parsing, interface calls, and propagation of search and filter state to child components.
Provides a text input for keyword-based article search and triggers searches based on the current query term.
Displays and manages filter controls for topics, audiences, date ranges, and minimum read time based on options returned from getBlogFilterOptions.
Allows users to select the current sorting for article results using sortOptions from getBlogFilterOptions.
Summarizes which filters and query are currently active and provides controls to remove individual filters or clear them all.
Displays a summary of the current result set, including total count and, optionally, the range of items shown on the current page.

Renders the list of articles returned by searchBlogArticles, showing at minimum the article title, excerpt, publish date, and read time, and provides navigation to article_detail.html when an article is selected.
Lets the user navigate between pages of results by adjusting the page parameter used in searchBlogArticles.
Provides loading and error feedback for both getBlogFilterOptions and searchBlogArticles calls, and can expose a retry mechanism.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page maintains the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width fixed header (logo left, nav mid-left, primary CTA right), followed by a full-width hero band for the Blog. Inside the hero, content is centered within a max-width container (~1140–1280px) and uses a two-column split: left column holds the blog category pill ('Blog'), page title, a short description, the blog-state-controller, and the blog-search-form; the right column holds a single large hero image that aligns vertically with the title area. Below the hero, the main content area is another centered container that splits into a left filter sidebar (blog-filter-panel) and a right content column holding the results header row (blog-results-summary + blog-sort-control), the blog-applied-filters-bar, the blog-article-list, and the blog-pagination. The blog-loading-error-panel appears contextually within the main content column, overlaying or replacing the article list when active.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page maintains the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width fixed header (logo left, nav mid-left, primary CTA right), followed by a full-width hero band for the Blog. Inside the hero, content is centered within a max-width container (~1140–1280px) and uses a two-column split: left column holds the blog category pill ('Blog'), page title, a short description, the blog-state-controller, and the blog-search-form; the right column holds a single large hero image that aligns vertically with the title area. Below the hero, the main content area is another centered container that splits into a left filter sidebar (blog-filter-panel) and a right content column holding the results header row (blog-results-summary + blog-sort-control), the blog-applied-filters-bar, the blog-article-list, and the blog-pagination. The blog-loading-error-panel appears contextually within the main content column, overlaying or replacing the article list when active.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header is a slim fixed band occupying roughly 10–12% of the viewport height. The hero occupies most of the above-the-fold area on desktop (~40–50% of viewport height), with about 55–60% width assigned to the left text/search column and 40–45% to the right hero image, respecting large outer margins and internal padding. Below, the main content area uses a 12-column layout: the filter sidebar takes about 3–4 columns (25–30% width) and the results column takes 8–9 columns (70–75% width). Vertical spacing between sections (hero to main content, main content to footer) follows the 64px+ pattern, and interior spacing between stacked components uses 16–32px increments, maintaining the spacious editorial feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page keeps a low content density: large headings, comfortable line heights, and generous white (dark-teal) space around every block. Each main block (hero left text, hero image, filter panel, results header, article cards, pagination) is separated by at least 32–48px of vertical space. Inputs (search, filter chips, sort dropdown) get 12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. No component feels cramped; there are clear groupings with breathing room, and only essential controls are visible at each level of the flow.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F/Z-pattern from the mockup. On arrival, the eye hits the logo at the top-left, moves across the main navigation to the header CTA on the right, then drops diagonally into the hero’s left column: the 'Blog' pill, page title, and supporting line. From there, the user notices the blog-state-controller tabs and the prominent blog-search-form below, then glances across to the hero image on the right. Scrolling down, the eye is led to the left-side blog-filter-panel, then across to the right-side results summary and sort control. The applied filters bar runs horizontally just below, guiding the eye down into the vertically stacked article cards, then finally to the centered pagination at the bottom. Any loading/error state reclaims attention by appearing centrally within the results area, temporarily overriding the usual flow.

layout.overall_description: The Blog page sits on a calm, dark teal canvas with a fixed, minimal header at the top. Just below, a spacious hero band introduces the blog: on the left, a small 'Blog' pill floats above a large, confident title and short supporting line; beneath that, a subtle row of state controls lets users toggle high-level views, and a wide, elegant search bar invites keyword discovery. To the right, a single hero image balances the composition, aligning with the vertical center of the text column and leaving generous teal margins around it. As the user scrolls, the layout resolves into a two-column main content area: a slim but tall filter sidebar on the left and a broader content column on the right. The results column opens with a tidy header row where a plain-language results summary on the left and a compact sort control on the right define the context of the list below. A horizontal applied filters bar sits just underneath, showing active chips with ample space between them. The article list flows downward as a series of roomy cards with clear separation, against the same dark teal backdrop or subtle panels, ending in a centered pagination control with plenty of breathing room. If something goes wrong or content is loading, a focused feedback panel appears in the middle of the results column, dimming or replacing the article cards so the message is unmistakable yet still consistent with the quiet, premium aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-state-controller appears inside the hero’s left column, directly under the page title and short description and above the blog-search-form. It is left-aligned with the hero heading and spans roughly 60–70% of the left column width (about 30–40% of the total page width), leaving generous padding to the right edge of the content container. Visually, it presents as a horizontal row of pill-shaped tabs or segmented controls with 8–12px internal padding and 16–24px gaps between items, maintaining plenty of air around each control. There is about 16–24px vertical space between the bottom of the description text and the top of this controller, and another 24–32px between its bottom edge and the blog-search-form. On smaller screens, it wraps to a second line if needed but stays grouped immediately above the search form, preserving a clear, stacked hierarchy in the hero area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the hero’s left column and scrolls with the page. On tablet and mobile, it remains directly above the search form, switching from a horizontal row to a wrapped or scrollable chip row as needed but always occupying the upper part of the content stack in the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-search-form is a prominent horizontal search bar located near the bottom of the hero’s left column, beneath the blog-state-controller. It is left-aligned with the hero heading and spans roughly 70–80% of the left column width (about 40–45% of the total page width), leaving a comfortable margin of dark teal space to its right. The input field has substantial padding (12px top/bottom, 20–24px left/right), and the entire control sits within a subtle, high-contrast surface (e.g., lighter panel on the dark background) with rounded corners. There is at least 24–32px of vertical spacing above it (from the blog-state-controller) and 32–48px below it before the hero band transitions into the main content section. On smaller screens, the search form expands to nearly full width within the content container and becomes the primary control visible under the title.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the hero’s left column and scrolls with the page. On mobile, it becomes a full-width element directly under the hero heading and state controller, maintaining its prominence as the main interaction point above the fold.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-filter-panel is a vertical sidebar that appears immediately below the hero, aligned to the left side of the main content container. It occupies approximately 3–4 of the 12 grid columns (about 25–30% of the content width) and extends from just below the hero’s bottom edge down past the article list, depending on filter content length. The panel sits on a slightly elevated card surface over the dark teal background, with 24–32px internal padding on all sides and 24–32px of outer margin separating it from the hero above and the results column to its right. Filters are stacked top-to-bottom with 16–24px gaps, and the panel maintains generous breathing space at the top, possibly with a small section title aligned to the left. A clear gutter of at least 32px exists between this panel and the right-hand results column, ensuring that the layout feels open rather than cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the filter panel behaves as a sticky sidebar: once the user scrolls past the hero, it remains pinned near the top of the viewport while the article list scrolls independently, up to the extent of its content. On tablets, it may still appear as a left column but with reduced width, and the sticky behavior can be retained or limited by viewport height. On mobile, it collapses into a hidden, slide-down or off-canvas panel triggered by a ‘Filters’ button near the top of the results column, temporarily overlaying the content when opened.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-sort-control sits in the main results column below the hero and to the right of the blog-results-summary, forming a shared header row above the article list. Within the 8–9-column-wide results area, the results summary is anchored to the left, while the sort control is right-aligned on the same horizontal baseline, creating a balanced row spanning the full width of the content column. The sort control itself is compact—a dropdown or segmented control—occupying roughly 20–25% of the results column width, with enough right padding (16–24px) before the container edge. There is 24–32px of vertical space between this header row and the applied filters bar below, and a similar amount separating it from the hero above. The control is visually subtle but clearly interactive, with 8–12px internal padding and clear focus/hover states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the results header row and scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, the sort control drops under the results summary, left-aligned but still within the same header block, so the vertical reading order becomes summary first, then sort control.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-applied-filters-bar is a horizontal band positioned directly below the results header row (results summary + sort control) within the right-hand results column. It spans the full width of this column (about 70–75% of the page width on desktop) and presents selected filters as pill-shaped chips laid out from left to right, wrapping to multiple rows if necessary. The bar’s background is subtly differentiated from the main background (e.g., a slightly lighter or darker strip) with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical spacing above and below the bar, separating it from the header and from the article list. When no filters are applied, the bar either collapses entirely (leaving extra vertical space between the header and list) or shows a very minimal placeholder state aligned to the left, still respecting the airy spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and scrolls with the page. On mobile and tablet, it remains directly under the results header but may allow horizontal scrolling if many chips are present, while still wrapping where possible to avoid feeling cramped.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-results-summary appears in the main results column at the top of the content area, forming the left side of the header row where the sort control occupies the right side. It is left-aligned within the results column and occupies roughly 50–60% of that column’s width, providing enough room for a short line like 'Showing 12 of 128 articles' without crowding. The text sits on the dark teal background or a very subtle card surface, with 0–8px of internal padding above and below the text itself. There is at least 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the hero above, and it is horizontally aligned with the top edge of the blog-filter-panel to the left, visually tying the two columns together. Below it, the applied filters bar sits with a clear 24–32px gap, maintaining the spacious rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the results header and scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, it remains at the top of the results column, above the sort control and applied filters bar in the vertical reading order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-article-list is the main vertical content block in the right-hand results column, starting just below the blog-applied-filters-bar. It spans the full width of the results column (8–9 of 12 grid columns, about 70–75% of the page width on desktop) and extends downward as a stack of spacious article cards. Each card has generous internal padding (24–32px), with 24–32px vertical spacing between cards, allowing the dark teal background or subtle separators to show through and create a strong editorial feel. The list is left-aligned with the results summary and hero text, maintaining a consistent column edge. There is at least 32–48px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the last card and the pagination control below. On larger screens, cards may include a small thumbnail aligned to the right or left within each card while preserving the overall airy, single-column list structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and scrolls within the page. On tablet and mobile, the article list becomes a full-width stack below any mobile representation of the filters, with card padding slightly reduced if necessary but still honoring the spacious design system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-pagination control is anchored at the bottom of the main results column, directly below the article list and centered horizontally within that column. It occupies a relatively small vertical footprint but is surrounded by at least 32–48px of vertical whitespace above (from the last article card) and 48–64px below (before any footer), giving it a clear, isolated presence. The pagination elements—previous/next buttons and page numbers—are arranged in a single horizontal row, with 8–16px spacing between items and comfortable internal padding (8–12px vertically, 12–20px horizontally). The control does not stretch full width; instead, it sits in a compact cluster at the center, leaving visible dark teal space to the left and right, echoing the minimalist SaaS aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, pagination remains centered but may switch to a simpler pattern (e.g., only previous/next buttons and a concise page indicator), while preserving the generous vertical spacing around it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The blog-loading-error-panel is a feedback surface that appears within the bounds of the main results column, visually centered where the article list would normally be. When active, it occupies roughly 50–70% of the height of the visible results area and the full width of the results column, with substantial internal padding (32–48px) on all sides. On error, it may sit on an elevated card with strong contrast against the dark teal background and optionally dim or blur the underlying article list with a semi-transparent overlay, drawing the eye immediately to the message and any retry actions. For loading states, it can present as a centered panel with skeleton placeholders or a loading indicator, again occupying the core of the results column. Vertical spacing to the results summary and filters above is maintained (24–32px), so the panel feels integrated rather than floating arbitrarily.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contextual overlay within the results column. It either temporarily replaces the article list area or appears as an overlay with a dimmed backdrop constrained to the results column, not the full viewport, so the header, hero, and filter sidebar remain visible. It scrolls with the page if taller than the viewport but is typically sized to fit within a single scroll frame for clarity.

name: Article Detail
title: Article
description: Displays a full blog article with bookmarking, saving to reading lists, and navigation to related content.

core_features: Load and display full article details (title, body, publish date, tags, estimated read time) based on the URL parameter `id` using `getBlogArticleDetail`.
Show bookmark state for the article and allow the user to toggle bookmark status using `bookmarkArticle` and `unbookmarkArticle`.

Allow the user to save the article into a reading list by either selecting an existing list (`getReadingLists` + `addArticleToReadingList`) or creating a new list (`createReadingList` + `addArticleToReadingList`).
Display related/recommended articles returned by `getBlogArticleDetail` and allow navigation to their detail pages.
Provide contextual navigation back to either the blog listing (`blog.html`) or the originating reading list (`reading_lists.html?readingListId=...`) using the optional `fromListId` URL parameter.
Provide a "View list" navigation after successfully adding the article to a reading list, sending the selected or newly created `readingListId` to `reading_lists.html` as a URL parameter.
user_workflows:
1) View an article from the blog listing:
   - User clicks an article on `blog.html`, which links to `article_detail.html?id={articleId}`.
   - On page load, `article_detail.html` reads `id` from the URL.
   - Page calls `getBlogArticleDetail(articleId)`.
   - The article header, body, metadata (publish date, read time, tags), and bookmark state (`isBookmarked`) are rendered.
   - Related articles from `relatedArticles` are shown at the bottom with links to `article_detail.html?id={relatedArticleId}`.

2) Bookmark or unbookmark an article:
   - User sees a bookmark control reflecting `isBookmarked` from `getBlogArticleDetail`.
   - If the control is in an unbookmarked state and the user clicks it, the page calls `bookmarkArticle(articleId)`.
   - On success, the UI state for the bookmark control switches to bookmarked without reloading the page.
   - If the control is in a bookmarked state and the user clicks it, the page calls `unbookmarkArticle(articleId)`.
   - On success, the UI state switches back to unbookmarked.

3) Add article to an existing reading list:
   - User clicks a "Save to reading list" control.
   - A modal or panel opens and triggers `getReadingLists()` if reading lists have not yet been loaded during this page session.
   - The component lists all available reading lists (using each `readingList` object and `articleCount` for context).
   - User selects a reading list and confirms.
   - The page calls `addArticleToReadingList(readingListId, articleId)`.
   - On success, the modal shows a success state with an option "View list".
   - If user clicks "View list", the page navigates to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={readingListId}`.

4) Create a new reading list and add the article:
   - User clicks "Save to reading list" and opens the modal.
   - In the modal, user chooses an option to create a new reading list.
   - User fills a form with list name and optional description and submits.
   - The page calls `createReadingList(name, description)`.
   - On success, the new `ReadingList` is added to the in-memory list of reading lists.
   - The article is then added to this new list by calling `addArticleToReadingList(newReadingList.id, articleId)`.
   - On success, the UI shows confirmation and a "View list" button.
   - If user clicks "View list", the page navigates to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={newReadingList.id}`.

5) Open an article from a reading list and navigate back:
   - On `reading_lists.html`, user clicks an article, which links to `article_detail.html?id={articleId}&fromListId={readingListId}`.
   - `article_detail.html` reads both `id` and `fromListId` from the URL.
   - Page loads article via `getBlogArticleDetail(articleId)` and displays content.
   - A back-navigation control detects `fromListId` and displays a "Back to list" action.
   - If user clicks this action, the page navigates to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={fromListId}`.

6) Explore related articles from an article detail page:
   - After reading the article, user scrolls to related articles.
   - Related articles list is built from `relatedArticles` returned by `getBlogArticleDetail`.
   - User clicks a related article item.
   - Browser navigates to `article_detail.html?id={relatedArticleId}` (without a `fromListId` unless explicitly added).
   - New article is loaded, and the workflow repeats.
interactions: Click on back-navigation control to return either to `blog.html` or `reading_lists.html?readingListId=...` depending on `fromListId` presence.
Initial page load triggers fetching of article details via `getBlogArticleDetail(articleId)` using the `id` URL parameter.
Click on bookmark control triggers either `bookmarkArticle(articleId)` or `unbookmarkArticle(articleId)` and updates bookmark state on success.
Click on "Save to reading list" button opens the reading list selection/create modal and may trigger `getReadingLists()` if lists are not yet loaded.
Selecting an existing reading list inside the modal and confirming triggers `addArticleToReadingList(readingListId, articleId)` and updates confirmation state.
Submitting the "create new reading list" form triggers `createReadingList(name, description)`, then on success triggers `addArticleToReadingList(newReadingList.id, articleId)`.
Click on "View list" in the success state of the reading list modal navigates to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={createdOrSelectedReadingListId}`.
Click on a related article item navigates to `article_detail.html?id={relatedArticleId}` and reloads article details via `getBlogArticleDetail`.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is derived from URL parameters and API responses. The `id` URL parameter is mandatory and identifies the article to display; it is read on page initialization and whenever navigation within the page changes the article (e.g., by clicking a related article). The page calls `getBlogArticleDetail(id)` to obtain the `article`, `isBookmarked`, and `relatedArticles`. The optional `fromListId` URL parameter indicates that the article was opened from a specific reading list and is used only to determine the behavior and target of the back-navigation control (to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={fromListId}` instead of `blog.html`). Bookmark state is initialized from `isBookmarked` and updated in memory based on responses from `bookmarkArticle` and `unbookmarkArticle`; no state is persisted via localStorage or other client storage. Reading lists are loaded via `getReadingLists()` only when needed (e.g., when the user first opens the reading list modal) and remain in memory for the duration of the page session. When a new reading list is created with `createReadingList`, it is appended to the in-memory list so that the UI immediately reflects it. After calling `addArticleToReadingList`, the page temporarily stores the `readingListId` that was used so it can construct a navigation URL for the "View list" action (linking to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={thatId}`). All subsequent navigations to other pages rely solely on URL parameters; this page does not use localStorage for any state.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading for the article, manages high-level page state (current article, bookmark status, reading list modal state), and passes relevant data to child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: read URL parameters `id` and optionally `fromListId`, then call `getBlogArticleDetail(articleId)`.
onArticleLoaded: store `article`, `isBookmarked`, and `relatedArticles` in page state and render subcomponents.
onBookmarkStateChange: update local `isBookmarked` when bookmark/unbookmark operations succeed.
onOpenReadingListModal: toggle reading list modal visibility and, if reading lists are not yet loaded, trigger `getReadingLists()`.
onArticleAddedToReadingList: store the `readingListId` used for the operation so the modal can offer a "View list" link to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={readingListId}`.
onNavigateToRelatedArticle: update the browser URL to `article_detail.html?id={relatedArticleId}` and re-run `onPageLoad` logic.
onBackNavigation: navigate to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={fromListId}` if `fromListId` is present, otherwise navigate to `blog.html`.

components.functionality: Provides contextual navigation back to the article list or to the originating reading list based on URL parameters.
event_handlers: onRender: inspect URL for `fromListId`; if present, configure back button to link to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={fromListId}`, otherwise to `blog.html`.
onClickBack: perform navigation to the computed target URL.

components.functionality: Displays the article title, publish date, estimated read time, and integrates the bookmark toggle control.
event_handlers: onDataBind: receive `article` and `isBookmarked` from `article-detail-container` and display title and metadata.
onClickBookmark: delegate to `bookmark-toggle` component to handle bookmarking logic.

components.functionality: Shows and updates the bookmark state for the current article using `bookmarkArticle` and `unbookmarkArticle`.
event_handlers: onInit: receive `article.id` and `isBookmarked` from the article header.
onClickToggleWhenUnbookmarked: call `bookmarkArticle(articleId)`; on success, update control state to bookmarked and notify `article-detail-container` via `onBookmarkStateChange`.
onClickToggleWhenBookmarked: call `unbookmarkArticle(articleId)`; on success, update control state to unbookmarked and notify `article-detail-container` via `onBookmarkStateChange`.
onBookmarkError: display a non-blocking error message when bookmark or unbookmark operations fail, without changing the current visual state.

components.functionality: Displays the full body content of the current article and any tags related to topic and audience.
event_handlers: onDataBind: render `article.body` and metadata fields such as `article.topic_tags`, `article.audience_tags`, `article.publish_date`, and `article.read_time_minutes`.
onTagClick: navigate to `blog.html` with appropriate tag-based filters if that behavior is defined elsewhere (outside this page's APIs), or remain non-interactive if not implemented.

components.functionality: Provides the main "Save to reading list" entry point that opens the reading list modal for the current article.
event_handlers: onClick: call `article-detail-container.onOpenReadingListModal` with the current `article.id` to open the reading list modal and ensure reading lists are loaded.

components.functionality: Allows the user to add the current article to an existing or new reading list. Uses `getReadingLists`, `createReadingList`, and `addArticleToReadingList`.
event_handlers: onOpen: when first opened, if no reading lists are stored in page state, call `getReadingLists()` and store the resulting list of `{readingList, articleCount}` objects.
onSelectExistingList: user selects a `readingListId`; store selection in modal state.

onConfirmAddToExistingList: call `addArticleToReadingList(selectedReadingListId, articleId)`; on success, show a success message and inform `article-detail-container` using `onArticleAddedToReadingList(selectedReadingListId)`.

onSubmitCreateNewList: read new list `name` and optional `description` from form; call `createReadingList(name, description)`; on success, append the returned `ReadingList` to the local list of reading lists in the modal.

onAutoAddArticleToNewList: after `createReadingList` succeeds, call `addArticleToReadingList(newReadingList.id, articleId)`; on success, show success state and notify `article-detail-container` with `onArticleAddedToReadingList(newReadingList.id)`.

onClickViewList: after a successful add operation, navigate to `reading_lists.html?readingListId={createdOrSelectedReadingListId}` where `createdOrSelectedReadingListId` is the last `readingListId` passed to `onArticleAddedToReadingList`.
onClose: hide the modal and clear transient UI state (such as selected list or in-progress form inputs) while keeping loaded reading lists in memory for faster reopening.

components.functionality: Displays related or recommended articles and enables navigation to their detail pages.
event_handlers: onDataBind: receive `relatedArticles` from `article-detail-container` (array of `BlogArticle` objects) and render each as a selectable item.

onClickRelatedArticle: when user selects a related article, call `article-detail-container.onNavigateToRelatedArticle(relatedArticle.id)` to navigate to `article_detail.html?id={relatedArticleId}` and trigger reload logic.

components.functionality: Optionally reiterates tags or categories and can provide a secondary "Save to reading list" entry point for users who reach the end of the article.
event_handlers: onDataBind: receive `article` and display supplementary metadata (e.g., tags) at the end of the article.
onClickSaveToReadingList: delegate to `reading-list-entrypoint.onClick` to open the reading list modal for the current article.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width hero-like article header at the top of the article detail view. Immediately under the global site header (outside this scope) sits the article-navigation-bar as a slim control band. Below it, the article-header forms a two-column hero: the left column contains the article metadata and title (including the bookmark-toggle and reading-list-entrypoint), while the right column is reserved for the large hero image associated with the article (visually paired with the article-header). Beneath this hero block, the layout transitions into a centered, long-form article-content-section. Below the article body, the article-metadata-footer runs full width of the content column, followed by a horizontally arranged related-articles-section. The reading-list-modal overlays the entire page when invoked, centered above all content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom flow anchored by a full-width hero-like article header at the top of the article detail view. Immediately under the global site header (outside this scope) sits the article-navigation-bar as a slim control band. Below it, the article-header forms a two-column hero: the left column contains the article metadata and title (including the bookmark-toggle and reading-list-entrypoint), while the right column is reserved for the large hero image associated with the article (visually paired with the article-header). Beneath this hero block, the layout transitions into a centered, long-form article-content-section. Below the article body, the article-metadata-footer runs full width of the content column, followed by a horizontally arranged related-articles-section. The reading-list-modal overlays the entire page when invoked, centered above all content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The article-detail-container uses a max-width content area of about 1140–1280px centered on the page. The article-navigation-bar is a shallow band (roughly 10–15% of the hero's height) spanning the full width, with its contents constrained to the max-width container. The article-header occupies most of the above-the-fold area, with roughly 55–60% of the width given to the text column on the left and 40–45% to the hero image on the right. Generous outer margins (at least 48–64px top/bottom and 32–48px side padding within the content container) maintain breathing room. The article-content-section sits below on a white background, spanning roughly 70–80% of the max-width (e.g., 8–9 of 12 columns) and centered, allowing comfortable left and right margins for readability.
