The related-articles-section uses nearly the full max-width in a card grid, while the article-metadata-footer shares the same width as the article body. The reading-list-modal is moderate sized (about 40–50% of viewport width) and vertically centered with ample padding.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density remains low and editorial: large headings, generous line-height for body copy, and clear separation between sections. Major vertical gaps (48–64px) separate the hero, the article content, the metadata footer, and the related articles. Within components, padding typically follows an 8px-based scale: 16–24px for smaller controls, 32–48px for larger blocks such as the hero text container and modal interior. Only essential controls (bookmark-toggle, reading-list-entrypoint) are surfaced prominently near the top of the article; secondary metadata and related articles are visually lighter and separated by whitespace to reduce clutter.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F/Z-pattern from the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left with the article-navigation-bar (back/section nav), travels horizontally across any navigation items, then to any right-aligned controls in that bar. From there, the eye drops diagonally to the left side of the hero, where the category pill and main article title in the article-header command attention. The bookmark-toggle and reading-list-entrypoint are placed near the top-right area of the text column, so after reading the title and metadata, the user naturally notices them. The gaze then moves horizontally across to the hero image on the right. As the user scrolls, the flow becomes vertical: down through the article-content-section, into the article-metadata-footer, and finally across the related-articles-section cards. When opened, the reading-list-modal interrupts this flow and becomes the dominant focal point in the center of the viewport.

layout.overall_description: The article detail page presents a premium, calm SaaS-like reading experience on a dark teal canvas at the top that transitions into a clean white reading surface. Immediately under the site’s global header, a slim article-navigation-bar stretches across the width, framing the reading context. Below it, a spacious hero section fills the upper fold: on the left, the article-header stacks a small category pill, a large multi-line white heading, and concise metadata on the dark teal background, creating a strong focal block that takes up slightly more than half the width. In the top-right area of this text column sit the bookmark-toggle and a subtle reading-list-entrypoint, visually grouped with the metadata but offset enough to feel like actionable tools. To the right, occupying the remaining width, a tall hero image aligns vertically with the title area and extends down slightly to add visual weight, surrounded by comfortable negative space to the outer right. Scrolling down, the background transitions to white as a centered article-content-section card emerges below the hero, slightly narrower than the hero container, with generous margins and airy line spacing for long-form reading. At the bottom of the article body, an understated article-metadata-footer spans the same width, followed by a broader related-articles-section that uses the full grid width to display a row of article cards. The entire layout feels orderly, confident, and uncluttered, with vertical spacing and alignment reinforcing a clear path from navigation to hero, then into the article and finally to related content. When triggered, the reading-list-modal gracefully overlays this structure, dimming the background and centering a padded, rounded panel that keeps the user anchored in the brand’s calm, premium aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-detail-container spans the full browser width from left to right and the full page height from top to bottom. It establishes the primary background treatments and the centered grid. From the very top of the viewport down through the hero area (which includes the article-header and the hero image), the background is a solid dark teal brand color, creating a strong, immersive header zone. Within this full-width band, a centered content column—bounded by a max-width of approximately 1140–1280px—is used to position all visible components. The container sets left and right padding of roughly 24–32px on smaller screens and 48–64px on larger screens to ensure generous margins against the viewport edges. Below the hero, the article-detail-container smoothly transitions the background to white (or very light neutral) for the reading area, while still respecting the same max-width and horizontal padding. All child components (article-navigation-bar, article-header, article-content-section, related-articles-section, article-metadata-footer) are stacked vertically within this central column with ample vertical spacing (48–64px) between each major block to maintain the low-density, editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container in normal document flow managing max-width, horizontal centering, and background transitions between hero and content sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-navigation-bar sits directly beneath the global site header, forming a slim horizontal band that spans the full width of the viewport but keeps its content constrained to the central max-width column. Vertically, this bar is relatively shallow—about 48–64px in height, matching 2–3 base spacing units—with comfortable padding above and below its elements. Inside, content is laid out in a single horizontal row: on the far left, a small, subtle 'Back to blog' or breadcrumb text/link aligns with the left edge of the main content grid; in the center-left, there’s room for section labels or the article category; on the far right, optional secondary controls (like a filter or share icon) align with the right grid edge. The bar uses either a slightly lighter or darker shade of the dark teal to differentiate it from the hero while still blending seamlessly into the top band. It creates a clear visual separation between the global navigation and the article hero without competing with the main title below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky element that remains pinned to the top of the viewport (just below the global header) once the user scrolls past the initial hero, ensuring article navigation access while reading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-header forms the left side of a two-column hero block that dominates the upper portion of the page. This section is positioned immediately below the article-navigation-bar, aligned to the central grid within the dark teal background. Horizontally, it occupies roughly 55–60% of the max-width container—equivalent to about 7 of 12 columns—starting from the left grid edge. The right edge of the article-header aligns roughly with the vertical center of the page, where it visually meets the hero image column on the right. Within this left column, the content is stacked vertically with generous spacing: at the very top-left of the column is a small category pill ('Blog' or specific category) with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding, anchored tight to the column’s top with about 24–32px top padding from the container. Immediately below, a large, multi-line heading occupies the bulk of the vertical space, set in high-contrast white against the teal background, stretching up to 2–3 lines and aligning flush left. Under the heading, a row of metadata (author, publish date, reading time) runs horizontally, starting from the left. The bookmark-toggle and reading-list-entrypoint are visually grouped with this metadata row but right-aligned within the same column: they appear either on the same horizontal line (metadata left, controls right) or just above it, creating a gentle diagonal from the title down to the controls. Below the metadata, there is an optional short excerpt or subtitle with slightly smaller, lighter text. The entire article-header column is padded internally with approx. 32–48px on all sides, leaving a notable block of negative space between the text and the outer edges of the teal background, especially above and below the heading, to maintain the calm, spacious feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static hero content anchored in the top-left of the hero band, participating in the two-column layout alongside the hero image on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The bookmark-toggle is a compact, visually distinct control placed within the article-header’s left column but aligned towards the upper-right area of that column. It appears as an icon button or small pill (for example, a bookmark icon within a rounded rectangle) with about 8–10px vertical and 16–20px horizontal internal padding. Horizontally, it aligns with the right boundary of the text column (not the page edge), sitting roughly flush with that column’s right padding. Vertically, it is positioned near the top of the information stack: typically aligned on the same horizontal axis as either the metadata row below the title or slightly above it, creating a clear, immediately visible save control as soon as the user reads the article title. There is a comfortable 8–16px gap between the bookmark-toggle and any adjacent control (such as the reading-list-entrypoint) to avoid crowding. The surrounding negative space of the hero keeps the control prominent but not overwhelming; it reads as a primary utility specific to this article rather than a global action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within the article-header’s layout, moving with the hero text and maintaining its relative position on responsive breakpoints (stacking above or next to metadata on smaller screens).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-content-section begins just below the hero area and is visually separated from it by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px. It sits on a white (or very light neutral) background that contrasts with the dark teal hero above. Horizontally, this section is centered within the same max-width container but is slightly narrower than the full grid to improve readability—typically occupying around 70–80% of the container width, equivalent to 8–9 of 12 columns, with equal left and right margins creating clear gutters. The body text is left-aligned and flows vertically in a single column, with line lengths kept moderate (around 60–80 characters per line). Internal spacing uses 24–32px between major typographic blocks (headings, images, quotes) and 16–24px between paragraphs, maintaining the low-density, editorial feel. Any inline images or pull quotes align to the content column’s left edge, sometimes extending slightly wider but never reaching the full viewport edge, preserving the centered reading experience. The top of this section aligns roughly with where the hero image ends on the right, creating a smooth visual transition from the two-column hero to the single-column article body.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable long-form content section occupying the main reading area, with height growing based on article length and pushing subsequent sections downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-entrypoint is a secondary action button associated with the bookmark functionality, placed close to the bookmark-toggle within the article-header’s left column. Visually, it appears as a small text-plus-icon button (e.g., 'Add to reading list') with subtle emphasis: a light border or a low-contrast fill against the dark teal background. It shares a similar vertical size to the bookmark-toggle, with 8–12px vertical and 16–20px horizontal padding, but is visually less bold to maintain its secondary status. Horizontally, it sits either immediately to the left of the bookmark-toggle (with 8–16px horizontal spacing between them) or directly beneath it, left-aligned with the metadata row, depending on the available width and responsive breakpoints. Vertically, the control group (bookmark-toggle plus reading-list-entrypoint) is located in the upper-middle portion of the text column, near the title and metadata, so users naturally encounter it after reading the article’s heading. Ample surrounding whitespace ensures this cluster feels intentional and uncluttered, never crowding the title or metadata.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline control within the article-header that triggers a modal; its position remains logically tethered to the bookmark-toggle in all responsive states, stacking below the bookmark on narrow viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-modal appears as an overlay that temporarily dominates the viewport when the user activates the reading-list-entrypoint. It sits centered both horizontally and vertically on top of a semi-transparent dark overlay that covers the entire page from edge to edge, subtly dimming the article content and hero beneath. The modal panel itself has a width of approximately 40–50% of the viewport on desktop (e.g., 480–640px) and a max-height of around 70% of the viewport height, with a white background and rounded corners to maintain the premium SaaS-like feel. It includes generous internal padding—24–32px on all sides—creating a calm, spacious interior. The top area of the modal contains a concise title (e.g., 'Save to reading list') aligned left, with a close icon aligned to the top-right corner. Below, a vertically stacked list of reading lists or options is displayed with 16–24px vertical spacing between items. At the bottom, a primary confirmation button and a secondary cancel link are aligned right, with enough spacing so they don’t feel cramped. The modal respects the overall typography scale from the rest of the page, using slightly smaller headings than the hero but still clear and confident.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, centered overlay above all page content with a full-screen dimmed backdrop; scrollable internally if content exceeds max-height, while preventing background scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related-articles-section is positioned below the article-metadata-footer at the bottom of the page, separated from the main article body by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to signal a transition from primary content to exploratory content. It sits on the same white background as the article body but can optionally use a very light neutral tint or a subtle top border to define the section. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full width of the central content container—about 10–12 of 12 columns—giving room for a row of related article cards. At the top-left of this section, a section heading like 'Related articles' is left-aligned, with a comfortable 16–24px space below it before the cards start. Below the heading, 3–4 article cards are arranged in a responsive grid: on large screens, a 3-column layout where each card occupies roughly 30–32% of the width with 24–32px horizontal gaps; on smaller screens, these cards stack into 2 or 1 column with consistent vertical spacing. Each card honors the spacious aesthetic with internal padding (16–24px), a small thumbnail image at the top, and concise titles and metadata. The entire section leaves substantial bottom margin (48–64px) before the end of the page to avoid the layout feeling cut off.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section in normal document flow, expanding vertically as more related article cards are present and responding to viewport width with a fluid grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The article-metadata-footer sits immediately below the article-content-section, separated from the last paragraph of the article by about 32–40px of vertical space. It shares the same width and horizontal alignment as the article body—centered and limited to roughly 70–80% of the container width, creating a cohesive vertical column. Visually, it appears as a lighter-weight, low-density strip of information: tags, categories, last updated date, and potentially simple share icons. The content is arranged in a horizontal row where possible: tags or chips aligned to the left, and optional actions (e.g., share icons) aligned to the right. On smaller screens, this row gracefully wraps into multiple lines while maintaining left alignment. Internal spacing is modest but still airy: 16–24px padding above and below the metadata, and 8–12px between individual tags or icons. A subtle top border or a slight tone shift from the article body text differentiates the footer, signaling that the primary reading experience has concluded and this area contains supplementary information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline section directly following the article body within the same content column, acting as a transitional band before the related-articles-section.

name: Reading Lists
title: Reading Lists
description: View and manage all reading lists, open a specific list to see its saved articles, remove articles, rename or delete lists, and navigate to article detail pages.

core_features: Load all reading lists for the current user using getReadingLists and display each list with its name and article count.
Support deep-linking to a specific reading list via the readingListId URL parameter and load its details with getReadingListDetail.
Allow users to select a reading list from the overview to open it, update the URL readingListId parameter, and fetch its articles via getReadingListDetail.
Allow users to rename a reading list by submitting a new name, calling renameReadingList, and updating all views showing that list name.
Allow users to delete a reading list by confirming an action, calling deleteReadingList, and removing it from the overview and current selection.
Allow users to remove an article from the currently opened reading list by calling removeArticleFromReadingList and updating the article list and counts.
Provide navigation from each article entry to article_detail.html using the articleId and fromListId (current readingListId) as URL parameters.
user_workflows:
View all reading lists:
1) User navigates to reading_lists.html (via header navigation or direct URL).
2) On page load, page-init-controller calls getReadingLists.
3) reading-lists-overview displays all returned lists with their names and article counts.
4) If there is at least one list and no readingListId is in the URL, the first list is set as selected and reading-list-detail-panel is loaded via getReadingListDetail.

Open a specific list via deep link:
1) User opens reading_lists.html?readingListId={id} from article_detail.html or dashboard.html.
2) page-init-controller parses readingListId from the URL and calls getReadingLists.
3) After lists load, the controller checks if a list with the given readingListId exists.
4) If it exists, it is set as the selected list and getReadingListDetail is called for that id.
5) reading-list-detail-panel displays that list’s metadata and its articles.

Switch between lists from the overview:
1) User clicks a different list in reading-lists-overview.
2) The current selectedListId in page state is updated to the clicked list’s id.
3) The URL query parameter readingListId is updated to this id (without full page reload if possible).
4) page-init-controller or the overview component triggers getReadingListDetail with the new readingListId.
5) reading-list-detail-panel re-renders with the new list’s information and articles.

Rename a reading list:
1) User clicks a rename action (for the selected list) in reading-list-detail-panel or in reading-lists-overview.
2) reading-list-rename-modal opens, prefilled with the current list name.
3) User edits the name and submits the form.
4) The modal calls renameReadingList with readingListId and newName.
5) On success, the updated ReadingList object is used to update the list name in page state.
6) reading-lists-overview and reading-list-detail-panel both reflect the new name, and the modal closes.

Delete a reading list:
1) User clicks delete for the currently selected list in reading-list-detail-panel or in reading-lists-overview.
2) reading-list-delete-confirmation-modal opens, summarizing that only the list will be removed (not the underlying articles).
3) User confirms deletion.
4) The modal calls deleteReadingList with the readingListId of the selected list.
5) On success, the list is removed from the list collection in page state.
6) If the deleted list was selected, page-init-controller selects a new list (e.g., the next remaining list, if any) and updates the readingListId URL parameter, triggering getReadingListDetail for the new selection; if no lists remain, the detail panel clears and an empty state is shown.
7) The confirmation modal closes.

Remove an article from a reading list:
1) In reading-list-detail-panel, user clicks a remove action for an article.
2) The action triggers removeArticleFromReadingList with the current readingListId and the articleId of the selected article.
3) On success, the article is removed from the current list’s article collection in page state.
4) The article disappears from reading-list-detail-panel, and the articleCount for that reading list in reading-lists-overview is decremented (either via state update or by re-calling getReadingLists).

Open article detail from a reading list:
1) In reading-list-detail-panel, user clicks the article title or a dedicated link within reading-list-article-item.
2) The click navigates to article_detail.html with URL parameters id={articleId} and fromListId={current readingListId}.
3) article_detail.html can use fromListId to provide context-aware navigation back to the same reading list.
interactions: Click on a list item in the reading-lists-overview selects that list, updates the readingListId URL parameter, and triggers loading of its details with getReadingListDetail.
Click on a rename control for a list opens reading-list-rename-modal with the current list name populated.
Submitting the rename form in reading-list-rename-modal triggers renameReadingList; on success, the updated name is propagated to all components displaying that list and the modal is closed.
Click on a delete control for a list opens reading-list-delete-confirmation-modal asking for confirmation.

Confirming deletion in reading-list-delete-confirmation-modal triggers deleteReadingList; on success, the list is removed from the overview and, if it was selected, a fallback list is auto-selected or the detail view is cleared.

Click on a remove or delete icon for an article inside reading-list-article-item triggers removeArticleFromReadingList; on success, the article is removed from the current list’s articles and the list’s articleCount is updated.
Click on an article title or 'View article' link inside reading-list-article-item performs a navigation to article_detail.html?id={articleId}&fromListId={current readingListId}.

Initial page load and any URL change that affects readingListId are handled by page-init-controller, which calls getReadingLists and, if applicable, getReadingListDetail to keep the UI in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is the currently selected reading list, represented by readingListId in the URL query string. On first load, page-init-controller reads readingListId from the URL. It then calls getReadingLists to fetch all reading lists. Once lists are available: (1) If readingListId is present in the URL and matches a list returned by getReadingLists, that list is set as the selected list and getReadingListDetail is called with that readingListId. (2) If readingListId is present but does not match any loaded list (for example, if it was deleted or invalid), the controller selects a default list if one exists (for example, the first returned list), updates the URL readingListId to this list's id, and calls getReadingListDetail with the new id. (3) If no readingListId is present and at least one list exists, the controller selects a default list, updates the URL to include readingListId for that list, and calls getReadingListDetail. (4) If no lists exist, reading-lists-overview shows an empty state and reading-list-detail-panel remains empty without calling getReadingListDetail. When a user manually selects a different list from the overview, the page updates the selectedListId in memory, updates the URL readingListId parameter (using client-side navigation without reloading when possible), and calls getReadingListDetail for the new id. After renameReadingList or deleteReadingList complete successfully, the in-memory collection of lists is updated and, if necessary, the readingListId parameter is changed (for example, when the selected list is deleted). After removeArticleFromReadingList succeeds, the current list's article collection in memory is updated and the related articleCount for that list is kept in sync either by adjusting the count locally or by re-calling getReadingLists. No localStorage or other client-side storage mechanisms are used; the URL readingListId parameter and live API data are the single sources of truth for which list is displayed.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and synchronization between the URL readingListId parameter and the currently selected reading list. On page load or when the readingListId URL parameter changes, it calls getReadingLists and, based on the result and the parameter, determines which reading list (if any) should be selected and whether to call getReadingListDetail.

Displays all reading lists returned from getReadingLists, each with its name and articleCount. Highlights the currently selected list based on the selectedListId provided by page-init-controller. Allows users to click a list to select it and trigger loading of its details. Provides entry points (buttons or menu actions) for renaming and deleting a list.

Displays the selected reading list's details, including its name and description, and a list of its associated articles returned from getReadingListDetail. Provides actions to rename or delete the current list, and for each article, exposes navigation to the article detail page and an option to remove the article from the list.

Opens when the user chooses to rename a list. Shows a form field prepopulated with the current list name. On submission, calls renameReadingList with the readingListId of the list being edited and the newName entered by the user. On success, updates the name of that list in the page’s list collection and the currently selected list (if applicable), then closes the modal.

Confirms deletion of a reading list. When opened, it references the id of the list that the user intends to delete. On confirmation, calls deleteReadingList with that readingListId. On success, removes the list from the in-memory collection and informs page-init-controller so that the selected list and URL readingListId parameter can be updated appropriately, then closes the modal.

Represents a single article within the currently selected reading list. Renders basic article information (such as title and metadata from BlogArticle) and provides two main actions: navigate to article_detail.html and remove the article from the current reading list. Navigation uses the articleId from the bound BlogArticle and the current readingListId to build the URL. Removal calls removeArticleFromReadingList and, on success, notifies the parent component to update the article list and articleCount.

Displays when getReadingLists returns no reading lists, or when a selected list has no articles, depending on configuration. In the case of no lists, it indicates that there are currently no reading lists to display. In the case of a list without articles, it indicates that the selected list has no saved articles. Does not perform API calls itself but responds to state provided by page-init-controller and reading-list-detail-panel.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width fixed header at the very top, then a full-width hero-like section for the Reading Lists feature. Within this hero section, content is arranged in a two-column split: the left column contains the small category pill (e.g., “Reading”), the large page title (“Reading Lists”), and a brief contextual line; the right column is visually anchored by the reading-list-detail-panel, mirroring the mockup’s hero image position. Directly under the heading block on the left, the reading-lists-overview list-view continues downward in the same column. The right column is occupied top-to-bottom by the reading-list-detail-panel and its reading-list-article-items. Empty states appear centrally within their respective column areas when needed. Modals overlay the entire viewport, centered above all content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width fixed header at the very top, then a full-width hero-like section for the Reading Lists feature. Within this hero section, content is arranged in a two-column split: the left column contains the small category pill (e.g., “Reading”), the large page title (“Reading Lists”), and a brief contextual line; the right column is visually anchored by the reading-list-detail-panel, mirroring the mockup’s hero image position. Directly under the heading block on the left, the reading-lists-overview list-view continues downward in the same column. The right column is occupied top-to-bottom by the reading-list-detail-panel and its reading-list-article-items. Empty states appear centrally within their respective column areas when needed. Modals overlay the entire viewport, centered above all content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies a shallow band at the top (roughly 10–15% of the viewport height) with a dark teal background. Below, the main content (hero + list/detail region) fills the remaining viewport height, also sitting on the dark teal or slightly variegated background. A centered content container (max-width ~1140–1280px) uses a 12-column grid. Within this container, the left column (hero text + reading-lists-overview) occupies about 55–60% of the width (roughly 7 of 12 columns), and the right column (reading-list-detail-panel) about 40–45% (roughly 5 of 12 columns). Vertical spacing between header and hero content, and between hero heading and list/detail content, uses generous 48–64px gaps. Internal paddings in panels and list items follow the 8px base scale (16–24px for items, 32–48px for panels and modals). Modals are sized to about 30–40% of viewport width on desktop with ample margin from the viewport edges.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout keeps density low and editorial: few elements per screen, large type, and plenty of whitespace. The hero title and active list title are treated with bold, oversized typography. Lists use generous vertical padding and increased line spacing, preventing visual clutter. Each list or article row is clearly separated with subtle dividers or spacing instead of tight table-like packing. Empty-state messages are intentionally sparse: a concise icon or glyph, a short heading, and a brief description, with plenty of whitespace around them. Modals show only essential fields and actions, with clear separation between body content and action buttons.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F/Z pattern. The eye starts at the logo in the top-left of the fixed header, moves horizontally across navigation to any primary CTA on the top-right, then diagonally down into the main hero content: first the small category pill on the left, then the large “Reading Lists” heading beneath it. From there, the eye naturally moves down the left column through the reading-lists-overview. When a list is selected, attention shifts right to the reading-list-detail-panel at the same vertical level as the hero’s heading, mirroring the hero image’s position in the mockup. The right panel’s list of reading-list-article-items is scanned vertically downward. When a modal opens, the flow is temporarily redirected to the centered overlay, which dims the background and clearly elevates the modal’s content as the primary focus.

layout.overall_description: A modern, dark-teal SaaS-like header stretches across the top of the viewport, slim but confident, with the logo on the left, navigation toward the center-left, and a succinct account or primary action area on the right. Beneath this, a full-width band of the same dark teal background holds the main Reading Lists hero content within a centered, max-width container. On the left, a small, pill-shaped label (for example, “Reading” or “Library”) floats near the top, followed by a large, multi-line title like “Reading Lists” set in bright, high-contrast typography. A short, calming line of supporting text may sit below, all surrounded by generous margins. This left column then transitions seamlessly into the reading-lists-overview: a vertically stacked list of user-created reading lists that fills the lower portion of the left side. On the right, aligned so that its top matches roughly the mid or upper area of the hero heading, the reading-list-detail-panel appears as a tall, card-like surface occupying about two-fifths of the container width. It feels like the hero image in the mockup but is instead a functional detail view with the selected list’s title, controls, and its articles. The background remains uncluttered, with wide gutters of teal space around the main content block, reinforcing a premium, spacious feel. When there are no lists or no articles, the empty-state-message appears centered within the relevant column, surrounded by breathing room. Rename and delete confirmation modals, when triggered, fade in as centered cards above a dimmed backdrop, retaining the same clean typography and generous spacing, and briefly becoming the dominant focal point of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component does not render any visible UI. Conceptually, it sits at the very top of the page structure, just below the global header and above the main content container in the DOM hierarchy, but it occupies no visual space and does not affect layout flow. It initializes and manages the data for reading-lists-overview and reading-list-detail-panel, determining which list is selected on load and which empty-state-message to show if there is no data.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, non-interactive component that does not participate in layout; logically positioned at the root of the page’s content container to orchestrate state for visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-lists-overview occupies the left column of the main content area, directly beneath the hero heading. It aligns with the left edge of the centered content container and spans roughly 55–60% of the container width, consistent with a 7-of-12 column allocation. Vertically, there is a gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the large page heading and the top of the overview component, keeping the hero’s title visually distinct. The overview itself reads like a calm, editorial list: a subtle section label (e.g., “Your lists”) and any header controls (such as a simple inline “New list” button) sit at the top, followed by a vertically stacked list of list items. Each list entry appears as a horizontally full-width row within this left column, with comfortable vertical padding (16–24px) and clear separation between rows (e.g., 8–16px vertical gaps or light dividers). The entire overview scrolls with the page rather than independently; on tall screens, it may show all lists without internal scrolling. There is a wide vertical margin (at least 48–64px) between the bottom of the last list item and the page footer, preserving the spacious feel. The right edge of this component aligns to form a clean vertical column boundary between it and the reading-list-detail-panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document flow inside the left column of the main container. Its height grows based on content; on overflow beyond the viewport, the entire page scrolls. It maintains a fixed left alignment and column width across breakpoints until small screens, where it would stack above the detail panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-detail-panel occupies the right column of the main content area and visually mirrors the hero image’s position from the mockup. It is positioned to the immediate right of the reading-lists-overview with a generous horizontal gap of about 32–48px between columns. It spans roughly 40–45% of the content width (about 5 of 12 grid columns), starting slightly lower than the top of the page but aligning so that its top edge is near the vertical middle or upper area of the hero heading block. This creates the sense that the hero title on the left and the detail panel on the right are part of the same two-column hero composition. The panel is styled as a large, card-like surface with rounded corners and internal padding of about 32–40px, floating against the dark teal background. At its top, the selected reading list’s name appears in a prominent, but slightly smaller than hero, heading. Inline with or just below this title are list-level actions (such as rename and delete triggers) aligned to the right edge of the panel. Below, the main body contains the list of reading-list-article-item entries stacked vertically with comfortable spacing. If the selected list has many articles, the panel’s content area scrolls with the page; optionally, the panel can have an internal scroll if its height is constrained, but it should still maintain generous top and bottom padding so the content never feels cramped. When no list is selected or a list is empty, the empty-state-message appears centered within this panel’s interior. The bottom of the panel generally aligns with or slightly above the bottom of the reading-lists-overview, keeping the overall composition balanced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the main content flow but visually treated as a distinct card anchored in the right column. On desktop, it remains side by side with the overview while the page scrolls. On smaller screens, it would naturally stack below the overview. The panel does not fix or float; it respects normal scrolling while preserving its two-column relationship at larger breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-rename-modal appears as a centered overlay card above the entire page when triggered from the reading-list-detail-panel. The background content (header, hero, overview, detail panel) is dimmed with a semi-transparent dark layer, reinforcing the modal as the current focal point. The modal card itself is horizontally centered, with equal left and right margins and positioned slightly above the exact vertical center (around 45% down from the top), mimicking the slightly elevated hero content feel. On desktop, it spans roughly 30–40% of the viewport width, never touching the edges, with a maximum width consistent with the grid (e.g., 480–560px). Internal padding is generous (32–40px), with a clear vertical stack: the modal title at the top (e.g., “Rename List”), followed by a single text input field spanning nearly the full width of the card, and then a horizontal row of actions (Cancel and Save) aligned to the right at the bottom. There is substantial vertical spacing (16–24px) between title, input, and actions, so the form feels simple and unhurried. The surrounding dimmed area provides ample negative space, echoing the mockup’s low-density aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay that sits above all other content and remains centered even when the page is scrolled. Background scrolling should be disabled or visually discouraged while the modal is open. The card’s size adapts slightly to viewport width but always keeps comfortable margins on all sides.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-delete-confirmation-modal is a compact, centered overlay card that appears above the dimmed page when a user chooses to delete a reading list, likely from within the reading-list-detail-panel. It shares the same visual structure as the rename modal but is slightly smaller to reflect its concise content. Positioned at the center of the viewport, it spans about 25–35% of the viewport width on desktop, again with generous side margins and never approaching the edges. Inside, a short, bold title (e.g., “Delete this list?”) appears at the top, followed by a brief explanatory line of text that may wrap to two lines, all with 16–24px vertical separation. At the bottom, a row of actions (e.g., Cancel and Delete) is aligned to the right, with the destructive action styled clearly but cleanly. The card uses 24–32px internal padding, with ample space above and below the text, maintaining the airy, premium feel. The dimmed backdrop and central positioning ensure the confirmation moment feels deliberate and focused.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position, centered overlay above all content, similar to the rename modal. It remains visually centered when scrolling and temporarily suspends interaction with the underlying layout until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each reading-list-article-item is a horizontally full-width row within the reading-list-detail-panel’s content area. It respects the panel’s internal padding, starting flush with the panel’s left content edge but leaving a comfortable margin (16–24px) above and below each row. The item layout is left-aligned: the article title appears in strong, readable typography at the top left of the row, with any secondary information (such as source, estimated reading time, or tags) in a smaller, lighter style beneath or beside it. On the far right side of the row, aligned with the article title’s baseline, a minimal remove or more-options icon sits, separated by ample horizontal space so it does not crowd the text. Rows are separated by either a subtle divider line or 8–12px of vertical whitespace, avoiding a cramped list appearance. When hovered or focused, the row can subtly lift or darken slightly to indicate interactivity, but the overall composition remains calm and uncluttered. Clicking on the main title area can navigate to the article detail page, while the right-aligned control remains clearly isolated for removal actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows vertically within the reading-list-detail-panel; its width is constrained by the panel’s width and grid alignment. It does not scroll independently; instead, the panel or page scrolls. Responsive behavior ensures the right-aligned control wraps gracefully below or near the title on very narrow screens while preserving comfortable spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-state-message appears contextually either within the left reading-lists-overview column when there are no reading lists, or inside the reading-list-detail-panel when a selected list has no articles. In both cases, it is centered within the available space of its parent container, both horizontally and, as much as possible, vertically. The message is composed of a simple icon or abstract shape at the top (optional), a short, friendly heading in moderately large type, and a one–two line description below, all arranged in a vertical stack with generous spacing (16–24px between elements and at least 32–48px padding to the container edges). In the overview context, the message sits directly under the hero heading, with enough top margin (32–48px) so it feels intentionally placed, not awkwardly attached to the title. In the detail-panel context, the message is centered within the card-like panel, taking on the role that the hero image played in the mockup: a focal, calm patch of content within otherwise empty space. Any suggested primary action (such as a link or button to create a list or add articles) is placed beneath the description with subtle emphasis, and ample room around it to avoid clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within its parent container and replacing the typical list content when active. It does not alter the parent’s size beyond normal content flow; it simply occupies the central area of the left column or right panel. It scrolls with the parent and does not fix or float.

name: Challenges
title: Challenges
description: Browse, filter, sort, and paginate structured challenges or programs, then open a specific challenge detail page.

core_features: Initialize available filter and sort options for challenges using getChallengeFilterOptions and apply any pre-set URL parameters.
Search and list challenges using searchChallenges with filters for topic, duration in days, and relative start date range.
Allow users to change filters (topic, duration, start-date window) and sort order, updating the URL parameters and reloading results via searchChallenges.
Provide pagination controls that call searchChallenges with a page index and page size, keeping pagination state in the URL.
Allow users to open a specific challenge detail page by clicking a challenge item, navigating to challenge_detail.html with the selected challenge id as URL parameter.
user_workflows: Workflow 1 – Basic browsing from navigation: 1) User opens challenges.html from header/footer navigation without URL parameters. 2) Page controller calls getChallengeFilterOptions to retrieve available topics, duration options, start-date ranges, and sort options. 3) After filter options are loaded, the controller determines default filter/sort values (for example using the first sort option) and the initial page index and page size (from app configuration if not provided via URL). 4) The controller calls searchChallenges with filters derived from current state and displays the list of results. 5) User scrolls the list and clicks any challenge card to open challenge_detail.html?id=<challengeId> in the same tab.

Workflow 2 – Pre-filtered entry from index.html: 1) User clicks a link on index.html that points to challenges.html with one or more URL parameters (topic, durationDays, startDateRange, sortBy). 2) On load, the page controller parses these URL parameters and stores them as the current filter/sort state. 3) The controller calls getChallengeFilterOptions and validates incoming URL values against the available options (for example checking if topic value exists in the returned topics list and if sortBy exists in sortOptions). 4) Invalid or unsupported URL parameters are removed from the current state (and may be removed from the URL) before the first search. 5) The controller calls searchChallenges with the validated filters and sortBy; the listing, filter controls, and active-filter summary all reflect these initial values.

Workflow 3 – Adjusting filters and sort order: 1) User changes a filter in the filter bar (for example selects a different topic or duration) or changes the sort option. 2) The filter bar component emits an event to the page controller with the new filter/sort state. 3) The page controller updates the URL parameters (topic, durationDays, startDateRange, sortBy) to match the new state and resets the current page index to the first page. 4) The controller calls searchChallenges again with updated filters and sortBy, keeping the same pageSize. 5) The challenge list and active-filter summary update based on the new results and state.

Workflow 4 – Pagination: 1) After results are loaded, the pagination component receives totalCount, current page, and pageSize. 2) User clicks a pagination control (next/previous or a specific page number). 3) The pagination component notifies the page controller with the requested page index. 4) The controller updates the URL parameter for page and calls searchChallenges with the same filters, sortBy, and pageSize but the new page index. 5) The challenge list updates to display results for the selected page while filter and sort controls remain unchanged.

Workflow 5 – Clearing filters and resetting the view: 1) User clicks a 'clear all filters' control in the filter bar or the active-filter summary component. 2) The component informs the page controller to reset the filter state to default (no topic filter, no duration filter, no start-date filter, and default sort option). 3) The controller removes related URL parameters (topic, durationDays, startDateRange, optionally sortBy if default is implied) and sets the page parameter to the first page. 4) The controller calls searchChallenges with the reset filters and default sortBy. 5) The challenge list shows the unfiltered results, and the UI components reflect that no filters are active.

Workflow 6 – Browser navigation with URL-based state: 1) User applies several filters and changes pages; each change updates the URL. 2) User presses the browser back or forward button. 3) The page controller listens to URL change events (for example using a popstate handler), re-parses the URL parameters, and restores filter, sort, and pagination state from the URL. 4) The controller calls searchChallenges again with filters and sortBy derived from the URL and updates the UI to match the restored state.

Workflow 7 – Opening a challenge detail: 1) From any search result page, user clicks on a challenge card or its title. 2) The challenge list component reads the associated challenge id from the bound Challenge data. 3) It triggers navigation to challenge_detail.html, appending a URL parameter id=<challengeId>. 4) The detail page (handled elsewhere) uses that id to load and display the full challenge information.
interactions: Changing topic filter: When the user selects a topic option, the filter bar emits an onFilterChange event with the new topic value; the controller updates the topic URL parameter, resets the page parameter to the first page, and calls searchChallenges with the updated filters.

Changing duration filter: When the user selects a duration option (mapped to an exact number of days from the durationOptions returned by getChallengeFilterOptions), the filter bar emits an onFilterChange event; the controller updates the durationDays URL parameter and triggers a searchChallenges call.

Changing start-date range filter: When the user selects a start-date range (using the value from startDateRanges returned by getChallengeFilterOptions), the filter bar emits an onFilterChange event; the controller updates the startDateRange URL parameter and re-runs searchChallenges.

Changing sort order: When the user selects a different sort option, the filter bar emits an onSortChange event with the selected sort option value; the controller updates the sortBy URL parameter, resets the page parameter, and calls searchChallenges with the same filters but new sortBy.

Submitting filters (if filters require an explicit submit): If the filter bar uses an apply button, an onSubmit event sends the current filter selections to the controller; the controller synchronizes all corresponding URL parameters and invokes searchChallenges once with the consolidated state.

Clearing all filters: When the user clicks a clear-filters control, the filter bar or active-filter summary emits an onFilterReset event; the controller removes topic, durationDays, and startDateRange from the URL, resets sortBy to its default, sets the page to the first page, and calls searchChallenges.

Pagination click: When the user clicks next, previous, or a specific page number, the pagination component emits an onPageChange event with the requested page index; the controller updates the page URL parameter and calls searchChallenges with the same filters, sortBy, and pageSize but the new page.

Challenge card click: When the user clicks on a challenge card or link, the challenge list component handles an onCardClick event by reading the challenge's id from the bound data and navigating to challenge_detail.html?id=<id>.

Handling no results: If searchChallenges returns totalCount equal to zero, the challenge list component shows a no-results state and, if available, a simple action (for example a button) that triggers onFilterReset to clear filters and reload results.

Responding to direct URL edits: If the user manually edits the URL parameters (for example typing a topic or changing page), the browser reload or navigation event causes the controller to parse and validate parameters, then call searchChallenges with those values or fall back to defaults when invalid.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for filters, sorting, and pagination, with API responses providing the actual challenge data and available filter options. On initial load, the controller reads topic, durationDays, startDateRange, sortBy, page, and pageSize from the URL query string. If any of these parameters are missing, the controller fills in defaults (for example, default page index and pageSize, and default sortBy based on the first option returned by getChallengeFilterOptions). The controller calls getChallengeFilterOptions first to obtain valid option sets for topics, durationOptions, startDateRanges, and sortOptions. After receiving filter options, it validates incoming URL parameters: topic is valid if it matches one of the topic option values returned; durationDays is valid if it matches one of the durationOptions days values; startDateRange is valid if it matches one of the startDateRanges values; sortBy is valid if it matches one of the sortOptions values. Invalid values are removed from the internal state and may also be removed from the URL to keep the URL consistent. The state object that the controller maintains (e.g. currentTopic, currentDurationDays, currentStartDateRange, currentSortBy, currentPage, currentPageSize) is always synchronized back to the URL when the user changes filters, sort order, or page. Any change in this state triggers a new call to searchChallenges with parameters: filters (an object containing topic, durationDays, and startDateRange if defined), sortBy (the selected sort option value), page (current numeric page index), and pageSize (current numeric page size). The search results (results array and totalCount) do not change the URL directly but they drive the UI components (list, pagination, empty state). When the browser back/forward buttons are used, or when the URL is reloaded with different parameters, the controller re-parses the URL, reconstructs the internal state, and re-calls searchChallenges so that the displayed list always matches the URL parameters. No client-side persistence mechanism like localStorage is used; all shareable state is encoded in the URL parameters and rebuilt using getChallengeFilterOptions and searchChallenges on each load.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, parses and validates URL parameters, manages in-memory filter/sort/pagination state, calls getChallengeFilterOptions once on load, calls searchChallenges whenever state changes, and synchronizes state with URL parameters.

Displays filter controls for topic, duration (in days), and start-date range, as well as a sort selector using options from getChallengeFilterOptions. Emits events when the user changes any control, optionally provides an apply/clear button, and reflects the current state from URL-derived values.

Shows a concise summary of currently active filters (topic, durationDays, startDateRange) derived from the controller state and getChallengeFilterOptions labels, and allows removal of individual filters or clearing all filters.
data_binding: FilterOptions (for mapping current values to human-readable labels)
Challenge (indirectly through filters applied to searchChallenges)

components.functionality: Renders the list of challenges returned by searchChallenges, showing basic details such as title, topic, duration, and start date for each challenge, and provides clickable elements that navigate to the challenge detail page.

Displays pagination controls based on totalCount from searchChallenges, along with current page and pageSize derived from URL/state. Emits events when the user selects another page, which the controller uses to call searchChallenges for that page.
data_binding: Challenge (totalCount and current page of results)

components.functionality: Listens to the current results from searchChallenges and, when totalCount is zero, hides the list and pagination and instead shows a no-results state with an optional action to reset filters.
data_binding: Challenge (results array and totalCount from searchChallenges)

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width global header (logo left, nav and actions right, implied but not part of this spec), then a hero-like page controller band at the top of the main content. Below that, interactive controls (filter bar and active filter summary) form a compact control strip, followed by the primary content region (challenge list or empty state) and finally pagination at the bottom. Within the hero band, the page controller content is left-aligned and occupies a larger left column, echoing the mockup’s text-left / image-right split, with the right side left visually lighter for breathing space or decorative elements.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a full-width global header (logo left, nav and actions right, implied but not part of this spec), then a hero-like page controller band at the top of the main content. Below that, interactive controls (filter bar and active filter summary) form a compact control strip, followed by the primary content region (challenge list or empty state) and finally pagination at the bottom. Within the hero band, the page controller content is left-aligned and occupies a larger left column, echoing the mockup’s text-left / image-right split, with the right side left visually lighter for breathing space or decorative elements.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header occupies roughly 10–15% of the viewport height. The hero-like page controller section beneath it takes about 30–40% of the above-the-fold area on desktop, with the left 55–60% of the content width reserved for title and context, and the remaining 40–45% kept relatively open. The filter controls occupy a shallow horizontal band below the hero, spanning the full content width but with modest height. The challenge list consumes the majority of vertical space below the fold, rendered within a centered content container (max-width around 1140–1280px). Generous vertical spacing (48–64px) separates major sections: between header and hero, hero and filters, filters and list, and list and pagination.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and editorial, mirroring the mockup. Elements are large and well-spaced: the page title is bold and takes multiple lines; the filter bar items are separated with comfortable horizontal gaps; chips in the filter summary have breathing room; challenge cards are arranged in a grid with substantial gutters and vertical spacing. There are no sidebars or crowded multi-column text blocks; instead, a single main content column within the centered container is used, with the grid applied only to card layouts inside that column.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern. The eye starts at the top-left with the brand and page title in the hero controller, moves horizontally across the top where global nav and potential primary actions live, then diagonally down to the left-aligned filters and active filter chips. From there, the gaze continues downward into the first row of challenge cards and then moves left-to-right across the grid. The strong contrast between the bright text and the dark teal background, plus the larger typography in the hero and card titles, anchors attention on the left before scan paths flow out across the card grid and down toward pagination.

layout.overall_description: The Challenges page sits on a dark teal canvas with a centered content container that never feels cramped. A slim, full-width header stays fixed at the very top, and beneath it a hero-like band introduces the page: a small pill and large, confident heading are anchored in the left 60% of the content area, while the right side remains visually lighter, allowing the typography and message to dominate. Below this, a horizontal filter bar stretches across the container, with a search field and sorted controls aligned along a single row; as you scroll, this bar can pin itself just under the header, quietly shadowed, so filters remain accessible. Directly under it, a row of small, pill-shaped active filters appears when needed, hugging the left edge and wrapping onto multiple lines if there are many. The bulk of the page is a grid of challenge cards arranged in three columns on desktop within the same content width, each separated by generous horizontal and vertical gaps that create a calm, premium feel. When no results match, the grid is replaced by a centered, card-like empty state panel that occupies roughly half the width and floats in ample negative space. At the very bottom, pagination controls sit centered below the grid with extra breathing room above and below, giving the page a clear, stepped hierarchy from hero to controls to content to navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller occupies the topmost portion of the main content area, directly beneath the fixed global header, separated by roughly 64px of vertical space to establish it as a distinct hero band. It sits within a centered content container (max-width about 1140–1280px), aligned to the page’s left margin, and follows a two-column proportion inspired by the mockup: the controller’s text block spans approximately 55–60% of the container width (about 7 of 12 grid columns) on the left, while the remaining 40–45% of the width on the right stays mostly open for negative space or optional decorative imagery managed outside this component. Inside the left text block, a small, pill-shaped category label like “Challenges” appears at the top-left, followed by a large, multi-line page heading directly below it. The heading can comfortably use 2–3 lines without feeling cramped, with around 32–48px of internal top and bottom padding around the block. The vertical height of this hero section on desktop takes roughly 30–40% of the above-the-fold area, providing enough space for the text to breathe and sit roughly mid- to upper-vertical alignment relative to the viewport. On smaller screens, the controller expands to nearly full width (10–12 columns), with the right-side negative space collapsing; the pill remains at the top, and the large heading stacks beneath with consistent spacing, maintaining a clean editorial feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; it sits directly under the fixed global header and scrolls away with the page once the user moves down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is positioned immediately below the page controller hero area, separated by approximately 32px of vertical space to clearly distinguish the interaction zone from the introductory copy. It spans the full width of the same centered content container (all 12 grid columns) but remains relatively shallow in height, forming a horizontal control strip. Within this strip, the primary filter controls are arranged left-to-right: a search field or keyword input begins at the left edge and typically occupies about 40–50% of the bar’s width; to its right, one or two dropdowns or segmented controls for filtering by category, duration, or difficulty are aligned with consistent 16–24px gaps; on the far right, a sort dropdown or toggle aligns flush with the container’s right margin. Each control uses pill-like or lightly rounded shapes with 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal internal padding, matching the spacious interactive style observed in the mockup. The bar has 16–24px vertical padding inside, giving the controls breathing room, and casts a very subtle shadow when it becomes sticky to reinforce its separation from the content below. On mobile, the filter bar collapses into a stacked or two-row layout: search at full width on the first row, with key filter and sort controls on a second row, keeping a clear vertical rhythm and around 16px gaps between rows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the viewport: once the user scrolls past it, the filter bar pins itself just below the fixed global header, spanning the full container width and maintaining a subtle elevation, so filters remain accessible while the challenge list scrolls beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The active filter summary sits directly beneath the filter bar, aligned to the same left edge of the content container, with a modest 8–16px vertical gap between the bottom of the filter bar and the top of the summary. It spans roughly 70–80% of the container width on desktop, occupying about 8–9 of the 12 grid columns starting from the left, leaving a small portion of the right side visually open to maintain the airy feel. Within this band, active filters are represented as small, pill-shaped chips arranged in a horizontal row starting from the left and wrapping to additional rows as needed; each chip is separated by about 8–12px horizontally and vertically to avoid visual clutter. When there are few or no active filters, this area stays very low in height—just a single line of chips or a subtle “No filters applied” label—keeping the focus on the challenge list below. A more generous 24–32px gap separates the bottom of the active filter summary from the top edge of the challenge list, reinforcing the transition from filter controls to content. On smaller screens, the summary spans the full width and chips wrap naturally, with comfortable vertical spacing so the block remains easy to scan and tap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrolls with the page; it remains directly beneath the (potentially sticky) filter bar and does not become sticky itself, sliding out of view as users scroll into the challenge list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The challenge list forms the main content region and begins just below the active filter summary (or directly below the filter bar when no summary is present), with approximately 24–32px of vertical spacing to mark a clear transition from controls to results. It resides entirely within the same centered content container and uses a 12-column grid to arrange challenge cards in a spacious, card-based layout. On desktop, each challenge card occupies about 4 columns, resulting in a three-column grid (3 cards per row) with 24–32px horizontal gutters between cards and 32–40px vertical spacing between rows. The cards are aligned from left to right and top to bottom, so the user’s gaze naturally follows a Z-pattern across each row before moving down to the next. The list area itself extends vertically as long as needed, and it occupies the majority of the viewport below the fold, with the dark teal background visible between cards and along the outer margins to preserve the calm, premium aesthetic. Within each card (handled by the card component design), content is kept uncluttered with consistent internal padding and generous whitespace, further reinforcing the low-density layout. On tablets, the layout typically shifts to two cards per row (each 6 columns wide), and on mobile it becomes a single-column stack (cards spanning all 12 columns), while maintaining similar vertical spacing so that the grid still feels open and easy to browse.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrollable page; it fills the space beneath the filters and above pagination, expanding vertically as more content is loaded or paged.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Pagination controls are positioned at the bottom of the challenge list, separated from the last row of cards by a generous 48–64px vertical gap to give a clear sense of completion before navigation options appear. They are horizontally centered within the same content container and typically occupy the middle 40–60% of the width, leaving ample negative space on the left and right. The controls—page numbers, previous/next buttons, and possibly a compact page size toggle—are arranged in a single horizontal line with 8–16px gaps between elements, maintaining a low visual weight. The area itself is relatively shallow; the controls sit on a single baseline with around 16px vertical padding above and below to ensure they aren’t cramped. There is an additional 64–96px of bottom margin between the pagination and the site footer, reinforcing the spacious, editorial feel and preventing the pagination from feeling squeezed. On smaller screens, the control group may compress into a more minimal layout (previous/next plus a compact dropdown), but it remains centered with comfortable spacing around it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the main content; it scrolls into view only after the user has traversed the challenge list and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state display appears in place of the challenge list when no challenges match the current filters. It is anchored in the same vertical slot where the list would begin—just below the active filter summary or filter bar—with an enlarged 64–80px vertical gap above it to heighten the sense of openness and focus on the message. The empty state is presented as a centered, card-like panel within the content container, occupying roughly 50–60% of the container width on desktop (about 6–7 of 12 grid columns) and aligned horizontally to the center of the page. Inside the panel, an icon or subtle illustration sits at the top center, followed by a concise heading and a short explanatory line, and then a primary call-to-action button or link, all vertically stacked with 16–24px spacing between elements. The panel itself has generous internal padding (32–48px on all sides) and may use a slightly lighter teal or dark surface to differentiate it from the background while preserving the minimal aesthetic. Surrounding the panel, there is ample empty dark teal space above, below, and to the sides, reinforcing the idea of no results and maintaining the calm, uncluttered feel. On tablet and mobile, the panel scales down to occupy 80–90% of the container width, but it remains centered with the same vertical spacing rhythm, so it feels like a hero message rather than a secondary alert.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and mutually exclusive with the challenge list and pagination: when the empty state is visible, it replaces those components in the layout and scrolls normally within the main content area.

name: Challenge Detail
title: Challenge Detail
description: Displays full information for a specific challenge and lets users enroll with configurable reminder settings.

core_features: Load challenge details using getChallengeDetail(challengeId) based on the URL parameter id and display title, topic, description, duration, start date, and overview fields.
Show a structured overview of the challenge goals and daily activities using the Challenge model returned by getChallengeDetail.
Provide an enrollment form that collects name and email for the selected challenge and sends them to enrollInChallenge together with reminder settings.
Allow configuration of reminder settings (email on/off, SMS on/off, frequency none/daily/weekly, and a time of day) before submitting the enrollment.
Call enrollInChallenge(challengeId, name, email, reminderEmailEnabled, reminderSmsEnabled, reminderFrequency, reminderTime) on form submission and handle the response.
Display a clear success or error message after enrollment based on the enrollInChallenge response, including a summary of the user’s chosen reminder settings when available.
Provide navigation back to challenges.html via a 'Back to challenges' interaction after viewing or enrolling.
user_workflows: View challenge details from the challenge list: 1) User visits challenges.html and selects a challenge; 2) challenges.html navigates to challenge_detail.html?id={challengeId}; 3) Challenge Detail page reads id from the URL; 4) Page calls getChallengeDetail(challengeId=id); 5) While loading, a loading state is shown; 6) On success, challenge title, topic, duration_days, start_date, goals_overview, and daily_activities_overview are displayed; 7) User reviews details and can scroll to the enrollment form.

View challenge details from a direct link: 1) User opens a direct URL challenge_detail.html?id={challengeId}; 2) Page reads id; 3) Page calls getChallengeDetail with that id; 4) On success, the same detail view is shown as in the list workflow; 5) If id is missing or getChallengeDetail fails, an error message is shown with a link back to challenges.html.

Enroll in a challenge with custom reminders: 1) User is on challenge_detail.html?id={challengeId} with challenge details loaded; 2) User scrolls to the enrollment form; 3) User enters name and email; 4) User configures reminder settings: toggles email and/or SMS reminders, selects reminder_frequency (none, daily, weekly), and optionally selects a reminder_time; 5) User reviews a live summary of the chosen reminder settings; 6) User submits the form; 7) Page validates required fields (name, email, minimum valid reminder configuration if business rules apply); 8) If valid, page calls enrollInChallenge with the current challengeId and form values; 9) While waiting, a submitting/loading state is shown and the form is disabled; 10) On success, page shows a confirmation panel using the returned ChallengeEnrollment; 11) User can stay on the page, adjust their settings via a new submission (if allowed), or click 'Back to challenges' to return to challenges.html.

Handle enrollment errors and retry: 1) User submits the enrollment form; 2) enrollInChallenge returns success=false or the request fails; 3) Page displays an error notification explaining that enrollment could not be completed; 4) The form is re-enabled with previously entered values preserved; 5) User can adjust reminder settings or correct input data and submit again; 6) On a later success, the success confirmation is shown.

Navigate back to the list after viewing/enrolling: 1) At any time after challenge details load, the user can click 'Back to challenges'; 2) The page navigates to challenges.html (no additional parameters required, but can be extended if needed); 3) If the user enrolled, the navigation still works the same, leaving the confirmation state behind on this page.
interactions: On page load, read URL parameter id and use it to trigger a call to getChallengeDetail; show loading and error states depending on the outcome.
If id is missing or empty, do not call getChallengeDetail; instead show an error message and a 'Back to challenges' link.
User clicks 'Back to challenges' which triggers navigation to challenges.html.

User fills in the enrollment form fields (name, email) and changes reminder options (email on/off, SMS on/off, frequency, time), which updates local form state and a real-time summary of reminder settings.
User submits the enrollment form, triggering client-side validation; if validation passes, enrollInChallenge is called with the current challengeId and form values.
During enrollment submission, show a disabled state for the form and a loading indicator; prevent multiple submissions until a response is received.

On enrollInChallenge success, display a confirmation component populated from the returned enrollment object (ChallengeEnrollment), including status, reminder_email_enabled, reminder_sms_enabled, reminder_frequency, and reminder_time.
On enrollInChallenge failure, display an error notification, keep existing form input values, and allow the user to correct and resubmit.

Optionally update the URL (e.g., adding an enrollmentStatus parameter via history API) after successful enrollment to reflect that the user has just enrolled, and read that parameter on load to decide whether to initially emphasize the confirmation section.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state for this page is derived from URL parameters and API responses. On initial load, the page reads the URL parameter id (challenge_detail.html?id={challengeId}). If id is present and non-empty, it is used as challengeId when calling getChallengeDetail. If id is missing or invalid, the page does not call getChallengeDetail and instead shows an error state with navigation back to challenges.html. The challengeId from the URL is treated as the single source of truth for which Challenge to display; if the user navigates to a different challenge via a new URL (changing id), the page must re-run getChallengeDetail with the new id and reset enrollment-related transient state. Enrollment does not change the URL by default; it updates only in-page state based on the enrollInChallenge response. Optionally, after a successful enrollment, the page can update or append a URL parameter such as enrollmentStatus=success using client-side navigation utilities; when loading, if enrollmentStatus=success is present, the page can choose to immediately show or scroll to the confirmation section. No persistent state is stored in localStorage or similar; all persistent data comes from the Challenge and ChallengeEnrollment data returned by getChallengeDetail and enrollInChallenge, and from the id parameter in the URL.

components.functionality: Reads the id parameter from the current page URL, validates it, and triggers getChallengeDetail(challengeId=id) to fetch the Challenge data needed for all detail components. Manages loading, success, and error states and provides the loaded Challenge to its child components.
Displays high-level Challenge information such as title, topic, duration_days, start_date, and end_date (if available) to give users a quick overview of what the challenge is and when it runs.

Shows the main descriptive fields of the Challenge, including description, goals_overview, and daily_activities_overview, allowing the user to understand the purpose of the challenge and what daily participation looks like.

Summarizes the schedule-related aspects of the Challenge, such as duration_days and start_date (and end_date if provided), and can calculate or display any derived schedule details needed for understanding when activities occur.

Collects user input required for enrolling in the current Challenge, including name, email, and reminder settings (reminder_email_enabled, reminder_sms_enabled, reminder_frequency, reminder_time). Performs basic validation (e.g., required name and email) and, on submission, calls enrollInChallenge with the challengeId from URLParam.id and the current form values. Manages submission state and exposes the enrollInChallenge response to other components.
