Displays a live summary of the reminder settings currently selected in the enrollment form (email/SMS enabled flags, reminder_frequency, and reminder_time), so users can confirm their configuration before submitting.

Shows loading, success, or error messages related to the enrollment submission. On success, it can briefly indicate that enrollment is confirmed before the more detailed confirmation panel. On failure, it provides feedback and prompts the user to try again.

After a successful enrollInChallenge call (success=true), displays key details from the returned ChallengeEnrollment object, such as status, reminder_email_enabled, reminder_sms_enabled, reminder_frequency, reminder_time, and enrolled_at. Also outlines what the user can expect next (e.g., receiving reminder communications according to the selected settings).
If getChallengeDetail fails or URLParam.id is missing/invalid, displays an error message explaining that the challenge could not be loaded and offers navigation back to the challenges list.
Provides a simple way for the user to return to the challenges listing page. When clicked, navigates to challenges.html without relying on stored state.

Optionally reads and writes URL parameters related to enrollment state (e.g., enrollmentStatus) so that the page can reflect that an enrollment just occurred, without using localStorage. After a successful enrollment, it can update the URL to include enrollmentStatus=success; on load, if such a parameter is present, it can instruct the page to prioritize showing the confirmation panel.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a top-to-bottom flow anchored by a fixed global header (logo/nav/actions) and a full-width, above-the-fold hero for the challenge. Within the hero, the challenge-header behaves like the blog hero in the mockup: a left-aligned text column (category pill + large title + key meta) and a right column for a large visual (photo/illustration) contained within the same component. Below the hero, place a full-width challenge-schedule-summary as a slim editorial card, then a secondary content band split into two columns: challenge-description-section in the wider left column and all enrollment-related components (form, status, confirmation, reminder summary) stacked in the narrower right column. Navigation and system-level components (back link, loader, error banners, URL state handler) sit logically above or around these main blocks without crowding the hero.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a top-to-bottom flow anchored by a fixed global header (logo/nav/actions) and a full-width, above-the-fold hero for the challenge. Within the hero, the challenge-header behaves like the blog hero in the mockup: a left-aligned text column (category pill + large title + key meta) and a right column for a large visual (photo/illustration) contained within the same component. Below the hero, place a full-width challenge-schedule-summary as a slim editorial card, then a secondary content band split into two columns: challenge-description-section in the wider left column and all enrollment-related components (form, status, confirmation, reminder summary) stacked in the narrower right column. Navigation and system-level components (back link, loader, error banners, URL state handler) sit logically above or around these main blocks without crowding the hero.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Respect the mockup’s proportions: global header at the top as a relatively shallow fixed bar (~10–15% of viewport height), then a hero that fills most of the above-the-fold height. Within the hero, allocate roughly 58–60% of the content width to the left text column and 40–42% to the right visual column, within a centered max-width container (~1140–1280px) with generous left/right gutters. Below the hero, the schedule summary spans roughly 80–90% of the container width. The lower two-column band places the description in a ~60% left column and the enrollment stack in a ~40% right column. Vertical gaps between major sections (back link → hero, hero → schedule, schedule → description/enrollment band) follow a 48–64px rhythm, while internal padding for cards and forms uses 24–32px on all sides.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low-density, editorial feel: large headings, comfortable line-height, and ample whitespace around every group. Each major card (header, schedule summary, enrollment form, confirmation, reminder summary) has clear breathing room from the next via 24–48px vertical spacing. Text blocks avoid crowding; paragraphs in the description use generous line spacing and 16–18px body text. Only essential elements are placed in the hero (category, title, key challenge meta, single visual); enrollment actions and details are visually lowered into the next section to avoid above-the-fold clutter while still remaining highly visible. Notification banners are slim and contained near the components they refer to, not stretching unnecessarily or competing with the hero.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The flow mirrors the F/Z pattern from the mockup: the eye starts at the top-left (global logo, then back-to-challenges-link just below), travels across the top navigation to the right, then drops diagonally down to the left hero column where the category pill and bold challenge title command attention. From there, the gaze moves horizontally to the right hero visual, then continues down into the schedule summary strip. Scrolling slightly, the eye is pulled again to the left for the detailed description, then back across to the right column where the enrollment form and related panels form a vertical decision stack. Notification banners appear directly above the content they relate to, creating a clear, local cause-and-effect visual relationship without interrupting the overall horizontal reading pattern.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a calm dark teal canvas with a fixed, full-width SaaS-style header at the very top. Below it, the main content is centered in a wide but bounded container, with a subtle back-to-challenges link at the top-left and plenty of space before the main hero. The hero itself stretches across most of the above-the-fold area, divided into a wider left column featuring a small category pill and a large, multi-line challenge title, and a slightly narrower right column holding a high-quality rectangular photo or illustration related to the challenge. Beneath this, a single, understated schedule summary card spans most of the width, acting as a bridge into the main body. Scrolling reveals a spacious two-column layout: a rich, readable description on the left and a tidy vertical stack of enrollment-related UI on the right – status banner, enrollment form, confirmation, and reminder summary – all housed in light, card-like surfaces. System states like loading and errors appear as overlays or banners near the top, but the page consistently preserves negative space and a simple hierarchy, resulting in a premium, editorial, and confident feel that matches the original mockup’s DNA.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, this loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay that covers the entire scrollable content area beneath the fixed global header, starting just below the header’s bottom edge and extending to the bottom of the viewport. It lightly dims the hero, description, and enrollment sections while keeping their basic shapes still faintly visible, maintaining the sense of overall layout. A centered loading indicator (spinner or progress element) sits in the visual middle of the viewport, horizontally aligned with the center of the main content container. The overlay uses generous padding around the spinner so it feels calm and uncluttered in the otherwise dark teal environment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a full-screen overlay anchored below the fixed header; positioned fixed or absolute relative to the viewport so it remains centered and blocks interaction with underlying components while loading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Positioned near the top of the page content, immediately below the back-to-challenges-link, this component occupies the full width of the central content container and forms the main hero region. Inside it, content is split into two columns: the left column spans roughly 58–60% of the container width and begins near the top with a small category/status pill (e.g., “Challenge”), followed by a large, multi-line heading and brief meta text such as duration or difficulty. The right column spans about 40–42% of the width and is aligned so its top edge roughly lines up with the upper half of the heading, housing a large, vertically-oriented rectangular hero image or visual block. Generous padding (32–48px) around the inner content ensures ample negative space between text and image, and the entire header block visually dominates the above-the-fold area while still leaving dark teal margins at the sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; defines the hero area and scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This section appears below the challenge-schedule-summary, aligned to the left side of the content container as the main narrative block for the challenge. It occupies roughly 60% of the container width on larger screens, aligning vertically with the left edge of the challenge-header’s text column to maintain a consistent column rhythm. There is a generous vertical gap (48–64px) above it, separating it clearly from the schedule summary, and a comfortable margin to the right to distinguish it from the enrollment column. Within this block, headings, paragraphs, and any lists are laid out with ample line spacing and 16–18px body text, forming a visually light, editorial column that can extend downward as needed without feeling dense.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the left column of the lower content band; on narrow viewports it stacks above the enrollment content and stretches closer to full width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The schedule summary is placed directly beneath the challenge-header, acting as a horizontal bridge between the hero and the detailed content below. It is centered within the main content container and spans approximately 80–90% of that width, with equal left and right margins, creating the sense of a sleek, self-contained card. Vertically, it sits with about 32–48px of whitespace above and below, separating it from the hero and the description/enrollment band. Inside, it likely presents a compact row of key schedule facts (start date, duration, frequency) laid out left-to-right, with ample horizontal padding (24–32px) and generous line spacing so it remains easy to scan and visually light.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width card within the main content column; it scrolls normally and visually anchors the transition between hero and body content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The enrollment form sits in the right column of the lower content band, roughly opposite the challenge-description-section. On larger screens it occupies about 40% of the main content container width and is aligned so its left edge lines up with the right edge of the description column, maintaining a clear two-column structure. There is a generous vertical gap (48–64px) between the bottom of the schedule summary and the top of this form, mirroring the spacing above the description. The form itself is styled as a clean, card-like panel with 24–32px internal padding, clear vertical stacking of fields, and a prominent primary action button anchored near the bottom, with ample spacing between fields to keep the density low and calm. It does not compete with the hero visually but becomes the focal point of the right side once the user scrolls slightly past the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; on smaller viewports it drops below the description, stretching closer to full width while maintaining generous internal padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This summary appears in the same right-hand column as the enrollment form, but vertically below it, forming part of a clear enrollment-related stack. It is presented as a smaller card or panel spanning the full width of the right column, with a modest height and 24px internal padding to keep its contents airy. A vertical gap of about 24–32px separates it from the form above (or from the confirmation panel if the form is replaced), signaling that it is secondary supporting information. The layout within the panel uses short lines of text and perhaps small badges or icons to show selected reminder options, arranged in one or two concise rows that are easy to scan without crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly beneath the enrollment section in the right column; on smaller viewports it follows underneath the form/confirmation, maintaining consistent card spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component appears as a slim notification banner directly associated with the enrollment form. It spans the full width of the right column and is placed immediately above the challenge-enrollment-form card, with only a small vertical gap (8–16px) between the banner and the top edge of the form. Its height is relatively shallow compared to other cards, ensuring it does not overwhelm the layout; text is left-aligned within the banner with ample horizontal padding, and any status icon sits to the far left for quick recognition. The banner’s color and subtle border give it enough contrast to be noticed, but it remains visually subordinate to the form and hero, in line with the low-density aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the right column flow; appears or disappears dynamically without shifting into fixed or overlay positions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful enrollment, this panel appears in the same spatial slot as the enrollment form, leveraging users’ focus on that area. It occupies the full width of the right column, with similar card styling and internal padding (24–32px) as the form, creating continuity. In many cases it will either replace the form entirely or sit directly above a minimized form state, with 24–32px of spacing to any components above (such as a success status banner) and below (such as the reminder-settings-summary). The content is vertically structured with a bold confirmation title, a short explanatory paragraph, and possibly a concise list of next steps, all laid out in a calm, spacious manner so the success state feels clear and reassuring without visual clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the right column, occupying the same visual territory as the enrollment form; appears after submission and scrolls with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When a challenge fails to load, this panel appears near the top of the main content area, directly beneath the back-to-challenges-link and above where the challenge-header would normally render. It spans nearly the full width of the central content container (roughly 90–100%), acting as a strong horizontal band that draws immediate attention. The panel uses generous vertical padding (24–32px) and clear, left-aligned messaging so it is easy to read, with sufficient margin beneath it (32–48px) before any fallback or alternative content. In an error state, the usual hero and body sections may be hidden or visually deemphasized, allowing this panel to become the dominant element in that vertical slice of the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline, anchored near the top of the content flow; it scrolls normally and does not overlay content, but may conditionally replace or suppress other components when active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This navigation link is positioned at the very top-left of the page’s content area, just below the fixed global header. It sits within the main content container, aligned with the left edge of the hero’s text column and offset from the top of the viewport by a comfortable margin (~24–32px below the header’s bottom edge). The link is relatively small and understated compared to the hero title, with ample surrounding whitespace above and below so it feels like a light breadcrumb rather than a competing heading. A small arrow or icon may precede the text, but the whole element remains compact, occupying only a narrow horizontal slice at the top.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; scrolls offscreen as the user moves down the page and does not remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component has no direct visual representation on the page. It operates behind the scenes to interpret URL parameters and adjust the initial state of enrollment-related UI, such as auto-expanding the enrollment form, pre-selecting reminder options, or scrolling the user to the right column. Any visual effect it causes manifests through other components (primarily the enrollment form, confirmation panel, and reminder summary) without introducing its own shapes, borders, or space in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it does not occupy layout space or affect the grid directly, but may trigger scroll-to behaviors that position visual components within the viewport.

name: Assessments & Tools
title: Assessments & Tools
description: Browse, filter, and sort self-assessment tools by category and question count, then open a selected assessment’s detail page to begin.

core_features: Load available categories, question count ranges, and sort options using getAssessmentFilterOptions to build dynamic filters.
Initialize filters and sorting from incoming URL parameters (category, minQuestions, maxQuestions, sortBy) when the page loads.
Search and list assessments using searchAssessments with the current filters, sortBy, page, and pageSize values.
Display assessments in a list where each entry shows key information from the Assessment model (such as title, category, and question_count).
Support filtering by category and question count range, with changes reflected both in the URL parameters and in subsequent searchAssessments calls.
Support sorting (e.g., by question count or popularity) using sort options returned from getAssessmentFilterOptions and reflected in URL parameters.
Provide pagination for assessment results using the page and pageSize arguments of searchAssessments, with the current page reflected in the URL.
Allow users to clear or adjust individual filters (category, question range) and re-run searchAssessments with updated filters.
Enable navigation to assessment_detail.html with the assessmentId of the selected assessment when a user clicks an assessment entry.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Initial page load without pre-set filters
1) User navigates to assessments.html without query parameters.
2) The page calls getAssessmentFilterOptions to retrieve available categories, question count range limits, and sort options.
3) The page determines that category, minQuestions, maxQuestions, and sortBy are not present in the URL, so it initializes an unfiltered state (no category filter, full question range, no explicit sort).
4) The page calls searchAssessments with filters reflecting this unfiltered state, a default sortBy choice if needed, and an initial page index and pageSize defined by the front-end configuration.
5) The returned results and totalCount are bound to the assessment list and pagination components for display.

Workflow 2: Initial page load with pre-applied filters from another page
1) User clicks a link on index.html that opens assessments.html with URL parameters such as category, minQuestions, maxQuestions, and/or sortBy.
2) On load, the page parses these URL parameters and stores them as the current filter and sorting state.
3) The page calls getAssessmentFilterOptions to obtain valid categories, question count range limits, and sort options.
4) Using the returned filter options, the page validates and applies the URL parameter values where possible (for example, using only category values that exist in the categories list).
5) The page calls searchAssessments with a filters object constructed from the URL parameters (category, minQuestions, maxQuestions), along with sortBy, page (defaulting to the first page if not present), and pageSize.
6) The assessment list is rendered using the searchAssessments results, and the filter controls display the pre-applied filter values so the user sees the context of the listing.

Workflow 3: User updates filters and sorting on the page
1) User interacts with the filter panel to select or change a category, adjust the minimum or maximum question count, or choose a sort option.
2) When the user confirms the filter change (e.g., by changing a control or submitting the filters), the filter component emits an event carrying the new filter and sort values.
3) The controller updates the URL parameters (category, minQuestions, maxQuestions, sortBy) to reflect the new state and resets the page parameter to the first page.
4) The controller constructs a filters object based on the updated state and calls searchAssessments with filters, sortBy, page set to the first page, and the configured pageSize.
5) The assessment list and pagination components are updated to reflect the new search results, while the applied filter summary component shows the active filters.

Workflow 4: User navigates through result pages
1) After viewing the first set of assessments, the user clicks a pagination control to move to a different page (e.g., next, previous, or a specific page number).
2) The pagination component emits an onPageChange event with the new page index.
3) The controller updates the page parameter in the URL query string and keeps the current category, minQuestions, maxQuestions, and sortBy parameters unchanged.
4) The controller calls searchAssessments again with the existing filters and sortBy, the new page index, and the existing pageSize.
5) The assessment list updates to show the results for the chosen page, and the pagination component reflects the new current page.
6) If the user refreshes the browser or shares the URL, the same page of filtered, sorted results is restored based on the URL parameters.

Workflow 5: User opens an assessment detail page
1) From the assessment list, the user clicks on an assessment entry (e.g., a row or card).
2) The list component emits an onAssessmentClick event containing the id of the selected Assessment entity.
3) The page navigates to assessment_detail.html with a query parameter assessmentId set to the selected assessment’s id (assessment_detail.html?assessmentId=<id>).
4) The assessment detail page (not part of this specification) uses the assessmentId to load and display the assessment so the user can start it.
interactions: On page initialization (onInit), the controller parses the current URL to read category, minQuestions, maxQuestions, sortBy, and an optional page parameter, and stores them as the initial state.
Immediately after parsing the URL, the controller calls getAssessmentFilterOptions and binds its response to the filter panel and sort control so only valid categories and sort options are available.

Once filter options are loaded, the controller normalizes the current filter state (e.g., ensures minQuestions and maxQuestions are within the allowed range, and category and sortBy match available options) before executing the first call to searchAssessments.

When the user changes the category selection in the filter panel (onCategoryChange), the filter component emits the new category value, which updates the URL’s category parameter and triggers a new searchAssessments call with updated filters.

When the user adjusts the question count range (onQuestionRangeChange), the filter component emits updated minQuestions and maxQuestions values, which update the corresponding URL parameters and cause searchAssessments to be called with the new range filter.

When the user selects a different sort option (onSortChange), the sort control emits the new sortBy value; the controller updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and calls searchAssessments with the existing filters and the new sortBy.

When the user clears a specific filter (e.g., removes a category or range via the applied-filters summary component’s onFilterChipRemove event), the controller removes the corresponding URL parameter and calls searchAssessments again without that filter.

When the user clicks a pagination control (onPageChange), the pagination component emits the target page index; the controller updates the page parameter in the URL and calls searchAssessments with unchanged filters and sortBy.

When searchAssessments is invoked, a loading state is set; the result list and pagination components are updated once results and totalCount are returned, and an empty-state message is shown if totalCount is zero.

When the user clicks an assessment entry in the list (onAssessmentClick), the list component triggers navigation to assessment_detail.html with assessmentId set to the clicked Assessment.id in the query string.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses the URL query string as the single source of truth for the current filter, sort, and pagination state. On load, it reads category, minQuestions, maxQuestions, sortBy, and optionally page from the URL. If any of these parameters are absent, the page treats them as unset (no category filter, no restricted range beyond the global limits from getAssessmentFilterOptions, no explicit sortBy, and a default first page). After retrieving filter limits and options via getAssessmentFilterOptions, the page validates any existing URL parameter values (for example, confirming that the category matches one of the available categories and that minQuestions and maxQuestions fall within the allowed questionCountRange). If a value is invalid, the page may normalize it or omit it from subsequent searchAssessments calls. Any user interaction that changes filters or sorting causes the corresponding URL parameters to be updated or removed, always resetting the page parameter to represent the first page of results after a filter change. Pagination interactions update only the page parameter, preserving all filter-related parameters. Each time the URL state changes through these interactions, the controller constructs a filters object that mirrors the URL (with fields such as category, minQuestions, and maxQuestions) and calls searchAssessments with filters, the current sortBy value, the current page index, and a configured pageSize. This ensures that reloading the page or sharing its URL recreates the same filter, sort, and pagination state without relying on localStorage or other client-side persistence.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization, URL state management, filter and sort updates, pagination, and API calls to getAssessmentFilterOptions and searchAssessments. It parses URL parameters, maintains the current filter/sort/page state in memory, updates the URL when state changes, and triggers data fetching to refresh the assessment list.

Handles reading and writing URL query parameters related to assessments filters and pagination. It exposes methods to read category, minQuestions, maxQuestions, sortBy, and page from the current URL and to push updated parameters to the URL (e.g., via history APIs) whenever filter, sort, or pagination changes.

Displays and manages interactive controls for filtering assessments by category and question count range, and optionally for applying filter changes as a group. It receives available categories and question count bounds from getAssessmentFilterOptions via the controller, and displays the current filter values based on URL-derived state.

Provides a selector for the sortBy option when listing assessments. It receives the list of valid sort options from getAssessmentFilterOptions and the currently active sortBy value from the controller. When the user changes the selection, it notifies the controller to update the sortBy parameter in the URL and re-run searchAssessments.

Shows a concise summary of currently active filters (such as selected category or non-default question count range). It allows the user to remove individual filters directly, which updates the corresponding URL parameters and triggers a new searchAssessments call via the controller.

Renders the array of Assessment results returned from searchAssessments as clickable entries. Each entry binds to properties such as id, title, category, question_count, and estimated_time_minutes without hardcoding specific values. Clicking an entry triggers navigation to the assessment_detail.html page with the selected assessmentId.

Displays pagination controls based on totalCount from searchAssessments and the configured pageSize. It shows the current page (derived from the URL’s page parameter) and lets users navigate between pages. When the user selects a different page, it updates the page parameter in the URL and requests new results via the controller.

Displays the current status of the search operation, such as loading, error, or empty results. It listens for events when searchAssessments is called and when results or errors return. For empty result sets (totalCount equal to zero), it shows a message and can prompt the user to adjust filters.

Ensures that the page does not call searchAssessments until getAssessmentFilterOptions has completed, so that the filter and sort state can be validated against the returned options first. Once filter options are ready and the URL state has been normalized, it triggers the initial searchAssessments call.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: a full-width fixed site header (logo, nav, primary CTA) sits at the very top, followed by a full-width hero band for the 'Assessments & Tools' page intro (title and short description on the left, supporting illustration or imagery on the right). Below this hero, the functional browsing area begins: first a horizontal control zone containing filters and sorting, then a light-weight summary and status row, and finally the list of assessments with pagination at the bottom. All interactive content sits within a centered max-width container on a dark teal background, using a 12-column grid with left-aligned text and components.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom flow as the mockup: a full-width fixed site header (logo, nav, primary CTA) sits at the very top, followed by a full-width hero band for the 'Assessments & Tools' page intro (title and short description on the left, supporting illustration or imagery on the right). Below this hero, the functional browsing area begins: first a horizontal control zone containing filters and sorting, then a light-weight summary and status row, and finally the list of assessments with pagination at the bottom. All interactive content sits within a centered max-width container on a dark teal background, using a 12-column grid with left-aligned text and components.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header consumes a slim band (about 10–15% of the viewport height) and stays fixed. The hero occupies the majority of the above-the-fold area, with roughly 55–60% of the inner width for the left text column and 40–45% for the right image, mirroring the mockup. The assessments browsing area below the hero is contained within the same max-width (around 1140–1280px) and uses spacious vertical spacing: approximately 48–64px above and below the filter/sort controls, 24–32px between control rows, and 32–48px between the list and pagination. Within the controls row, the filter panel takes about two-thirds of the width and the sort control about one-third, maintaining a clear hierarchy. The assessment list then occupies nearly the full content width, with generous side gutters on the dark background.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally low-density, echoing the mockup’s calm SaaS editorial feel. Controls are arranged in a single or at most two horizontal bands with clear breathing room: wide margins around the entire control area, ample spacing between filters, and uncluttered labels. The assessment list uses cards or rows with large typography, comfortable line spacing, and 24–32px padding per item. Only essential elements are visible at a glance: filters, sort, applied filter summary, search status, list entries, and pagination. There are no sidebars or crowded toolbars; instead, we rely on whitespace and alignment to convey structure.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow begins at the top-left logo, moves across the header navigation to the top-right CTA, then drops down to the hero’s left-aligned page title, following the same Z-pattern as the mockup. From the hero, the eye travels down to the filter bar on the left side of the content area, then sweeps across to the sort control on the right, forming a secondary horizontal scan line. From there, attention moves vertically down through the applied filters summary and search status (center-left) into the assessment list. The list items are the main focus within the browsing area, with pagination centered at the bottom as a clear end-cap. The left alignment of text and controls keeps the experience grounded and easy to scan in an F-shaped reading pattern through the list.

layout.overall_description: Within a full-width dark teal canvas and a fixed global header, the 'Assessments & Tools' page opens with a broad hero band where the page title and brief description sit in a wide left column and a single, polished image or illustration balances on the right, echoing the original blog hero structure. Beneath this, the main content snaps into a centered, max-width container aligned to an invisible 12-column grid. A spacious horizontal control strip appears first: filter options cluster starting from the top-left of the content area while the sort dropdown anchors to the top-right, all with generous padding and minimal chrome. Just below, a low-key row shows which filters are applied and the current search status, aligned left and given ample breathing room so it reads almost like editorial metadata rather than a busy toolbar. The assessment list then stretches across the grid with clean, card-like rows, each separated by comfortable vertical gaps to maintain the calm, premium feel. At the very bottom, pagination controls sit centered with ample top and bottom padding, acting as a visual footer to the list. Throughout, the dominant impressions are of left-aligned structure, wide margins, and a quiet, confident UI that feels like a modern SaaS interface applied to life coaching content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual orchestration component that sits logically above the page’s main content area, coordinating data fetching, filters, sorting, and pagination. It does not render any visible UI and therefore occupies no explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, you can imagine it as sitting behind the entire assessments section, spanning the same central max-width region but with zero height and no visual footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical controller with no rendered DOM; it does not affect or consume any layout space and moves with the page scroll only as a background process.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component manages URL query parameters and state persistence for filters, sorting, and pagination. It is not represented visually on the page. It conceptually overlays the whole assessments content region but remains invisible, with no width, height, or margin. It simply ensures that the visible filter, sort, and list components reflect and update the URL state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, stateful service with no physical layout presence; it does not scroll or occupy space and only influences other components’ configuration.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The assessment filter panel forms the left side of the primary control strip immediately below the hero section and within the central content container. It is aligned flush with the container’s left edge, starting about 64px beneath the hero’s bottom content edge to maintain the generous vertical rhythm. On desktop, it spans roughly 7–8 of the 12 grid columns (about 60–65% of the content width), leaving room on the right for the sort control. Filter elements (e.g., category dropdown, question-count range, tags) are arranged horizontally from left to right, with 16–24px gaps between controls and 8–12px internal padding within each pill or dropdown, following the component padding guidelines. The panel’s vertical height is modest, about the size of a single comfortable row of form controls, with 16–24px padding above and below within the control band. On smaller screens, the filter panel stacks above the sort control and expands to nearly 100% width, while retaining generous left and right padding inside the container.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the scrolling page content. On large screens it aligns horizontally with the sort control; on narrow viewports it flows above the sort control in the normal document flow without sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control occupies the right side of the same horizontal control strip as the filter panel. It is right-aligned within the content container, anchored to the container’s right edge with a clear visual separation from the filters on the left. On desktop, it spans roughly 3–4 of the 12 grid columns (about 30–35% of width), appearing as a compact dropdown or segmented control with its label aligned to the right edge. There is a comfortable horizontal gap of around 24–32px between the last filter element on the left and the sort control on the right to prevent crowding. Vertically, it is centered relative to the filter controls, so the entire control bar reads as a single, balanced band. On smaller viewports, the sort control drops below the filter panel, becomes left-aligned, and spans almost the full width of the container, but still keeps generous padding above (16–24px) to remain visually distinct.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page. It shares a single row with the filter panel on wide screens and reflows beneath it in the normal document flow on smaller breakpoints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied filters summary sits directly below the primary control strip, within the same central content container, creating a subtle metadata row. It is left-aligned with the filter panel and starts approximately 16–24px beneath the bottom edge of the filter/sort row, maintaining the low-density feel. The summary spans about 8–9 grid columns (roughly 65–75% of the content width) but visually feels light—small, unobtrusive text with optional pill-like tags representing active filters. These tags are arranged in a horizontal line with 8px gaps between them. The right side of this row intentionally remains mostly empty dark teal space, reinforcing the airy layout and helping the summary read as supportive, not dominant. On smaller screens, the summary stretches to full width but retains generous side margins and vertical whitespace so it never feels cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element that sits inline below the filter/sort controls and above the list. It scrolls normally with the content and does not use sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The assessment list is the main content block and sits directly below the applied filters summary within the central container. It begins about 32px beneath the summary to create a clear visual separation between controls/meta and content results. The list spans nearly the full width of the grid—around 10–11 of 12 columns—while leaving consistent left and right gutters (approximately 40–64px total horizontal padding from the viewport edge, depending on screen size). Each assessment item appears as a card or row with ample internal padding (24–32px on all sides), large heading typography, and comfortable line spacing, echoing the clean editorial feel. Vertical gaps of 24–32px separate each item, ensuring the list remains easy to scan without feeling dense. On larger screens, cards align in a single vertical column to preserve focus and avoid clutter; on smaller screens, they still stack vertically but fill more of the available width. The top of the list visually aligns with the left edge of the filter panel and applied filter summary, reinforcing a strong left-hand reading line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and scrollable as the main content body. It grows vertically with the number of items and pushes the pagination controls downward. No sticky behavior; users scroll through it in a natural, linear fashion.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination controls are positioned at the very bottom of the assessments browsing area, directly beneath the assessment list. They are centered horizontally within the content container, starting roughly 32–48px below the last list item to mark a clear end to the content. The pagination row itself is low-profile, with a horizontally arranged series of page numbers and next/previous controls, each component padded by about 8–12px vertically and 16–24px horizontally. There is generous whitespace above and below the pagination row (24–32px each) so it feels like a calm, deliberate footer to the list rather than cramped navigation. The left and right sides of the row remain mostly empty teal space, emphasizing the central alignment. On small screens, controls can wrap but remain visually centered and comfortably spaced.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the bottom of the list that scrolls into view as users reach the end. It does not stick to the bottom of the viewport; instead, it serves as a natural terminus to the page of results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search status indicator is a subtle, text-based element that communicates result counts and loading or empty states. It sits just above the assessment list and to the right of the applied filters summary, sharing the same horizontal band. Specifically, it aligns with the content container’s right side, beginning on the same baseline as the applied filters summary but justified to the right, occupying roughly 3–4 grid columns (about 25–35% width). The line height and font size are modest, ensuring it feels like secondary metadata. There is a small horizontal gap (16–24px) between the right edge of the summary’s last element and the status indicator. On narrow screens, the status indicator drops onto its own line beneath the applied filters summary, left-aligned, with about 8–16px vertical spacing so the block still feels light and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline with the summary/list transition. It scrolls with the page and does not float or stick; its vertical position adjusts as content above (filters, summary) grows or shrinks.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The initialization gate controls when the assessments content becomes visible (e.g., showing a brief loading or empty state while data is fetched). It conceptually wraps the entire assessments content region—from the filter/sort controls down through the pagination—but has minimal or no persistent visual representation once initialization is complete. During loading, it may temporarily center a simple spinner or status message within the content container, vertically offset slightly below the hero (e.g., 96–128px from the hero’s bottom), maintaining the same centered width and generous surrounding whitespace seen elsewhere on the page. After initialization, it collapses to an invisible wrapper with no spatial footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a conditional container: during initialization it occupies the main content area below the hero with centered feedback; once complete, it relinquishes that space to the filter, list, and pagination components and no longer affects layout.

name: Assessment Detail
title: Assessment Detail
description: Run through a selected self-assessment, capture responses, compute and display results, and optionally save them to the Dashboard. Also supports reopening and reviewing completed assessment instances.

core_features: Load either an assessment definition for a new run (via assessmentId) or a completed assessment instance for review (via instanceId).
Display assessment introduction details (title, description, question count, estimated time) with an action to start the assessment.
Create a new assessment instance when the user starts, using startAssessment, and keep track of the instanceId during the session.
Guide the user through all assessment questions one by one (or in sequence), capturing responses for scale and multiple-choice question types.
Submit responses to completeAssessment, receive the calculated score and result summary, and display a completion view.
Allow the user to save the completed assessment instance to their Dashboard via saveAssessmentResultToDashboard.
Provide navigation actions to go back to the list of assessments or to the Dashboard assessments section after saving.
When opened with instanceId, display a read-only view of the completed assessment: overall result, responses per question (if questions are retrieved), and saved-to-dashboard state.
user_workflows:
Start a new assessment from assessments.html:
1) User clicks an assessment from assessments.html, which navigates to assessment_detail.html?assessmentId={assessmentId}.
2) Page loads assessment details and questions using getAssessmentDetail(assessmentId).
3) User reads the introduction and clicks 'Start assessment'.
4) Page calls startAssessment(assessmentId) and stores the returned AssessmentInstance.id as the active instanceId.
5) Question runner is shown; user answers each question and navigates through them.
6) User clicks 'Complete assessment'; page calls completeAssessment(instanceId, responses).
7) On success, page shows score and result summary from the returned instance.
8) User clicks 'Save to Dashboard', triggering saveAssessmentResultToDashboard(instanceId).
9) After saving, user can click 'Go to Dashboard' to navigate to dashboard.html?section=assessments or 'Back to assessments' to go to assessments.html.

Reopen and review a saved assessment from Dashboard:
1) User clicks a saved assessment in dashboard.html, which opens assessment_detail.html?instanceId={assessmentInstanceId}.
2) Page calls getAssessmentResultDetail(assessmentInstanceId) to load the AssessmentInstance and associated Assessment.
3) If detailed questions are needed, page uses the assessmentId from the instance or returned assessment to call getAssessmentDetail(assessmentId) and retrieve questions.
4) Page displays the assessment title, completion timestamp, score, and result_summary in a read-only result view.
5) Page shows responses per question (if questions are available), without allowing edits or resubmission.
6) If the instance is not yet saved_to_dashboard, user may click 'Save to Dashboard' to call saveAssessmentResultToDashboard(assessmentInstanceId); otherwise this control is disabled or hidden.
7) User can navigate back to dashboard.html?section=assessments or to assessments.html.

Complete an in-session assessment and immediately save results:
1) User is mid-assessment with an active instanceId created by startAssessment.
2) User navigates through questions; responses are stored locally as they are given.
3) When the last question is answered, user clicks 'Complete assessment'.
4) Page validates that responses exist for all questions, then calls completeAssessment(instanceId, responses).
5) On success, the returned instance with score and result_summary is bound to the results view.
6) User clicks 'Save to Dashboard', which calls saveAssessmentResultToDashboard(instanceId) and updates the instance.saved_to_dashboard flag in page state.
7) Page updates the UI to indicate the assessment is saved and offers 'Go to Dashboard' navigation.

Handle invalid or missing URL parameters:
1) If the page loads without assessmentId and instanceId, it sets an error state and displays a functional error message with a button to return to assessments.html.
2) If getAssessmentDetail or getAssessmentResultDetail fail (e.g., invalid ID), the page shows a load-error state; the assessment start/question/result components are not rendered.
3) From this state, user can use 'Back to assessments' to navigate to assessments.html.
interactions: On page load, parse URL parameters to determine whether to load by assessmentId (new run) or instanceId (existing result) and call the appropriate interface(s).
Click 'Start assessment' to create a new AssessmentInstance via startAssessment and transition from the intro view to the question runner view.
For each question, user selects a scale value or one or more options depending on response_type; input changes are stored in local response state keyed by questionId.
Click 'Next' or 'Previous' within the question runner to move between questions while preserving already-entered responses.
Click 'Complete assessment' to trigger validation of responses and call completeAssessment with assessmentInstanceId and the collected responses array.
On successful completion, display the result summary and score from the returned AssessmentInstance and hide or lock the question inputs.
Click 'Save to Dashboard' to call saveAssessmentResultToDashboard; on success, update the UI to reflect that saved_to_dashboard is true and reveal the 'Go to Dashboard' action.
Click 'Go to Dashboard' to navigate to dashboard.html?section=assessments using standard link navigation.
Click 'Back to assessments' (available from intro, in-progress, and results views) to navigate back to assessments.html.
When opened with instanceId, render a read-only result view: no question editing, no submission; only optional saving (if not already saved) and navigation controls.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is determined solely by URL parameters and in-memory state during the session, without using localStorage.

1) Initial mode selection:
- On load, the page inspects the URL for query parameters.
- If `instanceId` is present and non-empty:
  - Set mode = 'viewInstance'.
  - Call getAssessmentResultDetail(instanceId) to retrieve `{ instance, assessment }`.
  - If the page needs question texts for displaying responses, it uses instance.assessmentId (or the assessment.id returned) to call getAssessmentDetail(assessmentId) and retrieve questions.
  - Sub-mode = 'resultReview' (read-only view).
- Else if `assessmentId` is present and non-empty:
  - Set mode = 'newAssessment'.
  - Call getAssessmentDetail(assessmentId) to retrieve `{ assessment, questions }`.
  - Sub-mode = 'intro' until the user starts the assessment.
- Else (no valid parameters):
  - Set mode = 'errorNoId'; display an error component and only show navigation back to assessments.html.

2) New assessment lifecycle (mode = 'newAssessment'):
- Sub-mode 'intro': assessment details loaded, no AssessmentInstance yet; 'Start assessment' enabled.
- On 'Start assessment':
  - Call startAssessment(assessmentId).
  - On success, store instanceId = returned AssessmentInstance.id and full instance object in state.
  - Initialize an in-memory responses array, typically shaped according to AssessmentInstance.responses (questionId + answer value), but not yet sent to the server.
  - Sub-mode transitions to 'inProgress'.
- Sub-mode 'inProgress':
  - CurrentQuestionIndex stored in memory to track which question is shown.
  - As the user answers questions, update the local responses array while remaining in 'inProgress'.
  - 'Complete assessment' triggers completeAssessment(instanceId, responses).
- On completeAssessment success:
  - State.instance is replaced/updated with the returned instance (including score, result_summary, completed_at, and saved_to_dashboard).
  - Sub-mode transitions to 'completed'.

3) Completed assessment state (mode = 'viewInstance' or sub-mode 'completed'):
- In both reopened and newly-completed flows, the page uses the AssessmentInstance from either getAssessmentResultDetail or completeAssessment response.
- The result display reads instance.score, instance.result_summary, and instance.completed_at (if present) to drive content.
- The control for saving to Dashboard reads instance.saved_to_dashboard:
  - If false: show an enabled 'Save to Dashboard' button.
  - If true: show as disabled or replaced by a status indicator.
- After saveAssessmentResultToDashboard returns, the instance state is updated to reflect saved_to_dashboard = true and UI updates accordingly.

4) Navigation and URL-driven state transitions:
- 'Go to Dashboard' always performs a navigation to `dashboard.html?section=assessments` and does not rely on existing page state after navigation.
- 'Back to assessments' navigates to `assessments.html` without parameters.
- Refreshing the page preserves only what can be re-derived from URL parameters:
  - If refreshed with assessmentId while mid-assessment (inProgress instance not encoded in the URL), the page will treat it as a new intro state because a previous unsaved instanceId is not persisted in the URL or storage.
  - If refreshed with instanceId, the page will reload the completed instance via getAssessmentResultDetail and display the result view.

components.functionality: Determines the initial mode based on URL parameters (assessmentId or instanceId), calls the appropriate interfaces to load data, and exposes loaded assessment, questions, and instance to child components.
Displays assessment metadata (title, description, question_count, estimated_time_minutes) when starting a new assessment and presents a 'Start assessment' action.
Handles creation of a new AssessmentInstance when the user starts an assessment by calling startAssessment(assessmentId) and storing the returned instanceId and initial instance state.
Guides the user through all AssessmentQuestions for a new run, manages the current question index, collects and updates local responses, and triggers assessment completion.

Coordinates submission of responses to completeAssessment using the active assessmentInstanceId and the collected responses array, then updates the instance state with the returned score and result_summary.
Shows the completed assessment's overall score, result_summary, and completion status for both newly completed and reopened instances.
Displays a read-only list of questions alongside the user's recorded responses from AssessmentInstance.responses for completed assessments, when questions are available.
Allows the user to save a completed assessment instance to the Dashboard using saveAssessmentResultToDashboard and updates UI based on instance.saved_to_dashboard.
Provides navigation links to go back to the list of assessments or to open the Dashboard assessments section after saving.
Handles and presents error states such as missing URL parameters or failed data-loading calls, and offers a way back to assessments.html.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom flow like the mockup: global header at the very top, then a full-width hero-like introduction for the assessment (intro panel as text on the left, supporting visual on the right). Below the hero, the main functional area is organized as a two-column workspace: the left column (primary) holds the active content (questions, results, response review), while the right column (secondary) holds supporting controls (save, navigation, completion). Loader and error states sit above this structure, either replacing or overlaying the main content area, while navigation and save controls stay adjacent to the primary content rather than competing with it.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a linear top-to-bottom flow like the mockup: global header at the very top, then a full-width hero-like introduction for the assessment (intro panel as text on the left, supporting visual on the right). Below the hero, the main functional area is organized as a two-column workspace: the left column (primary) holds the active content (questions, results, response review), while the right column (secondary) holds supporting controls (save, navigation, completion). Loader and error states sit above this structure, either replacing or overlaying the main content area, while navigation and save controls stay adjacent to the primary content rather than competing with it.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header consumes a shallow band at the top (~10–15% of viewport height). The hero section with the assessment intro uses most of the above-the-fold space, with roughly 55–60% width for the intro text (left) and 40–45% for a hero image or visual (right) within a centered content container (~1140–1280px max width). Below the hero, the two-column assessment workspace continues the same proportional split: the left column spans ~60% of the container width and the right column ~40%, with generous vertical spacing (48–64px) between major blocks (intro, runner, results, review) and 16–32px between sub-elements. Ample dark teal background is left visible around these blocks to preserve the premium, airy feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is intentionally sparse and airy: large headings, clear section breaks, and single primary actions per area. Each major component (intro, runner, results, review) appears as a distinct, generously padded card or block with plenty of whitespace around it, avoiding any sense of a crowded dashboard. Controls (save, navigation, completion) are grouped and visually compact but not crammed, with comfortable padding (around 12px vertical, 16–24px horizontal) and clear separation between control groups. Only the information needed for the current step is visible, minimizing cognitive load.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern: the eye starts at the logo and navigation in the header, moves to the assessment category pill and large title on the top-left of the hero, then across to the hero image on the right. From there, the eye is guided downward to the primary action in the intro (start or resume the assessment), then into the main left-column content (questions or results). Secondary attention is pulled to the right column where utility controls (save, progress, completion) are grouped, often vertically and optionally sticky, so the user can easily find them without them overshadowing the primary content. Subsequent sections (results and review) reuse the same left-dominant layout, reinforcing a predictable scan pattern.

layout.overall_description: The Assessment Detail page sits on a dark teal background with a slim, full-width header at the top containing the logo on the left, navigation toward the center-left, and a subtle account/CTA area on the right. Below it, a spacious hero-like section introduces the selected assessment: on the left, a small category pill (e.g., “Assessment”) sits above a bold, multi-line assessment title and short description, all left-aligned within a wide text column; on the right, a tall rectangular image or illustration aligns roughly with the upper half of the heading, occupying the narrower column. This hero section nearly fills the initial viewport, creating a calm, editorial feel with generous margins and breathing room around all content. Directly beneath the intro text, a clear “Start” or “Continue assessment” block leads into the functional area. As you scroll slightly, the main assessment workspace appears, still within the centered content container, organized into a 60/40 two-column split: the left column hosts the active step (questions, results, or review) in large, card-like panels, while the right column stacks compact control panels for saving, navigating, and completing the assessment, optionally staying in view as you scroll the left column. Loader and error states overlay or replace the central workspace without disrupting the header, and after completion, the hero intro remains as context while the left column swaps from questions to a clean result summary and then an optional detailed response review, all following the same spacious, modern SaaS aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay centered over the main content area, starting just below the global header and extending down to cover the hero and workspace sections within the viewport. It slightly dims the dark teal background and underlying components while keeping the header visible, reinforcing that the user is still within the same page context. In the visual center of the viewport (horizontally centered within the main content width and vertically centered between header and bottom edge), a simple, refined loading indicator and short status text are displayed. The overlay respects the site’s generous spacing, leaving a comfortable margin between the loader content and viewport edges, so it feels intentional and calm rather than intrusive.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a fixed overlay positioned relative to the viewport, covering the main content below the header while active; scroll is typically disabled beneath it until loading completes.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The intro panel is the primary content in the hero section, placed in the left column of a centered two-column layout beneath the header. It spans roughly 55–60% of the content container width and is aligned to the left edge of that container, with large left and right margins separating it from the viewport edges. At the top of this panel, a small pill-shaped label (e.g., “Assessment” or assessment category) sits with generous space above (around 32–48px from the bottom of the header) and below, followed by a large, multi-line assessment title that dominates the left side visually, then a brief descriptive paragraph. The panel’s vertical height aligns roughly with the hero image’s mid-to-upper region on the right, leaving breathing room below for the initializer CTA and creating a clear block of content that feels substantial yet uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; on scroll, it moves normally with the page and defines the top-left anchor for the rest of the assessment content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The instance initializer sits directly beneath the introductory text within the same left hero column, visually separated by a comfortable vertical gap of around 24–32px. It appears as a concise but prominent horizontal band or card containing the main call-to-action (e.g., “Start assessment” or “Resume where you left off”), aligned left within the column but not spanning its full width, so there is visible dark teal space to the right that echoes the hero’s airy feel. On larger screens, the initializer’s baseline roughly aligns with the lower third of the hero image on the right, reinforcing the two-column relationship. Its compact, button-centric design ensures it stands out after reading the intro while remaining subordinate to the main heading in visual hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored under the intro text; when activated, it transitions the user’s focus downward into the main assessment workspace without changing its own position abruptly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The question runner lives in the main content area immediately below the hero section, occupying the left column of a two-column layout that continues the hero’s 60/40 split. It spans roughly 60% of the centered content width, aligned with the left edge of the intro panel above, creating a tall, card-like block with ample internal padding (around 32px on all sides) and a clear top heading or step indicator, followed by the current question, options/inputs, and any helper text. There is generous vertical space (48–64px) separating the bottom of the initializer from the top of the question runner, so it feels like the next section in a linear journey rather than part of the hero. As the user progresses, the content within this left column card updates, while its outer dimensions and alignment remain consistent, preserving a stable reading area that dominates the central portion of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main scroll flow; on longer assessments, the question runner extends downward, with the right column controls potentially remaining visible via their own sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The completion controller is positioned just below the main question content within the left column, inside the same general card region, appearing once the user reaches the final step or is eligible to submit. It is aligned to the left edge of the question area, with a moderate vertical gap (16–24px) above it to clearly separate it from the last question or instructions. It may present a concise summary line (e.g., “You’ve answered all questions”) and a prominent primary button (e.g., “View results”), sized similarly to the initializer button but visually contextualized as part of the current assessment flow. When results are available, this controller either collapses or shifts slightly downward, maintaining spacing so the transition to the result summary feels smooth rather than abrupt.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the left column’s content stack; it enters and exits the layout based on assessment state, without affecting the surrounding section structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After submission, the result summary replaces the active question content in the left column, occupying the same 60% width and aligning flush with the same left margin used by the intro and question runner. The top of this block appears just below the hero section (once the user scrolls to it), with a bold result heading echoing the scale and weight of the original assessment title, followed by key metrics or scores arranged in a spacious, card-like layout with generous padding and clear separation between result elements. There is ample whitespace above and below each result segment, maintaining a calm, editorial feel that invites reflection rather than data overload. The right column controls remain adjacent, now contextualized around saving or bookmarking results and navigating to review responses.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block in the left column; it effectively swaps into the same spatial slot previously used by the question runner, minimizing layout shift.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The response review panel is placed directly beneath the result summary within the left column, separated by a comfortable vertical gap of about 32–48px to mark it as a new, optional deep-dive section. It spans the same 60% column width and presents past questions and responses in a vertically stacked format, with each question group taking the form of a lightly outlined or subtly separated sub-card. Generous spacing (16–24px) is used between questions, and line lengths are kept within the main text column width to ensure readability. This section can extend significantly downward for longer assessments, with the right column staying comparatively shorter or sticky, reinforcing the left side as the primary reading lane.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the main flow; as the user scrolls through their responses, the right column may remain partially in view depending on viewport height and sticky settings.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save controls occupy the upper portion of the right column in the main assessment workspace, starting just below the top edge of the question runner’s vertical alignment and spanning approximately 40–45% of the content container width. They appear as a compact, vertically stacked block or small card with internal padding and one or two clearly labeled actions (e.g., “Save progress”, “Save to Dashboard”), aligned to the right edge of the content container. There is a generous gap (32–48px) between the right edge of the left column and the left edge of this controls card, ensuring the layout never feels cramped. This block remains visually subordinate to the large content area on the left but is always easy to spot due to its consistent placement at the top of the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, the save controls card can be sticky within the right column, maintaining its proximity to the top of the viewport as the user scrolls through the left column; on smaller viewports, it behaves as a normal block stacked above navigation and completion controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation controls are positioned in the right column, directly beneath the save controls, with a modest vertical gap (16–24px) to distinguish the two control groups. They are arranged horizontally within a compact, right-aligned area: typically a row of buttons such as “Previous”, “Next”, or “Skip”, constrained within the width of the right column so they do not stretch across the page. On larger screens, their vertical center roughly aligns with the mid-height of the currently visible question in the left column, reinforcing the sense of lateral adjacency between content and controls. The buttons follow the same padded, minimalist style used throughout, avoiding overcrowding while ensuring clear tap targets.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Grouped within the right column, optionally part of a sticky vertical stack with save controls so that horizontal navigation remains accessible as the user scrolls through questions; on small screens, they may collapse below the question runner but still maintain a consistent gap from primary content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state replaces the main assessment workspace area when triggered, occupying the central portion of the page below the header and hero, or—in the case of a load failure—appearing where the hero and workspace would normally be. It is centered within the main content width, spanning roughly 50–60% of the container, with substantial padding (32–48px) and generous vertical whitespace above and below so the message feels calm and controlled rather than alarming. The error icon, heading, and explanation are left-aligned within this block, with a single primary action (e.g., “Retry”) positioned beneath, aligned with the text column. Surrounding dark teal background and empty space to the left and right reinforce the minimalist, premium feel even in failure states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that appears in place of normal content; it pushes other components (like intro or workspace) out of view or below, ensuring the user’s focus is solely on resolving the error.

name: Dashboard
title: Dashboard
description: Central page for viewing and navigating saved assessments, custom coaching plans, and worksheets, with quick access to details and progress summaries.

core_features: Load a consolidated dashboard overview using getDashboardOverview, including saved assessments, custom plans, worksheets, and aggregate stats.
Display high-level stats showing counts of saved assessments, plans, and worksheets from the stats object.
Provide internal navigation (tabs or section controls) between the Assessments, Plans, and Worksheets sections, controlled by the section URL parameter when present.
List saved assessment instances that have been saved_to_dashboard, with actions to open the full result in assessment_detail.html and to view a quick preview using getAssessmentResultDetail.
List saved custom plans with actions to open each plan’s detailed view in custom_plan.html.
List saved worksheets/resources with actions to open each worksheet’s detail in worksheet_detail.html.
Handle empty states per section when no saved items exist, based solely on the data returned by getDashboardOverview.
Handle loading and error states for API calls so users understand when data is being fetched or if something went wrong.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Load dashboard overview
1. User navigates to dashboard.html (optionally with a section query parameter).
2. On page initialization, the page parses the section query parameter (if present) to determine the active section/tab.
3. The page calls getDashboardOverview once to retrieve savedAssessments, savedPlans, savedWorksheets, and stats.
4. While loading, the page shows a loading indicator for the entire dashboard content area.
5. On a successful response, the dashboard summary stats and the three lists (assessments, plans, worksheets) are populated and filtered per active section.
6. If the request fails, an error message with a retry action is shown instead of the lists.

Workflow: Navigate directly to a specific section using URL parameter
1. User opens dashboard.html with a query parameter, e.g., dashboard.html?section=assessments.
2. The page reads the section value and sets the Assessments tab as active on initial render.
3. The getDashboardOverview data is fetched once.
4. The assessments list is shown as the primary visible section; other sections are accessible via tab controls.
5. If section does not match a known value, the page defaults to a predefined section (e.g., assessments) without using any hardcoded content data.

Workflow: Switch between sections via internal navigation
1. User clicks on a section tab (Assessments, Plans, or Worksheets).
2. The active section state is updated in memory and the UI switches to show the selected section’s list.
3. The section query parameter in the URL is updated (e.g., ?section=plans) via navigation or history manipulation so that the selected section is preserved on refresh or when the link is shared.
4. No additional API calls are required for this switch because the data was already loaded from getDashboardOverview.

Workflow: Open a saved assessment detail page
1. In the Assessments section, user locates a saved assessment entry.
2. User clicks the primary action on the assessment entry (e.g., the card itself or an "Open" control).
3. The page navigates to assessment_detail.html with the instanceId set to the AssessmentInstance.id of the selected item (assessment_detail.html?instanceId={assessmentInstanceId}).
4. The assessment_detail.html page handles further data loading; the dashboard does not make any additional calls for that navigation.

Workflow: Quick preview of an assessment from the dashboard
1. In the Assessments section, user clicks a secondary action (e.g., "Quick preview") on a saved assessment entry.
2. The page calls getAssessmentResultDetail with assessmentInstanceId equal to the selected AssessmentInstance.id.
3. While the preview data is loading, a local loading state is shown in the preview panel or modal.
4. On success, the preview component displays summary information from the instance (e.g., score, result_summary, dates) and details from the assessment definition (e.g., title, category) returned by getAssessmentResultDetail.
5. On error, an inline error state is displayed within the preview panel with an option to retry the preview load.

Workflow: Open a saved custom plan
1. In the Plans section, user views the list of saved custom plans, each showing at least the plan name, total_cost, and timestamps derived from the CustomPlan fields.
2. User clicks a plan entry.
3. The page navigates to custom_plan.html with the planId URL parameter set to the CustomPlan.id (custom_plan.html?planId={customPlanId}).
4. The destination page handles further logic; the dashboard does not perform additional plan-related API calls.

Workflow: Open a saved worksheet/resource
1. In the Worksheets section, user views a list where each item combines SavedWorksheet data with the associated Worksheet data returned from getDashboardOverview.
2. User clicks a worksheet card or "Open" action.
3. The page navigates to worksheet_detail.html with the id URL parameter set to the Worksheet.id (worksheet_detail.html?id={worksheetId}).
4. The worksheet_detail.html page is responsible for displaying full worksheet details or downloads.

Workflow: Handle empty states per section
1. After getDashboardOverview returns, the page checks lengths of savedAssessments, savedPlans, and savedWorksheets arrays.
2. If the currently active section has an empty array, the section-specific list component displays an empty-state message or guidance (no static example content, only generic messaging driven by the fact that the list is empty).
3. The tabs remain available so users can switch to other sections that may contain data.

Workflow: Retry after error
1. If getDashboardOverview fails, an error component is displayed with a retry control.
2. User clicks the retry button.
3. The same getDashboardOverview call is made again.
4. On success, the dashboard data is rendered; on failure, the error remains visible with the possibility of further retries.
5. Similarly, if getAssessmentResultDetail fails during a quick preview, the preview component offers a local retry button that re-calls getAssessmentResultDetail for the same assessmentInstanceId.
interactions: Click on section tabs (Assessments, Plans, Worksheets) to switch the visible section and update the section URL parameter.
Click on an assessment entry card or its primary action to navigate to assessment_detail.html with the corresponding instanceId parameter.
Click on an assessment entry’s quick preview action to load and display additional assessment details via getAssessmentResultDetail in a dedicated preview component.
Click on a custom plan entry to navigate to custom_plan.html with the corresponding planId parameter.
Click on a worksheet entry to navigate to worksheet_detail.html with the corresponding worksheetId parameter.
Click a global or section-level refresh/retry action to call getDashboardOverview again after an error or when the user wants updated data.
View-only interactions with summary statistics: the stats component reads values from the stats object returned by getDashboardOverview and updates automatically when data changes.
Close or dismiss the assessment quick preview panel or modal to return focus to the list, without affecting the URL parameters.

If present, interact with sorting or simple filtering controls within a section (e.g., sort assessments by completed_at) that operate purely on the in-memory arrays returned by getDashboardOverview, without introducing any static data.
page_functionality.state_logic:
