Save a custom plan with a user-defined name and the currently selected items using saveCustomPlan, and expose a navigation action to go to the Dashboard plans section (dashboard.html?section=plans) after successful save.
When a planId URL parameter is present, load the full details of the saved custom plan (including resolved items) using getCustomPlanDetail and present it in a detail/edit mode.
List the user’s saved custom plans in a sidebar or secondary section using getCustomPlans and allow switching between plans by updating the planId URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Build a new custom plan from scratch
1. User navigates to custom_plan.html without a planId parameter.
2. Page-mode router detects absence of planId and sets mode to "builder-new".
3. Builder loader component calls getCustomPlanBuilderOptions to retrieve available individualSessions, groupWorkshops, and pricingGuidance.
4. The page displays initial lists of individual sessions and group workshops and a blank working plan summary (no items, zero total cost).
5. User optionally adjusts filters for individual sessions (e.g., maximum price per session) and submits the filter form, which updates relevant URL parameters and triggers searchIndividualSessions.
6. The individual sessions list updates to show the filtered results; each entry offers an "Add" action that adds the selected item to the working plan state and updates the running total.
7. User optionally adjusts workshop filters and submits them, which updates URL parameters and triggers searchGroupWorkshops; the workshops list updates accordingly and supports "Add" actions added to the working plan.
8. User defines plan constraints (e.g., desired counts of session types and a maximum total cost) in the constraints form. These constraint values are stored in in-page state and may be encoded into URL parameters.
9. The plan summary component continuously recomputes counts and total cost and compares them against the constraints, displaying validation feedback (e.g., whether constraints are met or which ones are not met yet).
10. When satisfied, the user opens the save plan form, enters a plan name, and submits.
11. The save form composes the items payload (with item_type and the appropriate individualSessionId or groupWorkshopId) from the working plan state and calls saveCustomPlan.
12. On success, the page receives the saved CustomPlan object, updates internal state, optionally updates the URL with the returned plan.id as planId, and shows a confirmation with an option to "Go to My Plans".
13. If the user clicks "Go to My Plans", the page navigates to dashboard.html?section=plans.

Workflow 2: Open an existing plan from the Dashboard in detail mode
1. User clicks a saved plan from dashboard.html, which navigates to custom_plan.html with a planId URL parameter.
2. Page-mode router reads planId from the URL and sets mode to "builder-detail".
3. The plan-detail loader component calls getCustomPlanDetail with planId.
4. When the response arrives, the page binds the returned plan and items to the working plan state and displays the plan name and all line items (sessions and workshops) in the plan summary.
5. In parallel, the builder loader may call getCustomPlanBuilderOptions to provide a full catalog of available sessions/workshops and pricingGuidance to support adjustments.
6. The user reviews the plan details, including computed total_cost from the CustomPlan and/or a recomputed total from items.
7. The user may optionally set or adjust plan constraints (independent of what was originally saved) and see whether the existing plan satisfies those constraints.
8. The user can add additional sessions or workshops from the listings (using the same add actions as in a new plan) or remove items from the plan summary; the working plan state updates and may diverge from the originally saved plan.
9. The user saves changes with a new or updated plan name via the save plan form, which calls saveCustomPlan with the current items. On success, the page may update planId in the URL to the new plan.id.
10. The user may navigate away using browser navigation or "Go to My Plans" to return to dashboard.html?section=plans.

Workflow 3: Switch between saved plans from within the Custom Plan Builder page
1. User opens custom_plan.html (with or without an initial planId).
2. The saved-plans sidebar component calls getCustomPlans to retrieve all CustomPlan records.
3. The sidebar lists plan names as navigable items; the current plan (if any) is indicated based on the planId URL parameter.
4. When the user clicks a different saved plan in the sidebar, the component updates the URL to custom_plan.html?planId=<selectedId> (preserving or resetting other relevant parameters as defined).
5. The page-mode router detects the planId change, triggers the plan-detail loader, and calls getCustomPlanDetail for the selected planId.
6. The main builder area rebinds to the new plan’s details and items; the working plan state is reset to match this newly selected saved plan.
7. The user may repeat this process to quickly compare, adjust, and save different plans.

Workflow 4: Apply and persist filters for sessions and workshops via URL parameters
1. User is on custom_plan.html, viewing individual sessions and group workshops.
2. User sets a maximum price for individual sessions in the individual-session filters form and submits.
3. The filters form updates the URL query string with a dedicated parameter (e.g., maxSessionPrice) and any other filter or sortBy parameters required by searchIndividualSessions.
4. The filters form then calls searchIndividualSessions with the current filters and sortBy values derived from form inputs and/or URL parameters.
5. The individual sessions list updates to show the new results; the working plan state remains unchanged.
6. If the user reloads the page or shares the URL, the initialisation logic re-reads those parameters and re-applies them by populating the filter form defaults and re-running searchIndividualSessions so that the filtered view is restored.
7. The same pattern applies independently for group workshops via the workshop filters form and searchGroupWorkshops, using their own URL parameters (e.g., maxWorkshopPrice, workshopSort).

Workflow 5: Validate a plan against user-defined constraints
1. User is building or editing a plan and opens the plan constraints form.
2. The user specifies desired counts of individual sessions and group workshops and a maximum total cost (and optionally whether counts are exact, minimum, or maximum, if supported by the form design).
3. On form change or submit, the constraint values are stored in working state and optionally reflected as URL parameters (e.g., targetSessions, targetWorkshops, maxTotalCost) so they can be restored on reload or share.
4. The plan summary component listens to changes in both working plan items and constraint values.
5. Whenever an item is added or removed, or constraints are changed, the summary recomputes:
   - Total number of items by type (individual sessions vs group workshops).
   - Aggregate total cost by summing the price fields from the current working items.
6. The summary evaluates each constraint and marks them as met or unmet; for example, it may indicate if the required number of sessions has not yet been reached or if the maximum total cost has been exceeded.
7. This validation feedback is updated in real time as the user continues to adjust the plan, guiding them toward a configuration that satisfies all constraints before saving.
interactions: Changing URL parameter planId directly (e.g., by navigation from the Dashboard or via internal links) triggers a switch between new-plan mode and detail/edit mode, which in turn triggers getCustomPlanDetail when planId is present.
Page load and certain URL parameter changes trigger getCustomPlanBuilderOptions to refresh available individual sessions, group workshops, and pricingGuidance.

Submitting the individual-session filters form triggers URL query updates (e.g., maxSessionPrice, sessionSort) and a call to searchIndividualSessions with the derived filters and sortBy, updating the individual session list.

Submitting the workshop filters form triggers URL query updates (e.g., maxWorkshopPrice, workshopSort) and a call to searchGroupWorkshops with the derived filters and sortBy, updating the workshop list.

Clicking an "Add" control on an individual session list item adds that session to the working plan’s items collection (as an unsaved CustomPlanItem-like structure), updates the running total cost, and re-runs constraints validation.
Clicking an "Add" control on a workshop list item adds that workshop to the working plan’s items collection, updates the running total cost, and re-runs constraints validation.
Clicking a "Remove" control for an item in the plan summary removes that item from the working plan state, recalculates counts and total cost, and re-runs constraints validation.
Editing constraint fields in the constraints form (e.g., changing counts or maximum total cost) updates constraint state, may update URL parameters, and triggers a re-validation of the current plan.

Submitting the save plan form triggers validation (e.g., ensuring a plan name is provided and, if configured, that constraints are met or the user acknowledges unmet constraints), then calls saveCustomPlan with the plan name and items derived from the working plan state.

Receiving a successful response from saveCustomPlan updates the working plan’s reference planId to the returned plan.id, updates the URL (e.g., adding or replacing the planId query parameter), and enables the "Go to My Plans" navigation action.
Clicking "Go to My Plans" after saving navigates the user to dashboard.html?section=plans using a direct URL change.
The saved-plans sidebar uses click interactions on list items to update the planId URL parameter, which triggers re-loading of that plan’s details without the user manually reloading the page.
A reset or "Start New Plan" interaction clears the planId parameter from the URL, resets working plan items and constraints to their default empty state, and reloads builder catalog data if needed.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state coordination is handled via URL parameters combined with in-memory working state.

1. planId parameter
- If planId is present in the URL (e.g., custom_plan.html?planId=<id>):
  - The page mode is set to a "detail/edit" mode for an existing plan.
  - On initial load or whenever planId changes, getCustomPlanDetail is called with the new planId.
  - The response's plan and items are mapped to the working plan state (current plan name, items collection, and total cost), effectively overwriting any prior working plan.
- If planId is absent:
  - The page mode is set to "new plan" builder mode.
  - No getCustomPlanDetail call is made.
  - Working plan state starts empty and is populated only through user actions (adding items).

2. Filter-related parameters
- Independent URL parameters (names to be defined consistently, such as maxSessionPrice, sessionSort, maxWorkshopPrice, workshopSort) represent current filters and sorts for individual sessions and group workshops.
- On page load:
  - The filter form components read existing filter parameters to pre-populate input controls.
  - If any relevant filter parameters exist, the corresponding search interface (searchIndividualSessions or searchGroupWorkshops) is called immediately with filters derived from these parameters.
  - If no filter parameters exist, initial lists may be populated from getCustomPlanBuilderOptions.
- On filter form submission:
  - The components update the URL query string with the current filter values (adding, replacing, or removing parameters as needed).
  - Then they call the appropriate search interface to refresh the lists.

3. Constraint-related parameters
- Optional URL parameters (e.g., targetSessions, targetWorkshops, maxTotalCost, and potentially flags specifying whether the counts are exact/min/max) may be used to persist and restore constraint settings.
- On page load:
  - The constraints form reads these parameters and populates its fields.
  - The constraints evaluator uses them immediately against any existing working plan items (from a loaded plan or an empty new plan).
- On constraint form change:
  - Internal constraint state is updated immediately.
  - The URL query string may be updated to reflect new constraints.
  - The plan summary component re-evaluates constraint satisfaction.

4. Other view parameters
- Additional URL parameters (for example, a tab parameter to switch between a "builder" view and a "plan detail" or "summary" sub-view) can control which sections are currently visible. These parameters do not affect data loading directly but control which components are rendered or focused.

5. In-memory working state (not in URL)
- The current working plan items (unsaved CustomPlanItem-like structures), running total cost, and transient UI states (such as which rows are expanded) are kept in memory only; they are not persisted via URL.
- When planId changes, in-memory working state is reset to reflect the newly loaded plan.
- When planId is removed (e.g., user starts a new plan), the in-memory state resets to an empty plan.

No state is persisted using localStorage or similar browser storage; URL parameters and API responses are the only persistence/restore mechanisms.

components.functionality: Determines whether the page is in new-plan builder mode or existing-plan detail/edit mode based on the presence of the planId URL parameter, and orchestrates initial data loading sequences.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Read planId from URL; if present, set mode to 'detail' and trigger plan-detail-loader; if absent, set mode to 'new'.
onUrlParamChange(planId): When planId changes, reset working plan state and trigger plan-detail-loader if planId exists, or reset to new-plan mode if it is removed.

components.functionality: Loads the base catalog of available individual sessions and group workshops, and pricing guidance for filters and constraints, via getCustomPlanBuilderOptions.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Call getCustomPlanBuilderOptions with no parameters and store individualSessions, groupWorkshops, and pricingGuidance in builder state.
onManualRefresh: User-triggered action to re-call getCustomPlanBuilderOptions (e.g., if catalog may have changed).
onApiSuccess(getCustomPlanBuilderOptions): Provide data to individual-session-list, workshop-list, and pricing-guidance-panel components.

components.functionality: When planId is present, loads full details of the saved plan, including resolved session and workshop items, via getCustomPlanDetail, and maps them to the working plan state.
event_handlers: onPlanIdAvailable: Call getCustomPlanDetail(planId) when router sets mode to 'detail'.
onApiSuccess(getCustomPlanDetail): Map response.plan to current plan metadata (name, total_cost) and response.items to the working collection of plan items used by plan-summary and other components.
onApiError(getCustomPlanDetail): Clear working plan state and optionally signal an error state to the user (e.g., plan not found).

components.functionality: Displays dynamic pricing guidance (e.g., suggested maximum prices for sessions and workshops) received from getCustomPlanBuilderOptions and optionally offers quick-apply actions to set filter values or constraints.
event_handlers: onDataUpdate(pricingGuidance): When builder-initial-loader updates pricingGuidance, refresh the displayed guidance and expose quick-apply controls.

onClickApplyToFilters: When the user clicks a quick-apply control, propagate suggested values into the filters forms (individual-session-filters-form and workshop-filters-form) and optionally update the URL.

components.functionality: Collects filter and sort criteria for individual sessions (e.g., maximum price per session, topic, sort order), persists them in the URL, and triggers a filtered search via searchIndividualSessions.
event_handlers: onInit: Read existing session-related filter parameters from the URL (such as maxSessionPrice and sessionSort) and populate form fields.

onSubmit: Build a filters object (including max price and any other criteria) and sortBy value from form inputs; update corresponding URL parameters; call searchIndividualSessions({ filters, sortBy }).
onApiSuccess(searchIndividualSessions): Forward results to individual-session-list for display.
onResetFilters: Clear relevant filter parameters from the URL and optionally re-display base results from getCustomPlanBuilderOptions.

components.functionality: Collects filter and sort criteria for group workshops (e.g., maximum workshop price, topic, format, sort order), persists them in the URL, and triggers a filtered search via searchGroupWorkshops.
event_handlers: onInit: Read existing workshop-related filter parameters from the URL (such as maxWorkshopPrice and workshopSort) and populate form fields.
onSubmit: Build a filters object and sortBy value from form inputs; update corresponding URL parameters; call searchGroupWorkshops({ filters, sortBy }).
onApiSuccess(searchGroupWorkshops): Forward results to workshop-list for display.
onResetFilters: Clear relevant workshop filter parameters from the URL and optionally re-display base workshop results from getCustomPlanBuilderOptions.

components.functionality: Displays a list of available individual session offerings, sourced either from getCustomPlanBuilderOptions or from searchIndividualSessions results, with actions to add items to the working plan.
event_handlers: onDataSourceUpdate: When new sessions are received from either builder-initial-loader or searchIndividualSessions, update the list items.

onClickAddSession(session): Create an in-memory plan item (with item_type set to 'individual_session' and individualSessionId populated) and add it to the working plan's items collection; trigger recalculation of the plan’s running total and constraints validation.
onClickViewDetails(session): Optional interaction that may expand or show additional fields from IndividualSessionOffering in-place without changing data.

components.functionality: Displays a list of available group workshops, sourced either from getCustomPlanBuilderOptions or from searchGroupWorkshops results, with actions to add items to the working plan.
event_handlers: onDataSourceUpdate: When new workshops are received from either builder-initial-loader or searchGroupWorkshops, update the list items.

onClickAddWorkshop(workshop): Create an in-memory plan item (with item_type set to 'group_workshop' and groupWorkshopId populated) and add it to the working plan's items collection; trigger recalculation of the plan’s running total and constraints validation.
onClickViewDetails(workshop): Optional interaction that may expand or show additional fields from GroupWorkshop in-place without changing data.

components.functionality: Allows users to define constraints for their plan, such as required counts of individual sessions and group workshops and a maximum total cost, and stores these for ongoing validation.
event_handlers: onInit: Read any constraint-related URL parameters (e.g., targetSessions, targetWorkshops, maxTotalCost, and constraint mode flags if used) to pre-populate inputs.
onChange: Update in-memory constraints state whenever a field is changed; optionally update URL parameters to match the new constraint values.
onSubmit: Confirm that constraint inputs are valid (e.g., numeric) and finalize the constraints used by the plan-validation logic in plan-summary-panel.
onReset: Clear constraint fields and remove the corresponding URL parameters, reverting to an unconstrained state.

components.functionality: Shows the current working plan: list of selected items (sessions and workshops), total counts by item type, computed total cost, and validation feedback against constraints and (optionally) the saved plan’s total_cost.
event_handlers: onWorkingPlanUpdate: Whenever items in the working plan change (additions/removals or when a plan is loaded), recompute item counts grouped by item_type and recalculate total cost based on item prices.
onConstraintsUpdate: Whenever constraint values change, re-run validation logic and update the displayed status (e.g., indicate which constraints are met or unmet).
onClickRemoveItem(planItem): Remove the item from the working plan state and re-run computations.

onClickViewUnderlyingItem(planItem): If needed, map planItem to its underlying IndividualSessionOffering or GroupWorkshop (from current lists or from getCustomPlanDetail data) for richer detail display.
onPlanDetailLoad(plan, items): Bind the loaded plan’s name and items from getCustomPlanDetail into the summary to reflect the currently selected saved plan.

components.functionality: Handles naming and saving the current working plan as a CustomPlan via saveCustomPlan, then updates URL state and exposes navigation to the Dashboard plans section.
event_handlers: onInit: If a plan is loaded via planId, pre-populate the plan name field with the loaded plan.name; otherwise, start empty.

onSubmit: Validate that a plan name is provided and optionally that constraints are met or user has confirmed saving despite unmet constraints; build an items payload from the working plan items (with item_type and the appropriate individualSessionId or groupWorkshopId) and call saveCustomPlan({ name, items }).

onApiSuccess(saveCustomPlan): Receive the saved plan object, update in-memory reference to match the returned plan, and update the URL with planId=<plan.id>; show a confirmation message and enable "Go to My Plans" navigation.
onApiError(saveCustomPlan): Present inline error feedback and keep the working plan state unchanged.
onClickGoToMyPlans: Navigate to dashboard.html?section=plans using URL-based navigation.

components.functionality: Lists all saved custom plans for the user using getCustomPlans, indicates the currently loaded plan, and allows switching between plans by updating planId in the URL.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Call getCustomPlans to retrieve all CustomPlan records.
onApiSuccess(getCustomPlans): Render the list of plans with their names and relevant metadata; highlight the entry whose id matches the current planId URL parameter.
onClickPlanItem(plan): Update the URL to custom_plan.html?planId=<plan.id> (preserving or adjusting other parameters as needed); this triggers page-mode-router and plan-detail-loader.
onClickStartNewPlan: Remove planId from the URL (navigate to custom_plan.html without planId) and cause the page to reset into new-plan builder mode.

components.functionality: Centralizes updates to and reading from the URL query string so that filters, constraints, and plan selection are consistently reflected in navigation state.
event_handlers: onInit: Parse the URL query string and expose structured state (planId, filter parameters, constraint parameters, view parameters) to other components.
onFilterChange: When filters forms request changes, update the URL parameters (add/replace/remove) without reloading the page.
onConstraintChange: When constraints form changes, update constraint-related URL parameters.
onPlanSelectionChange: When a new plan is selected or a new plan is created/saved, update the planId parameter in the URL accordingly.
onPopState: Listen to browser back/forward navigation and re-initialize components based on updated URL parameters.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width global header (logo left, nav mid-left, actions right, assumed but not defined as a component) sits at the very top. Directly beneath it, a hero-like builder header occupies the majority of the above-the-fold area in a two-column split. The left column (roughly 55–60% width) carries the Custom Plan Builder context: a small mode indicator (via page-mode-router), a compact 'category' treatment for the builder, a large page title (non-component), and the key control form (plan-constraints-form) with pricing-guidance-panel close by. The right column (40–45%) mirrors the hero image from the mockup with the plan-summary-panel acting as the focal 'card', containing the save-plan-form near its lower portion. Below this hero band, the main builder workspace continues the left-dominant layout: the left area stacks the individual-session-filters-form and individual-session-list followed by the workshop-filters-form and workshop-list. The right area continues downward with the saved-plans-sidebar under (or alongside) the sticky plan-summary-panel, forming a persistent right-hand rail.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow: a full-width global header (logo left, nav mid-left, actions right, assumed but not defined as a component) sits at the very top. Directly beneath it, a hero-like builder header occupies the majority of the above-the-fold area in a two-column split. The left column (roughly 55–60% width) carries the Custom Plan Builder context: a small mode indicator (via page-mode-router), a compact 'category' treatment for the builder, a large page title (non-component), and the key control form (plan-constraints-form) with pricing-guidance-panel close by. The right column (40–45%) mirrors the hero image from the mockup with the plan-summary-panel acting as the focal 'card', containing the save-plan-form near its lower portion. Below this hero band, the main builder workspace continues the left-dominant layout: the left area stacks the individual-session-filters-form and individual-session-list followed by the workshop-filters-form and workshop-list. The right area continues downward with the saved-plans-sidebar under (or alongside) the sticky plan-summary-panel, forming a persistent right-hand rail.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: A centered content container (max width ~1140–1200px) sits on a dark teal background with generous side margins. The global header consumes roughly 10–15% of viewport height. The hero/builder header below it fills most of the remaining above-the-fold space. Within the hero, the left column occupies about 7 of 12 grid columns (≈58%) and the right column about 5 of 12 (≈42%). The right column is visually lighter but solid, with the plan-summary-panel sized similarly to the image card in the mockup. Vertically, large blocks (hero band, filter sections, lists) are separated by 48–64px. Individual cards and forms use 24–32px internal padding. The right-hand column (summary + saved plans) remains narrower and more compact to keep focus on the builder controls and results on the left while still feeling substantial and anchored.
layout_strategies.content_density: The density matches the mockup’s calm SaaS/editorial feel: low content density, generous breathing room, and minimal visible chrome. Each major component sits within its own card-like area or clearly separated vertical segment, with 24–32px padding inside and 32–48px spacing between blocks. Typography is large and readable; forms are not cluttered—filters are grouped logically with clear separations and ample vertical spacing between fields. Only essential controls and data for constructing and evaluating the plan appear above the fold: mode selection, constraints, pricing guidance, plan summary, and entry points to sessions/workshops. Saved plans, detailed lists, and helper elements appear clearly but do not overload the initial view.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z pattern: after landing on the page, the eye moves from the top-left logo through the top navigation to any primary CTA on the top-right. From there it drops diagonally down to the hero’s left column, first encountering the page-mode-router (as a subtle entry control) and quickly anchoring on the large page heading and plan-constraints-form area. This is where the user understands and sets the planning frame (budget, frequency, timeline). The eye then moves horizontally to the right column to read the plan-summary-panel, checking totals and alignment. As the user scrolls, the flow loops vertically down the left column through filters and lists (individual sessions first, then workshops) while regularly glancing right to the sticky plan summary and the saved-plans-sidebar. This establishes a repeated left-to-right rhythm: adjust or select on the left, validate and save on the right.

layout.overall_description: The Custom Plan Builder page sits on a dark teal canvas with a centered, max-width content column that feels spacious and premium. A slim full-width header anchors the top with logo, navigation, and account actions. Just below, a hero-like builder header stretches across the screen: on the left, a subtle mode selector, a small pill labeling the area as the Custom Plan Builder, and a bold, multi-line page title lead into a concise constraints form that sets budget and planning rules, with a slim pricing guidance panel nested nearby. On the right, a tall, elegant plan summary card mirrors the placement of the hero image in the mockup, floating mid-way in the hero area and summarizing cost, number of sessions, and key metrics, with a save form integrated along its lower edge. Large amounts of teal background peek around both columns, maintaining the open, editorial feel. Scrolling down, the left side becomes a workbench: first a refined filter area for individual sessions and a list of matching sessions, then a similar pair for workshops, all left-aligned and full-width within the main column. On the right, the plan summary remains fixed near the top as the user scrolls, while a saved plans list fills the remaining vertical space in a narrow, well-structured sidebar. Invisible infrastructural components (routing, loaders, URL sync) orchestrate the experience without disrupting the clean aesthetic, resulting in a calm, focused builder interface where the user repeatedly looks left to configure and select, then right to review and save.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-mode-router is positioned at the top-left of the main builder content, immediately below the global header and aligned with the primary left margin of the centered content container. It appears as a horizontal pill-style or tab-style control, spanning roughly 30–40% of the content width (about 4–5 of 12 grid columns) but visually anchored to the left edge. There is approximately 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the global header and the top edge of the router, maintaining the airy feel. Below it, separated by about 16–24px, sits the page’s hero heading and the plan-constraints-form, so the router reads as a subtle mode selector above the primary context. On smaller viewports, it remains at the top of the content stack, stretching closer to full width while preserving margins on both sides.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero’s left column; it does not scroll independently but moves with the page. It controls which builder mode or state is visible but does not change its own position or size on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The builder-initial-loader manifests as a full-overlay layer that covers the entire builder area—from just below the global header down to the bottom of the visible viewport and spanning the full width of the centered content container. It slightly dims the dark teal background and any underlying components (router, constraints, summary, lists) with a translucent veil. At the center of this overlay, a minimal loading indicator and short message are vertically and horizontally centered, maintaining the clean aesthetic. Margins around the loader’s content follow the same generous padding as other components (~32px), and the overlay respects the outer left/right margins of the content container to keep it visually anchored within the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned as an absolute or fixed overlay relative to the main content container, blocking interaction with underlying components while visible. It scrolls with the page if the user scrolls, but the loader content remains centered within the current viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-detail-loader appears as a localized overlay that covers only the primary builder workspace: the hero left/right columns and the lists area below them, while leaving the global header visible. It spans the full width of the centered content container and extends from just below the header to just above the page footer. The overlay has a subtle darkening of the background and underlying cards, with a small loading indicator centered roughly in the middle of the hero region (aligned between the left constraints and right summary areas). This maintains the visual focus near the core of the builder without overwhelming the entire page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Absolute overlay anchored to the main builder container. When active, it prevents interaction with plan-related components but allows the page to remain scrollable if content extends beyond the viewport. It disappears once the selected plan details have loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing-guidance-panel is a compact, card-like helper panel nested within the hero’s left column, positioned directly beneath or to the immediate right of the plan-constraints-form, depending on available width. In a desktop layout, it typically sits below the constraints form, aligned to the same left edge, and spans about 60–70% of the left column’s width (roughly 4–5 of the 7 left grid columns). There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the constraints form and this panel, preserving visual separation. The panel’s content is concise, with 16–24px padding inside and a subtle border or elevated surface, so it reads as an advisory module tied to constraints without competing with the main heading or summary. On smaller screens it stacks directly under the constraints form and stretches closer to full width with consistent side margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column. It scrolls with the page and remains grouped with the plan-constraints-form as part of the hero band; no sticky or floating behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The individual-session-filters-form sits below the hero band in the main left column, aligned flush with the same left margin as the page title and constraints form. There is a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px between the bottom of the pricing-guidance-panel (or constraints form if guidance is hidden) and the top of this filter form to clearly signal a transition from framing to detailed configuration. The filter form occupies nearly the full width of the left column (about 7 of 12 grid columns), with 24–32px internal padding, and may be visually grouped in a light card or under a subtle section heading like “Individual Sessions.” Filter controls are arranged in one or two columns depending on viewport width but always with ample horizontal spacing. On smaller screens, this form becomes a full-width block below the hero, preserving vertical spacing to avoid crowding.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left content column, scrolling naturally with the lists and other sections below it. It does not stick or float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The workshop-filters-form is placed further down the same left column, directly below the individual-session-list and separated from it by 48–64px of vertical space. It mirrors the styling and width of the individual-session-filters-form, spanning nearly the full left column with 24–32px internal padding and a section heading such as “Workshops” to provide clear hierarchy. The filters within are arranged in a similarly spacious layout, with aligned labels and controls that maintain the same grid and spacing rhythm as the individual session filters. This form signals a shift from configuring 1:1 sessions to configuring group workshops while feeling like part of the same workflow lane.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing down the left column as the user scrolls. It does not overlay or dock; it remains contextually tied to the workshop-list below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The individual-session-list appears immediately below the individual-session-filters-form within the left column, separated by a modest 24–32px vertical gap to indicate dependency yet maintain clarity. It fills the full available width of the left column and extends downward as far as needed to show individual session cards or rows, with each item following the same 8–16px internal spacing rules. The list is scrollable with the page (not inside an inner scroll area) to preserve the clean, editorial feel. As the user scrolls, the left column feels like a continuous feed of sessions, while the right column’s plan summary remains visible as a reference. On smaller screens, this list fills the available width and stacks below the hero and filters.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; the page scroll controls its visibility. It does not use its own scroll container on desktop, relying instead on the browser’s main scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The workshop-list is positioned directly below the workshop-filters-form in the left column, with about 24–32px of vertical spacing between the form and the first workshop item. It spans the full width of the left column, echoing the layout and visual treatment of the individual-session-list, but clearly separated with a section heading and spacing to reduce cognitive load. As users scroll further down the page past the individual sessions, the workshop-list continues the same rhythm of cards or rows, maintaining consistent margins and gutters. The list can grow vertically and remains visually balanced by the persistent right-hand plan summary and saved plans rail.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; its visibility is controlled by the main page scroll. It does not introduce nested scrolling to keep the overall layout simple and predictable.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-constraints-form is the core interactive element in the hero’s left column, positioned directly below the primary page heading and any small category pill or label. It begins roughly 24–32px below the heading, aligned precisely to the left edge of the content container’s grid, and spans about 70–80% of the left column’s width (approximately 5–6 of the 7 left grid columns), leaving some negative space to the right to maintain the airy feel. Inside, key fields like budget, duration, and frequency are arranged in a single row for larger screens and collapse into a vertical stack on smaller ones, using 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. This form occupies the upper-middle zone of the hero area, aligning vertically with the mid/upper part of the plan-summary-panel on the right, echoing the alignment of the heading and hero image in the original mockup. It sets the planning frame and visually anchors the user’s first interaction with the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero section; it scrolls out of view as the user moves down to the lists, but its influence is always reflected in the sticky plan-summary-panel on the right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-summary-panel occupies the hero’s right column and visually replaces the hero image from the mockup. It is positioned so that its top edge aligns roughly with the upper third of the constraints form on the left, creating a balanced horizontal relationship across the hero. The panel spans about 4–5 of the 12 grid columns (≈35–40% of the content width), with 24–32px padding inside and rounded corners to present a polished, card-like silhouette against the dark teal background. Its vertical size is enough to comfortably display total cost, time commitment, and key metrics, plus the embedded save-plan-form near its base, leaving sufficient teal space above and below within the hero band to preserve the spacious feel. As the user scrolls down through filters and lists on the left, the plan summary remains in the right-hand rail, providing a constant visual reference akin to a persistent hero image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the right-hand column: on desktop, it remains fixed to the top of the viewport (with a small offset, ~80–96px below the global header) once the user scrolls past its original position, so it stays visible while the left column content scrolls. On smaller screens, it becomes a full-width block stacked below the hero content and scrolls normally to avoid overwhelming the limited width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The save-plan-form is embedded inside the plan-summary-panel, occupying the lower portion of the card. It appears as a dense but compact set of fields and actions—such as plan name, optional notes, and a primary Save button—aligned left within the summary panel’s internal grid. There is about 16–24px of space between the summary metrics and the top of the save form, and an additional 16–24px padding below the Save button to match the card’s internal spacing. The save form stretches nearly the full internal width of the summary panel, but with consistent side padding, so it feels integrated into the panel rather than a separate element. When the summary panel is sticky, the save form moves with it, ensuring saving is always one glance and one tap away.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within and spatially bound to the plan-summary-panel. It inherits the panel’s sticky behavior on desktop and scrolls in tandem on smaller viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The saved-plans-sidebar forms the lower portion of the right-hand rail, directly beneath the plan-summary-panel. In the initial above-the-fold view, its header (e.g., 'Saved Plans') peeks just under the summary panel, with about 24px of vertical separation. It spans the same width as the summary panel, aligning perfectly in the right column. The sidebar’s list area extends downwards and can occupy the remaining vertical space of the right column, often scrolling independently within its own panel when the list grows long. Items inside are individually spaced with 8–16px gaps, but the overall sidebar retains 16–24px padding and a compact typographic hierarchy to avoid competing with the summary. This sidebar allows users to quickly load or compare plans without leaving the current builder view, while maintaining the clean, right-aligned visual anchor.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On desktop, it sits within the same sticky right-hand rail as the plan-summary-panel. The right column itself is effectively sticky (fixed relative to viewport with an offset from the global header), and the saved-plans list may have its own internal scroll if content exceeds available height. On smaller screens, this sidebar stacks below the summary panel and above the lists, scrolling naturally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is not visually represented in the UI. Conceptually, it sits at the root of the builder page’s main content container, tracking changes to filters, constraints, selected items, and loaded plan IDs. Its presence ensures that the visible layout—hero configuration on the left, summary and saved plans on the right, and selected sessions/workshops—can be reconstructed from the URL, but it introduces no visible elements, spacing, or chrome into the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-spatial. It operates in the background, synchronizing state with the URL without affecting the physical arrangement or scrolling behavior of any visible components.

name: About & Contact
title: About & Contact
description: Page to display structured information about the coaching practice, its mission and background, provide a general contact form for inquiries, and present combined privacy and terms information. Sections are addressable via the 'section' URL parameter.

core_features: Load and display structured About content (title, body, highlights) using getAboutPageContent, including mission, values, background, and a policies highlight if provided.
Provide section-based navigation (About, Contact, Policies) that reacts to the 'section' URL parameter and updates the visible section and scroll/focus state accordingly.
Expose a general contact form with fields for name, email, message, and topic, and submit the data via sendGeneralContactMessage.
Display inline feedback (success or error) based on the response from sendGeneralContactMessage without leaving the page.
Present a combined Privacy & Terms section using content derived from getAboutPageContent, and ensure the page scrolls/focuses to this section when accessed with section=policies.
user_workflows:
View About information:
1. User opens about_contact.html directly or via navigation without a section parameter.
2. page-controller parses the URL; if no 'section' is present, it defaults to 'about'.
3. page-controller invokes getAboutPageContent.
4. Once the content is returned, about-content-section renders the title, body, and relevant highlights.
5. page-controller scrolls and focuses the About section based on the current 'section' state.

Open page focused on Contact section:
1. User clicks an internal or external link to about_contact.html?section=contact.
2. page-controller reads section=contact from the URL.
3. page-controller invokes getAboutPageContent to ensure all sections can render.
4. contact-form-section is rendered and scrolled into view, and it receives focus (e.g., its heading or the name input).
5. section-navigation updates its active state to highlight the Contact section.

Open page focused on Privacy & Terms:
1. User clicks a footer link (e.g., 'Privacy & Terms') which navigates to about_contact.html?section=policies.
2. page-controller reads section=policies from the URL.
3. page-controller invokes getAboutPageContent.
4. policies-section renders policy-related content derived from getAboutPageContent (for example, from a specific highlight or segment of the body).
5. The page scrolls to and focuses the policies-section; section-navigation updates its active item to Policies.

Submit a general contact inquiry:
1. User navigates to the Contact section (via URL parameter, internal navigation, or scrolling).
2. In contact-form-section, user fills in Name, Email, Message, and optionally Topic.
3. User clicks the form submit button.
4. contact-form-section validates required fields on the client (e.g., Name, Email, Message not empty; email format basic check).
5. If validation fails, inline validation messages are shown and submission is blocked.
6. If validation passes, contact-form-section calls sendGeneralContactMessage with the entered name, email, message, and topic.
7. While awaiting the response, the submit button is disabled and a pending state is shown in contact-status-banner.
8. On success (response.success = true), contact-status-banner shows a success message based on response.message, and the form may optionally reset its fields.
9. On failure (response.success = false), contact-status-banner displays an error state using response.message; the form remains filled so the user can adjust and resubmit.

Navigate back to Home:
1. User clicks the logo or a 'Back to Home' link on the About & Contact page.
2. header-navigation intercepts the click event and navigates to index.html without additional parameters.
3. No state from About & Contact is persisted, as URL parameters are not used across pages in this flow.
interactions: Initial load: On DOM ready, page-controller reads the 'section' URL parameter and sets an internal activeSection state (allowed values, e.g., 'about', 'contact', 'policies'). It then calls getAboutPageContent and, after data is loaded, scrolls to the chosen section.

Section navigation click: When a user clicks a section-navigation item (About, Contact, Policies), section-navigation updates the activeSection state in page-controller and triggers a URL update (e.g., about_contact.html?section=contact) via history API without reloading the page, then scrolls/focuses to the associated section.
Logo / Back to Home click: In header-navigation, clicking the logo or 'Back to Home' triggers navigation to index.html.

Contact form input: As the user types into the Name, Email, Message, and Topic fields, contact-form-section updates its internal formState; any validation errors are updated in real time (e.g., clearing an error once the input becomes valid).

Contact form submit: On form submission, contact-form-section prevents default browser submission, runs client-side validation, and on success invokes sendGeneralContactMessage with the current formState. It then interprets the returned object (success, message) and passes this to contact-status-banner.

URL parameter change (within the page): When a section-navigation item is clicked, page-controller updates the URL's ?section parameter (via pushState/replaceState) to reflect the new active section. If the user uses the browser Back/Forward buttons, page-controller listens to popstate events to read the updated 'section' value and adjust the visible section and scroll position accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the 'section' URL parameter. On page load, page-controller parses window.location.search to obtain section. If section is missing or does not match a recognized value, it defaults to 'about'. Recognized values include 'about', 'contact', and 'policies'. This activeSection state controls: (1) which section is scrolled into view and receives focus, (2) which navigation item in section-navigation appears active, and (3) which ARIA attributes (e.g., aria-current) are applied. When users click internal navigation items, page-controller updates activeSection and uses the History API to modify the 'section' parameter without reloading the page. When the browser Back/Forward buttons are used, a popstate listener re-evaluates the current 'section' parameter and syncs the UI. All content for About and Policies is loaded via a single call to getAboutPageContent on each page load; no additional data fetches are triggered by section changes to avoid unnecessary calls. The contact form state (field values, validation errors, submission status, and last API response) is held in memory in contact-form-section and contact-status-banner only; it is not stored in URL parameters or any persistent storage. The sendGeneralContactMessage response determines transient UI states (success or error) but does not affect URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading, URL parameter parsing, active section state, and scroll/focus behavior for the About, Contact, and Policies sections.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse URL 'section' parameter, set default activeSection, call getAboutPageContent, and dispatch content to child components.
onContentLoaded: After getAboutPageContent resolves, notify about-content-section and policies-section with the content payload, then scroll and focus to the section corresponding to activeSection.

onSectionChange: When notified (e.g., from section-navigation) that the user selected a different section, update activeSection, update the 'section' URL parameter via History API, and scroll/focus to the corresponding section element.
onPopState: Listen for browser history changes, re-parse the 'section' parameter from the URL, update activeSection, and adjust visible section and scroll/focus.

components.functionality: Provides top-level navigation controls relevant to this page, including a clickable logo and a 'Back to Home' link.
event_handlers: onLogoClick: Navigate to 'index.html' without additional parameters.
onBackToHomeClick: Navigate to 'index.html' without additional parameters.

components.functionality: Allows users to switch between the About, Contact, and Policies sections and keeps the UI in sync with the activeSection state and URL parameter.
data_binding: URL parameter: section (read only for initial setup)
event_handlers: onAboutClick: Notify page-controller to set activeSection to 'about' and update the URL parameter accordingly.
onContactClick: Notify page-controller to set activeSection to 'contact' and update the URL parameter accordingly.
onPoliciesClick: Notify page-controller to set activeSection to 'policies' and update the URL parameter accordingly.

components.functionality: Renders the mission, values, and background information for the coaching practice using data from getAboutPageContent. This section is the default focus when no 'section' parameter is provided.
event_handlers: onDataReceive: When page-controller passes in the getAboutPageContent result, render the main About content, mapping title/body to the section heading and text, and mapping non-policy highlights to structured sub-sections.
onSectionActivated: When page-controller sets activeSection to 'about', ensure this section is visible, scrolled into view, and an appropriate element receives focus (e.g., the heading).

components.functionality: Presents the general contact form (Name, Email, Message, Topic) and submits inquiries using sendGeneralContactMessage. Manages local form state and client-side validation.
data_binding: Form field state: name, email, message, topic
Result of sendGeneralContactMessage (success, message) as passed through to contact-status-banner
event_handlers: onSectionActivated: When page-controller sets activeSection to 'contact', ensure the section is visible, scrolled into view, and a suitable focus target (e.g., the Name field) is focused.
onFieldChange: Update internal formState when the user types or changes any field (name, email, message, topic).

onFormSubmit: Prevent default form submission, run client-side validation for required fields and basic email format, and if valid set isSubmitting to true and call sendGeneralContactMessage with current formState.
onSubmitSuccess: When sendGeneralContactMessage returns with success=true, set isSubmitting to false, pass the response to contact-status-banner, and optionally clear form fields.

onSubmitError: When sendGeneralContactMessage returns with success=false or if the request fails, set isSubmitting to false and pass the error information to contact-status-banner while leaving form values intact.

components.functionality: Displays feedback about the most recent contact form submission, including success and error states, using the response from sendGeneralContactMessage.
event_handlers: onStatusUpdate: When contact-form-section provides the latest API response, render a success or error message based on response.success and response.message.
onSubmittingChange: When isSubmitting is true, optionally show a pending indicator and hide previous success/error messages until the result is known.
onDismissClick: If the user dismisses the banner, clear the visible status without altering the underlying submission history.

components.functionality: Displays combined Privacy & Terms information. The content is derived from the structured data returned by getAboutPageContent, for example by using a specific highlight or portion of the body designated for policy content.
data_binding: Result of getAboutPageContent (title, body, highlights, with policy-related entries selected by front-end logic)
event_handlers: onDataReceive: When page-controller passes in the getAboutPageContent result, extract and render policy-related content (e.g., highlights whose heading or metadata indicates they are Privacy & Terms) into this section.
onSectionActivated: When page-controller sets activeSection to 'policies', ensure this section is visible, scrolled into view, and an appropriate element (e.g., the section heading) receives focus.

layout.grid_system: Implied 12-column responsive grid with a centered maximum content width (likely 1140–1280px). Hero content appears to span about 6–7 columns for text and 5–6 columns for the image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow with a fixed full-width header at the top, a section-level navigation bar directly beneath it, and then a hero-like About section as the first main content. Below the hero, the Contact form section and then the Policies section are stacked vertically. Within sections, the layout favors two-column splits where appropriate: text/content anchored on the left, imagery or open space on the right, maintaining the mockup’s left-weighted emphasis on primary text blocks.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom flow with a fixed full-width header at the top, a section-level navigation bar directly beneath it, and then a hero-like About section as the first main content. Below the hero, the Contact form section and then the Policies section are stacked vertically. Within sections, the layout favors two-column splits where appropriate: text/content anchored on the left, imagery or open space on the right, maintaining the mockup’s left-weighted emphasis on primary text blocks.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header occupies a slim horizontal band at the top (about 10–15% of viewport height). The first main section (About) behaves like a hero, visually taking up the majority of the initial viewport height (~65–80%), using roughly 55–60% width for the text column and 40–45% for the image column. The Contact form section below uses a similar horizontal allocation (a left column for the form card spanning about 55–60% of the content width, with the remaining width as breathing room). The Policies section uses a single, narrower text column (about 60–70% of the content width) centered or left-aligned within the main content container. Major sections are separated by 64–96px vertical gaps to preserve the open, editorial feeling.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low across the page: large headings, generous line height, and wide margins around all blocks. The About hero section shows only essential elements above the fold: small section/category label, main heading, short supporting copy, and the hero image. The Contact section presents a single, focused form card with a status banner when needed and avoids additional clutter; any extra details are kept minimal and aligned with the form. The Policies section uses long-form text but constrained to a narrower column with ample spacing between subsections and paragraphs to maintain readability and calmness.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup’s F/Z-pattern: the eye starts at the logo on the top-left, moves horizontally along the header navigation to the right-side CTA, then drops diagonally to the section navigation and the About label + large heading on the left. From there, the gaze moves across to the hero image on the right. As users scroll, the eye follows a consistent left-aligned spine of key content: About text, then the Contact form card, then the Policies copy. Optional sticky behavior for the section navigation keeps a secondary horizontal scanning line just below the header, making it easy to jump between About, Contact, and Policies without disrupting the overall left-weighted visual hierarchy.

layout.overall_description: The page presents as a modern, polished SaaS-style single column of content framed by a full-width dark teal background. A fixed, slim header bar sits at the very top with the logo on the left, navigation roughly centered, and a clear call-to-action on the right. Directly beneath, a horizontal section navigation row aligns with the main content width, offering pill-like tabs for About, Contact, and Policies. Below this, the screen opens into a spacious hero-like About section where a small ‘About’ pill and a bold, multi-line heading sit in a wide left column, while a large portrait-style photo fills a slightly narrower right column; both are surrounded by generous negative space in the dark teal canvas. Scrolling down, the Contact area appears as a clean, card-like form anchored to the left side of the content container, with ample empty space to its right preserving the light, editorial feel. A slim status banner hugs the top of the form card when needed. Further down, the layout transitions into the Policies section, where long-form legal and privacy text is presented in a narrower text column aligned to the left within the same central grid, with comfortable vertical spacing between headings and paragraphs. From top to bottom, consistent margins, a restrained color palette, and a repeated left-aligned content spine create a calm, confident experience that clearly separates sections yet feels like a cohesive, premium single page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-controller acts as the structural wrapper for all content below the fixed header. It occupies the full browser width and extends from just beneath the header/navigation bar down to the bottom of the page. Inside it, all major sections (section-navigation, About, Contact, Policies) are aligned to a central content container that is horizontally centered and constrained to roughly 1140–1280px maximum width, matching the mockup’s grid. Vertically, it stacks the About hero section first, followed by the Contact form section and then the Policies section, with 64–96px of vertical spacing between each block. The page-controller applies a top padding equal to the header height plus a small offset (e.g., header height + 16–24px) so that content never sits beneath the fixed header and the section-navigation row positions directly under it. It coordinates scrolling behavior so that, when a section is targeted (via the URL parameter or section-navigation interaction), the corresponding section is scrolled into view such that its top edge sits just below the header and section-navigation, preserving the clean visual alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-page wrapper; manages scroll offsets so that section anchors land immediately under the fixed header and any sticky section-navigation. It does not render visible chrome itself but ensures all child sections align to the same centered grid and maintain consistent vertical gaps.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The header-navigation forms a full-width horizontal bar anchored to the very top of the viewport. It is approximately 72–80px tall, consuming roughly 10–15% of the viewport height on typical laptop screens. The background is a solid dark teal that matches the site’s brand foundation. Within this bar, content is constrained to the central max-width container (around 1140–1280px) and horizontally centered on the page. Inside this container, the logo area occupies the leftmost 20–25% of the width, a row of navigation links sits roughly centered-left taking about 40–50% of the width, and a primary action (e.g., a ‘Book Session’ button) is right-aligned in the remaining 25–30%. Elements are vertically centered within the bar, with 16–24px horizontal padding between items, and 24px of padding on the left and right edges of the container. The bottom edge of the header may have a subtle 1px divider line or shadow to visually separate it from the content below without adding clutter.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed at the top of the viewport, always visible as the user scrolls. It spans the full browser width and does not scroll with the content. The rest of the layout accounts for its height by adding corresponding top padding/margins in the page-controller and section-navigation components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section-navigation is positioned immediately below the fixed header, aligned with the same central content container. Horizontally, it spans roughly 60–70% of the container width, starting from the left margin so that it visually aligns with the left edge of the hero text and subsequent content blocks. It appears as a row of pill-like tabs (e.g., ‘About’, ‘Contact’, ‘Policies’) placed in a single horizontal line, each pill about 104–144px wide with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding inside, separated by 8–16px gaps. There is 24–32px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the header bar and the top of the section-navigation row, and another 32–40px of spacing between the section-navigation and the About hero section below. The dark teal background from the page continues behind the section-navigation, while the pills themselves use a lighter or contrasting fill to stand out without overwhelming the hero below. The currently active section is highlighted through stronger contrast (e.g., solid pill) and possibly a subtle underline aligned with the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky positioning just beneath the fixed header: as the user scrolls down through the About, Contact, and Policies sections, the section-navigation remains pinned at the top of the content area (directly under the header) until the user reaches the end of these sections. This provides a stable horizontal control to jump between sections while preserving the mockup’s clean, top-aligned navigation structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-section acts as the hero of this page and occupies the topmost main content area beneath the section-navigation. It spans the full browser width but constrains its inner content to the central max-width container. Vertically, it is tall enough to dominate the initial viewport—typically 65–80% of the viewport height—so that the category pill, large heading, and hero image are all visible without scrolling on common desktop resolutions. Inside, a two-column layout is used: the left column takes approximately 55–60% of the container width, and the right column takes 40–45%. The left column starts near the top of the section with a small, rounded ‘About’ pill at the top-left, followed by 16–24px of vertical space, then a large multi-line heading that spans most of the text column’s width, and then a short paragraph or two of supporting copy below with 16–24px spacing. The right column contains a single, high-quality rectangular photo (e.g., the coach’s portrait or an office scene) that fills most of the column width and has a fixed aspect ratio (e.g., 4:5 or 3:4). The top of the image is vertically aligned around the mid to upper area of the heading block so that the heading text visually leads and the image complements it. Both columns are surrounded by generous padding: 48–64px top and bottom within the section, and 24–32px between the columns. The dark teal background extends behind everything, with large areas of visible negative space above, below, and to the right of the image, reinforcing the calm, premium aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that anchors the top of the content flow. On smaller screens, it stacks vertically with the text appearing first and the image below, maintaining generous vertical spacing while the section height naturally grows to accommodate the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-form-section sits directly below the About hero, separated by a generous 64–80px vertical gap to clearly signal a new functional area. It also spans the full width of the browser, with content constrained inside the central max-width container. Within this container, the Contact form is presented as a card-like block aligned to the same left edge as the About text column. The form card itself uses approximately 55–60% of the container width on larger screens, leaving the remaining 40–45% as open negative space on the right, echoing the hero’s text-heavy-left / lighter-right composition. The card has a slightly lighter surface than the dark teal background (or a subtle border) and features 32–40px of internal padding on all sides. A section label or heading (e.g., ‘Contact’ or ‘Get in touch’) appears at the top-left of the card, with 16–24px of space before the first form fields. The fields are stacked vertically with 16–24px vertical spacing and extend nearly the full width of the card, maintaining a clean, linear flow. The primary submit button sits left-aligned at the bottom of the card with 24–32px of spacing above it. The vertical padding of the section ensures that the form feels comfortably spaced from both the About hero above and the Policies section below, typically 64–80px from each.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section that scrolls with the page. On smaller screens, the form card expands to occupy close to 100% of the content width while preserving internal padding and vertical spacing, maintaining the low-density, calm aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-status-banner is a slim horizontal strip associated directly with the contact-form-section. It appears only when there is a status to communicate (e.g., success, error, or pending) and is positioned immediately above the contact form card, sharing the same left alignment and width as the card. If the form card occupies about 55–60% of the container width, the banner matches that width exactly, creating a tight, vertically stacked unit. The banner has a height of roughly 40–56px, with 12–16px of vertical padding inside, and contains concise text left-aligned within it. Its background color contrasts clearly against both the page background and the form card (e.g., a soft green for success, amber for warning, or red for error) while preserving the overall muted, premium palette. There is a small 8–16px gap between the bottom of the banner and the top edge of the form card so the two elements feel visually linked but not merged. The surrounding section spacing remains unchanged whether the banner is visible or hidden, avoiding jarring jumps in layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, non-sticky element that appears and disappears within the normal document flow inside the contact-form-section. It does not overlay content; instead, it pushes the form card down slightly when present, maintaining the established vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-section forms the final major content block on the page and is positioned beneath the contact-form-section, separated by approximately 80–96px of vertical space to signal a shift to more formal, informational content. Like the other sections, it spans the full width of the browser with text constrained to the central max-width container. Within this container, the policies content (combined privacy and terms) is placed in a single, narrower text column occupying about 60–70% of the container width, aligned to the left so it forms a continuous visual spine with the About text and Contact form card above. A small label or heading (e.g., ‘Policies’ or ‘Privacy & Terms’) sits at the top-left of this column, potentially using the same pill style as the About label, followed by 24–32px of space and then the body text. The body text is broken into clearly labeled subsections with subheadings, each separated by 24–32px of vertical spacing and comfortable line height for long-form reading. The background remains the same dark teal, while the text is in high-contrast white or near-white. The section includes generous bottom padding—at least 64–80px below the final paragraph—so the page ends with breathing room rather than an abrupt cutoff.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static terminal section of the page. It scrolls into view after the Contact section and extends as long as needed to accommodate policy content, without special sticky or fixed behavior.
